incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Primary Containment
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Crane Lifting Incident
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid
Summary: On May 2, 2024, during 5-year recertification load testing of the Forward Starboard knuckle boom crane (KBC #1) on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse, a winch brake assembly failure occurred at approximately 70 metric tons while attempting to reach the full load test of 93.5 MT. The failure resulted in uncontrolled descent of a water bag test load, causing a Loss of Primary Containment (LOPC) due to hydraulic test port failure. The water bag contacted the take-on station corner, ripped open, and descended into the Gulf of Mexico. Property damage totaled $168,150. Root cause was determined to be crane overload due to improper sea lift area designation combined with lack of personnel training on Automatic Overload Protection System (AOPS) functionality and inadequate procedures.
summary: On May 2, 2024, during 5-year recertification load testing of the Forward Starboard knuckle boom crane (KBC #1) on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse, a winch brake assembly failure occurred at approximately 70 metric tons while attempting to reach the full load test of 93.5 MT. The failure resulted in uncontrolled descent of a water bag test load, causing a Loss of Primary Containment (LOPC) due to hydraulic test port failure. The water bag contacted the take-on station corner, ripped open, and descended into the Gulf of Mexico. Property damage totaled $168,150. Root cause was determined to be crane overload due to improper sea lift area designation combined with lack of personnel training on Automatic Overload Protection System (AOPS) functionality and inadequate procedures.
recommendations: ["Reset sea lift area when performing 5-year load test over the side of the vessel", "Provide proper training on AOPS functionality to personnel performing load tests", "Revise job instructions to include safety system checks before load test, not after", "Improve maintenance procedures for brake overhaul and installation to ensure Quality Control checks", "Update work task references and load test procedures to specify sea lift area reset requirements"]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped load striking personnel
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall with struck-by injury
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: drill line (2-inch diameter steel), nylon web sling
Summary: On 7 March 2024 at 1500 hours, during slip and cut drill line operations on the Seadrill West Neptune drillship, a 2-inch diameter drill line (approximately 19 feet long, 148 pounds) slipped from a nylon web sling being used with an air tugger and fell freely to the rig floor. A Seadrill employee attempting to exit the area tripped over coiled drill line and fell, and was subsequently struck by the falling drill line on his hardhat, middle back, and left shoulder. The employee sustained a torn labrum and shoulder strain requiring 10-12 weeks of physical therapy.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Traveling block struck crown saver assembly
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Material Handling
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drill pipe, drilling fluid
Summary: On July 8, 2024, at 1908 hours, while pulling out of the hole with drill pipe on the jack-up Enterprise 205 at South Timbalier Block 23, the traveling block struck the crown saver assembly with approximately 117,000 lbs overpull, resulting in $11,210 in damages to the crown saver and crown-o-matic. The driller lost situational awareness by focusing on pipe tally while pulling, and the crown-o-matic failed to trip when contacted by the drill line because the barrel was not set properly. The incident was not immediately reported to BSEE.

recommendations: ["Develop rig-specific procedure for checking the crown-o-matic with step-by-step instructions", "Ensure Job Safety Analysis identifies the hazard of traveling block striking crown block assembly", "Implement requirement for crew member to monitor wire rope and count wraps during crown-o-matic check", "Ensure proper setup and maintenance of crown-o-matic barrel", "Improve communication of job instructions for pulling out of hole operations", "Establish fleetwide procedure for checking crown-o-matic"]

Top Event: Slip/Trip/Fall with Wave Strike
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: workover
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 10-NOV-2024 at 0145 hours, an injured person (IP) on the Transocean Deepwater Invictus drillship was struck by a 15-foot wave while standing at the aft handrail despite being informed the stern was secured due to deteriorating weather and building seas. The IP was knocked approximately 20 feet across the rig, possibly striking a bollard, and sustained a fractured right femur. The IP had less than one year offshore experience and was not assigned duties in the secured area but chose to enter it to observe the water.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Head injury from winch assist handle strike
Incident Type: Other Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: lifeboat capsule
Summary: On July 11, 2024, at 0730 hours, a contract pipeline operator from Quality Process Services suffered a serious head injury when struck by a winch assist handle during a routine lifeboat capsule inspection at the High Island A-5 Manta Ray Gathering Facility. The handle remained in the socket when the powered winch was engaged, causing it to rotate and strike the operator in the head. The operator sustained a fractured skull requiring surgery and was transported via USCG to UTMB in Galveston. The incident was caused by failure to remove the winch assist handle before engaging the powered winch, compounded by a bypassed safety device and lack of a Job Safety Analysis.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped load from crane
Incident Type: structural damage
Operating Phase: plug and abandon
Materials: tools, lifting equipment
Summary: On March 13, 2024, at approximately 20:10 hours, a 4x40' basket of plug and abandon tools weighing 13,000 pounds was being offloaded from the M/V GOL Cowboy to the upper cantilever of the jack-up Enterprise 264 using the port side crane. The stinger, rated for 9.80 tons in vertical pull, failed due to internal corrosion buildup from moisture in the socket. The basket fell and landed on the control room, P-tanks, and platform on top of the living quarters, causing structural damage estimated at $7,500. No injuries occurred. The failure to inspect the stinger prior to use was identified as a significant contributing factor.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Caught Between Incident
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane Other Lifting
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: 16 inch casing
Summary: On February 16, 2024, at 13:29 hours, a deck foreman (IP) was struck by a suspended bundle of 16-inch casing weighing approximately 11,500 lbs during offloading operations on the Nabors MODS 400 rig. The IP was supervising the operation and verifying casing tally with his back turned to the load. The casing bundle swung in his direction and struck him on the right hip, forcing his left hip into a sub-rack basket, resulting in three pelvic fractures. The IP was medivaced to a hospital and discharged on February 20, 2024.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped Drill Pipe
Incident Type: Structural Damage / Dropped Load
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: drill pipe, drilling fluid
Summary: On February 18, 2023, at 21:27 hours, while performing pulling out of hole (POOH) operations on the TA004 well at MC 778, the Driller inadvertently opened the pipe elevators before the Pipe Racking System (PRS) could secure the 13th stand of 4-1/2 inch drill pipe. The stand fell across the rig floor, striking the auxiliary PRS hoist and falling within close proximity of three Floor Hands. One Floor Hand slipped on the deck while evacuating and sustained injuries to his knee and hip, requiring medical evacuation.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Amputation injury during conductor removal operations
Incident Type: Amputation - Lost Time Accident (>3 days)
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: marine growth, steel conductors
Summary: On June 18, 2023 at 18:35 hours, an injured person (IP) suffered amputation of the middle and ring finger of his left hand while adjusting chains on a marine growth removal system (MGRS) during conductor removal operations at Platform Gail. The IP placed his left hand fingers under the chain during an assumed pause in conductor lifting. The conductor resumed rising, tightening the chain and trapping the IP's hand, resulting in amputation of finger tips between the fingernail and first knuckle. The incident occurred due to inadequate verbal communication regarding the 'all stop' command, lack of risk assessment for the MGRS methods employed, and deviation from the approved Application for Permit to Modify (APM) which specified use of diver methods with water jets.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Casing String
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: 22" casing, drilling fluid
Summary: On January 18, 2024, during drilling operations at GC 478, OCS-G35662, Well 004 on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse, a 22" casing connection failed at the rig floor. The S-90/MT pin connection of joint 16 was cross-threaded and made up only ¼ - 3/8 turn instead of the required ½ - ¾ turn. When the flush-mounted slips holding joint 17 were opened to install anti-rotation keys, the axial load transferred to the improperly made-up connection, causing the pin to separate from the box. Seventy-two joints of casing fell partially into the hole and onto the seafloor, breaking apart and impacting critical subsea infrastructure. No injuries or environmental impacts occurred, but damages exceeded $15 million.

When the flush-mounted slips holding joint 17 were opened to install anti-rotation keys, the axial load transferred to the improperly made-up connection, causing the pin to separate from the box. Seventy-two joints of casing fell partially into the hole and onto the seafloor, breaking apart and impacting critical subsea infrastructure. No injuries or environmental impacts occurred, but damages exceeded $15 million.
recommendations: ["Ensure visual inspection of casing connections is completed as per approved running procedures to detect cross-threading and makeup discrepancies", "Clarify in JSA and running procedures when anti-rotation keys should be installed relative to slip release", "Implement supervisor verification of connection makeup before slips are released", "Establish clear communication protocols between crew members regarding casing positioning and connection verification"]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Hand caught between crane cable and sheave resulting in finger lacerations and partial amputation
Incident Type: Injury requiring evacuation
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: N/A
Summary: On July 20, 2024, at 2200 hours, a rigger working on abandonment operations at Ship Shoal 224 D platform suffered lacerations to his ring finger and partial amputation of his pinky finger when his right hand was caught between the crane cable and crane block sheave while guiding a casing cutter tool through the deck. The incident occurred during a blind lift operation where the injured person used his hands instead of push poles to guide the suspended load, contrary to the operator's Material Handling policy. The JSAs performed that night did not identify the hazard of being caught between the crane cable and sheave, and Stop Work Authority was not invoked when the unsafe hand placement began.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped Objects - Traveling Block and Drill Line
Incident Type: Dropped Objects
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drill line, drilling fluid
Summary: On 20 July 2017 at 16:45 hours, Arena Offshore, LP experienced a dropped objects incident during drilling operations at Eugene Island Block 251C. While conducting non-routine top-drive removal and installation, the traveling block descended approximately 10-20 feet and drill line unspooled from the drawworks drum, causing the traveling block to land on the rotary table and approximately 123 feet of drill line to fall to the rig floor. The incident was caused by improper drill line tension and inadequate clamp design, resulting in damage to the drawworks drum drill line clamp and drill line. No injuries or structural damage occurred.

recommendations: ["Prior to top-drive removal, slip additional wraps of drill line onto drawworks drum to prevent slack and unseating from drum grooves", "Verify properly sized drawworks drill line clamp is installed with certified bolts", "Verify recertification of drawworks drum shafts includes inspection of drum drill line clamp", "Incorporate inspection of drum drill line clamp into rig's drawworks preventative maintenance program and annual lifting gear inspection"]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in Glycol Reboiler Dehydration System
Incident Type: fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: glycol, natural gas
Summary: On January 5, 2023, at 1730 hours, a fire occurred in the Glycol Reboiler Dehydration system at High Island 547 B platform. The still column vent pipe became plugged, causing pressure buildup until rupture. Glycol spray contacted the reboiler and exhaust stack (operating at 750-800°F), igniting the glycol mixture. The fire was extinguished in ten minutes with no injuries or pollution. Damage exceeded $100,000.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Power
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: electrical equipment, power systems
Summary: On January 3, 2023, at 2210 hours, the Universal Power Supply (UPS-7560) at Perdido Spar failed, releasing smoke and activating smoke detectors in the UPS room. The DC capacitor burst upon failure, causing arcing/sparking and triggering an automated facility shut-in and full power loss. The automated safety system functioned as designed. No injuries or pollution were reported. Estimated repair cost was $56,830.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped Object
Incident Type: Structural Damage
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: marine riser, riser bolt insert, drilling fluid
Summary: On March 21, 2023 at 02:30 hours, a 25-pound riser bolt insert dislodged from a riser flange and fell approximately 75 feet to the rig floor on the drillship Valaris DS-18. The object passed between two Floorhands and struck an aluminum stairway, damaging two steps. The riser retainer pin was either never installed or fell out during transport. The retention cable (sling) required by WIMS Step 6, Item 'd' was not installed. Personnel failed to follow proper procedures and did not stop the job when the incident occurred.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Hand injury from caught-between hazard
Incident Type: Caught between C-plates during wireline tool disconnection
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: N/A
Summary: On April 22, 2024, at 0815 hours, an injured person (IP) assisting a wireline helper to disconnect wireline tools on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse had his right hand caught between upper and lower C-plates weighing 175 lbs. The IP placed his hand under the upper C-plate to stabilize the tool string while the helper released the tools, causing them to fall 4 inches and crush the IP's hand. The IP sustained lacerations to the right hand ring and middle fingertips, a broken ring finger tip, and required 14 stitches. The IP was evacuated and released to light duty on 4/22/24 and full duty on 5/22/24. Root causes included inadequate training on wireline tools, lack of deepwater experience by the helper, poor communication about hazards, and failure to identify pinch point hazards.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fall from height through open hole
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid
Summary: On March 28, 2024, a Transocean Subsea Superintendent fell approximately nine feet through an open hole in a BOP work platform while performing maintenance on the Deepwater Atlas drillship. The hatch cover securing the hole had been damaged and removed during a pre-lift check without proper communication or barriers being installed. The IP was wearing a safety harness but was not tied off. He sustained a knee injury and abrasions and was evacuated for medical treatment. The incident was caused by failure to communicate the open hole, flawed hatch cover design, and lack of proper hazard analysis and barriers.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Uncontrolled descent of rope access technician
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: production
Materials: seawater, chemically treated seawater
Summary: On May 31, 2023, at 09:00 hours, a Rope Access Technician (RAT #2) employed by Southey Contracting experienced an uncontrolled descent of approximately 90 feet while inspecting the Seawater Reject Caisson (SWRC) on the Stampede tension leg platform. The technician's working rope slipped through an improperly threaded I'D descender device at the anchor point. During the fall, the technician's left leg struck a SWRC support clamp, causing lacerations and ligament damage. The fall was arrested approximately 8 feet above the waterline when the anti-error catch safety feature of the I'D engaged near the stopper knot. The injured person was rescued by an alternate rope access team and evacuated via helicopter for medical treatment.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Structural Damage to Crane Boom
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: N/A
Summary: On May 31, 2024 at 01:00 hrs, during offloading and backloading operations at GC 52 A Platform, a stinger attached to the crane's main block latched into a lace on the crane boom. Crane Operator 2 (CO-2) observed this and stopped operations, notifying the Person In Charge (PIC). The PIC instructed Crane Operator 1 (CO-1) to lower only the boom without lowering the main block, contrary to CO-2's recommendation to lower both simultaneously. As the boom was lowered, the stinger pulled tight and snapped a weld on the boom lace. The damaged crane continued in operation until June 6, 2024, when BSEE inspectors removed it from service. The boom tip was replaced at a cost of $70,000.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Severe injury from falling/shifting construction tool house during crane lift
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On May 31, 2017 at 1410 hours, an employee was severely injured when a 10,000 lb construction tool house being lifted by a platform crane came into contact with channel iron protruding from a structural member. The load shifted and struck the rigger who was positioned between the tool house and handrails, causing a fractured pelvis. Contributing factors included lack of line-of-sight communication between crane operator and rigger, ineffective radio communication, and the rigger being positioned in a pinch point hazard zone.

Top Event: Hand injury from loss of control of lowered load
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall - Hand Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 22-Feb-2014, an Exxon Mobil crew member attempted to lower an 85-pound hydraulic hose from a crane landing 38 feet above the deck using an improper technique. The crew member held a 1/2" rope to control the descent while another crew member fed the coiled hose over the railing. When the full weight of the hose went over the railing, the injured party lost control of the rope, pulling his left hand into the railing and tearing skin at the base of the thumb. The injured party was evacuated via Medevac and received medical treatment.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in accommodation cabin
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: diesel, hydraulic fluid
Summary: On March 12, 2016, at 10:06 hours, a fire occurred in accommodation cabin 417 on the drill ship Maersk Valiant during drilling operations in Alaminos Canyon 475. The smoke alarm system was activated and personnel mustered. Fire Teams responded and extinguished the fire using fire hoses after handheld fire extinguishers proved ineffective. The fire was extinguished at 10:26 hours. Forensic analysis could not definitively identify the ignition source; the Samsung tablet could not be eliminated as a possible source but could not be proven. The source was classified as undetermined. Damage was estimated at $50,000 with no injuries reported. A similar fire had occurred in the B-deck linen closet on December 13, 2015.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Lifting equipment failure resulting in dropped load
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid, mud pump discharge module
Summary: While lifting a 2.69 Ton discharge module from a 14-P-220 Mud Pump using a 3 Ton chain hoist trolley, the internal chain guides failed in a bent condition, causing the module to fall approximately 3 inches onto metal grating. Third party testing determined the failure resulted from overload conditions caused by unforeseen forces including friction from the load catching on bolts or lip edges, shock loading, and backup equipment pulling against the load. The lift plan did not accurately identify the load weight, all lifting gear, or account for deviations from the original plan.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of drill string section
Incident Type: Equipment failure due to human error
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: Heavy Weight Drill Pipe (HWDP), Bottom Hole Assembly (BHA)
Summary: On 1 April 2022, during well abandonment operations on the Helix Q-4000 semi-submersible rig in well SA001 (G-19409 Lease, Alaminos Canyon 815), four sections of 5-7/8 inch Heavy Weight Drill Pipe (HWDP) and the Bottom Hole Assembly (BHA) weighing 6000 pounds and 123 feet long disconnected from the drill string and dropped to the sea floor. The disconnection occurred on 31 March 2022 at 15:42 when a loss of 5000 pounds was recorded on the drill string weight indicator, but was not identified until 1 April 2022 at 08:20 via ROV monitoring. The root cause was determined to be an improperly torqued drill pipe connection. The lost equipment came to rest 92 meters from well center and 377 meters from the center of the safe operating zone. No subsea infrastructure damage was sustained. Recovery and inspection costs totaled $2,300,000.

Incident Type: Struck-by injury requiring evacuation
Operating Phase: production
Materials: saltwater, drilling fluid
Summary: On 08-FEB-2016 at 1315 hours, an H&P Mechanic was struck in the face by a flange associated with a butterfly valve while tightening bolts on a flange connection secured in a bench vise. The flange rotated and released from the vise due to excessive force applied during torqueing of the second-to-last bolt. The mechanic sustained lacerations above the right eyebrow and down the bridge of the nose, requiring medical evacuation and stitches. Contributing factors included improper body positioning, excessive force applied to the tool, and excessive wear on the vice jaw.

Incident Type: Equipment failure
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: casing, drilling fluid
Summary: During tripping in the hole with 22 inch casing on the landing string, a Floor Man monitoring the hydra racker drag chain noticed excessive derrick shaking and observed smoke from the main side crown cluster. The first reduction 78 inch sheave in the crown cluster was found to have a vertical crack and had shifted downward approximately 3 inches. The root cause was identified as weld defects near the root of the weld between the web plates and the hub as noted in NOV Product Bulletin No. 87819987.

Top Event: Worker pinned by Pipe Racking System arm
Incident Type: Struck-by / Crushing Injury
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drill pipe, bottomhole assembly
Summary: On October 12, 2024, at 0540 hours, a Derrickman (Injured Person/IP) working on the upper fingerboards of the Pipe Racking System during tripping operations was caught and pinned between the upper PRS arm and a stand of pipe. The IP had repositioned himself from the designated safe observation area onto the upper fingerboards without a proper working-at-heights permit to manually secure a malfunctioning fingerboard latch in the open position. While attempting to tie the latch, the PRS initiated an automatic move to retrieve a stand of pipe in the same row, extending the upper arm and pinning the IP. The IP was freed by manual intervention and evacuated for medical treatment.

Top Event: Lost Time Injury - Personnel Contact with Moving Top Drive System
Incident Type: Struck by moving equipment
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drill pipe, drilling fluid
Summary: On 8 December 2023 at approximately 08:30 am, a Floorhand sustained a lost time injury when struck by the Top Drive System (TDS) on the Noble Globetrotter II at Alaminos Canyon 735. The injured person was positioned on the port pipe racker upper access platform approximately 80 feet above the drill floor, attempting to troubleshoot a slack rope alarm. The injured person failed to notify the Driller of their return to the platform. When the Driller lowered the TDS, the lower port TDS roller assembly contacted the injured person, folding their upper torso around the handrail. The injured person sustained a punctured lung, fractured ribs, and fractured scapula. The incident resulted from inadequate communication, lack of hazard identification on the JSA, absence of engineering barriers, and no visible warnings identifying the moving equipment hazard.

Top Event: Arc Flash Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: base oil, crude oil
Summary: On October 3, 2024, at approximately 9:00 am, an arc flash fire occurred in the SCR house on Shell's Perdido SPAR when a Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR) failed. During wireline logging operations and cement pumping for hydrate mitigation, the cement unit's electronic panel lost power. Personnel entering the SCR room heard a loud popping sound and observed smoke. Investigation identified arcing between DC buss bars, arcing on circuit breaker heat sinks, and a failed buss insulator in SCR cabinet #1. The fire was not detected by the rig fire and gas alarm system and was only identified when personnel entered the SCR house. Property damage was estimated at $25,000.

Top Event: Fire in Subsea Motor Control Center
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On June 21, 2023, at 0930 hours, the Variable Frequency Device A (VFD-A) failed at the Perdido Spar platform in Alaminos Canyon Block 857, releasing smoke and activating smoke detectors in the Subsea Motor Control Center room. The failure was caused by the B-phase reactor on the VFD-A failing to ground, resulting in smoke generation. The automated facility shut-in system functioned as designed, triggering personnel muster. Cabinet A reached 350°F while other cabinets reached 210°F. No injuries or pollution were reported. The estimated replacement cost was $65,000.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Mooring Line
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: mooring chain, crude oil
Summary: On July 18, 2021, the Diana Hoover Spar facility experienced loss of mooring line #4 due to chain link failure. The 160 mm R4 grade chain link, in service for approximately 20 years, fractured at the crown and flash weld. Investigation by Stress Engineering Services determined the fractures were caused by hydrogen embrittlement resulting from improper heat treatment during manufacturing, which resulted in high hardness, low ductility, and low impact toughness. The facility remained stable with the remaining 11 mooring lines per 2010 Facility Model analysis. Estimated repair cost is $10-15 million with 6-9 months required for replacement chain manufacturing.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Total Loss of Power
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On July 17, 2021 at 10:30 hours, the Transocean Offshore Deepwater Pontus Drillship experienced a complete loss of power while conducting well completion operations on the SA 008 Well in Alaminos Canyon Block 815. The loss of power was caused by failure of an 11,000 volt to 110-volt transformer that sent 11,000 volts to ground, triggering the ground fault sensing device to trip low voltage breakers open. This caused all six power generating units to shut down, resulting in vessel drift. The crew successfully activated the Emergency Disconnect System to disconnect the LMRP from the subsea BOP within 35 seconds. The Chief Electrician manually reset the breakers to restore power after the vessel had drifted approximately 1,000 meters. The LMRP sustained damage from dragging on the seafloor for approximately 320 feet. No injuries or pollution occurred. Total property damage was estimated at $2,150,000.

Summary: On April 26, 2021 at 0100 hours, while conducting well operations on the H&P 205 rig located on Perdido Spar in AC 857, a swivel pad eye mounted on a Drill Quip Radial Bolt Connector (RBC) contacted a trolley beam during lifting of the BOP double ram. The contact caused the swivel pad eye's mounting Allen bolt to shear, allowing the pad eye to separate into four pieces and fall 26 feet to the deck below. The 5.9 lbs lifting pad eye ring landed within 2 feet of a crewmember. Total damage was $2,500.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Tree Valve Leak
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: Mono Ethylene Glycol, seawater
Summary: On 18 March 2022 at 1800 hours, during abandonment operations on Shell's Alaminos Canyon 815 well SA001 from the Helix Q-4000, a pressure test of a five-barrel cement squeeze failed to hold 1000 psi. Investigation revealed the chemical injection tree valve 1 (CIT1) on the subsea tree was leaking approximately 188 barrels of a 50/50 mixture of Mono Ethylene Glycol and seawater into the Gulf of Mexico. The CIT1 valve had not been functioned to verify closure prior to the pressure test; operators relied on documented position from well handover procedures. Manual ROV intervention and hydraulic functioning of the valve eventually stopped the leak.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fall from Height
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 23 January 2022 at approximately 1545 hours, a Blake International floorhand on the Nansen SPAR platform was instructed to ascend the derrick to check a light plug connection. After completing the task, the injured party (IP) began ascending to retrieve a Self Retracting Line (SRL) at the top of the derrick. The Driller ordered the IP to descend immediately. Upon reaching the derrick access platform approximately 11 feet above the A-frame walkway, the IP disconnected both fall protection lanyards, removing his 100 percent tie-off. The IP then lost his balance, fell off the access platform, struck his head on the A-frame walkway edge, and sustained moderate internal injuries and head lacerations requiring multiple back surgeries and sutures.

Top Event: Dropped Objects
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: riser
Summary: On December 3, 2021, at approximately 13:00 hours, the riser elevation crane on the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk drillship dropped one end of a riser joint into the riser holding area during drilling operations at MC 82. The incident resulted in damage to the aft stairway and handrail. Investigation revealed that the crane's hydraulic flow control valve had become worn and stuck in the fully engaged position, preventing proper indication of latch status to the crane operator. The evening crew flagger was new to the position and had not been trained on latch verification procedures.

Top Event: Deckhand Injury from Unbalanced Crane Load
Incident Type: LTA (>3 days)
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel
Summary: On 26 April 2017 at approximately 0830 hours, a Deckhand was injured while offloading a 550 gallon diesel tote tank from the M/V Miss Julie at platform BAA 105A. The Deckhand attached the crane's stringer to a pre-slung four-part wire rope sling, signaled to lift, but then moved toward the tote tank without stopping the lift when he noticed one sling leg was caught on a shackle. The tote tank lifted while unbalanced, swinging and pinning the Deckhand between the tank and starboard gunwale. The Deckhand sustained a fracture to his lower right leg and two fractured left ribs, requiring surgery.

Top Event: Valve actuator separation and ejection
Incident Type: Equipment failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On May 25, 2021, at approximately 05:30 hours, a valve actuator on the +12 level of Platform A at Brazos Area Block A133 separated from the valve stem and was ejected approximately 25 feet upward, striking a support beam. The valve stem and body remained intact with no pollution or gas release. The incident was not immediately reported to BSEE. Investigation revealed high pressure was suspected to have been introduced to the exhaust/vent side of the actuator control, causing release of stored energy. The facility remained shut-in pending repairs.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Subsea Leak
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Rod Piston Seal Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hydraulic fluid, natural gas
Summary: On 6 June 2022 at 1030 hours, a subsea leak of approximately 9 barrels of hydrocarbon was discovered at Well TD004 (Slot 47/MC822-13/TD004) on the Thunderhorse platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 778. The leak was caused by a rod piston seal failure in the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV). The incident was preceded by a platform power loss on 5 June 2022 that triggered an unplanned shut-in and revealed increased hydraulic fluid consumption. The leak was detected through ROV inspection and resulted in a visible sheen observed in satellite imagery and during aerial surveys. BP's subsea leak detection system (C-ROC Algorithm) was active but could not detect this relatively small discharge.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Pipeline Leak
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 8 June 2022 at 1310 hours, production personnel at MC 778 Thunderhorse South reported an external subsea leak at the P2 Riser Touchdown Connector - Pipeline End Termination (PLET) No. 5. The leak was discovered during a ROV survey initiated following a platform blackout on 5 June 2022. An estimated 12.8 to 128.1 gallons of crude oil was released. The root cause was determined to be connector failure on the PLET, potentially due to design flaw, improper installation, or manufacturing defects. The leak was temporarily sealed using a Connector Actuation Tool pending permanent replacement.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Allision with platform
Incident Type: Allision
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 6 October 2019 at 14:40, the Motor Vessel Ms. Emily struck the Breton Sound (BS) 54 #13 platform operated by Cantium, LLC. The vessel was traveling at 10 knots or less on a west northwest course when large ground swells began pushing it towards the platform. The Captain attempted to avoid collision by steering full right rudder and shifting engines to reverse, but all 4 engines died due to a safety feature that automatically shuts down engines when shifting from forward to reverse gear without allowing the required 6 second delay. The vessel's bow struck the platform, causing structural damage estimated at $150,000. There were no injuries, pollution, or loss of primary containment. No personnel were on the platform at the time of the allision.

Incident Type: Structural Damage - Allision
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 13 October 2020 at 0730 hrs, the shrimp boat Master Paul (86 feet long) collided with the upper deck of the unmanned BS 55 EB Platform in Breton Sound Block 55, Gulf of Mexico. Incident Type: Lost Time Accident - Crane Operation
Operating Phase: production
Materials: chemical transporter fluid
Summary: On 8 November 2022 at 0645 hours at MP 42 Delta platform, a contract operator (Quality Production Management) assisting with offloading a 500-gallon chemical transporter from a field boat sustained a crush injury to his left hand. As the crane lowered the transporter to the platform deck, it swung toward the injured personnel who was holding a tag line. The injured personnel placed his left hand on the transporter to stop the swinging motion, but the transporter contacted a stationary transporter on deck, crushing his hand between the two units. The injured personnel lost consciousness and was evacuated for medical treatment. Expected recovery time: 2-3 months.

Top Event: Structural damage to Crown Motion Compensator with falling object hazard
Incident Type: Equipment failure and human error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid, drill line
Summary: On September 21, 2020, at approximately 2100 hours, the Driller on the Transocean Deepwater Conqueror attempted to take drill string weight with the Crown Motion Compensator (CMC) while the CMC cylinders were at mid-stroke. The Driller opened the CMC valve without fully retracting the cylinders to the beams as required by procedure. The CMC rapidly dropped 13 feet, shearing all 7 bolts on the starboard equalizing bumper assembly bracket (48 pounds), which fell 203 feet to the drill floor. The port bumper assembly had 6 of 7 bolts sheared. A software logic error in the CMC auto-equalizing feature prevented pressure equalization before the Olmsted valve opened, causing the rapid retraction. No injuries were reported.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Spill
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, oily water
Summary: On 19-OCT-2022, during facility startup after a three-day shutdown, the back pressure valve (BPV) for the NBK 0420 Chemelectric Oil Treater failed to operate as designed. The valve remained open, allowing 94 PSI of gas into the treater, causing the Pressure Safety Valve to relieve to the relief header and vent flare. The vent flare emitted a fine mist of oily water to the atmosphere. An estimated 0.774 gallons of oil entered Gulf of Mexico waters, with satellite imagery later detecting a sheen measuring 2.44 nautical miles in length and 2.67 nautical miles at the widest point. The incident was reported to the National Response Center.

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: engine oil, diesel fuel
Summary: On 30 January 2022 at approximately 0543 hours, a fire occurred on Main Pass 73 A C/F production platform. An internal engine failure caused a piston connecting rod to break, creating a hole in the engine block. Engine oil escaped through the hole, contacted the hot exhaust manifold, and ignited. The resulting flame triggered the Temperature Safety Element, initiating platform Emergency Shut Down. The operator successfully extinguished the fire using a handheld fire extinguisher within 15 seconds.

Top Event: Fire at gas compressor engine starter
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 27 January 2022 at approximately 1625 hours, a fire occurred near the starter of a rental gas compressor on Main Pass 73-A production platform. The fire resulted from an improperly tightened JIC fitting on the engine's starter exhaust hose that backed off over time due to vibration, combined with a leaking Flow Safety Valve (FSV) that allowed natural gas to migrate to the hot exhaust area (>900 degrees). Four platform operators successfully extinguished the fire using handheld fire extinguishers within three minutes. No injuries or pollution occurred. Damage was limited to compressor wiring and FSV internal parts, estimated at $12,000.

Incident Type: Equipment Failure

Summary: On 29 July 2022 at approximately 0900 hours, a gas leak was discovered on a 3" line on the KAH-F002 High Pressure gas pipeline pig trap at West Delta 74, Platform F. Top Event: Structural Damage to Crane Aux Line Cable
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On March 14, 2021, during offloading operations from Motor Vessel Captain Rod at ST 26 CDF, a crane aux line cable experienced high strain. The crane operator lifted a load manifested at 4,300 lbs, but the actual weight was 8,500 lbs. Poor communication between the dock crane operator and dispatcher resulted in incorrect weight labeling on the manifest. Although the aux cable and winch were rated for loads exceeding 8,500 lbs, the cable experienced high strain. Post-incident inspection revealed no damage to the jib assembly, but the exact cause of aux line cable damage could not be determined.

Top Event: Injury from uncontrolled pressure release and whipping hose
Incident Type: Laceration injury requiring evacuation
Operating Phase: production
Materials: saltwater, hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 6 April 2022 at 1215 hours, a contractor from Paradigm Flow Solutions USA Inc. was injured while bleeding down pressure from a Flexi-Coil hose system on platform WD 109 A. The injured person accidentally fully opened a ball valve while a needle valve was simultaneously open, causing rapid pressure release. The Flexi-Coil hose whipped uncontrollably, striking the coil reel unit. The ball valve broke apart and the handle struck the injured person's right thumb, causing a laceration. The injured person was wearing latex gloves instead of impact-resistant gloves and was evacuated to a medical facility onshore.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge via Vent Boom
Incident Type: Pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 25 February 2022 at 0530 hours, a pollution event occurred at West Delta 73 C production platform. An estimated 660 gallons (later revised to approximately 111 gallons) of unrecoverable oil was discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the platform's vent boom. The incident resulted from a combination of a Water Skimmer float device failure (pinhole defect) and failure of the Level Safety High (LSH) device on the Vent Scrubber to operate as designed. Contributing factor was operator fatigue, with OP2 working 21 hours (0600 on 24 Feb to 0300 on 25 Feb) and becoming extremely fatigued before the incident occurred.

Top Event: Boom section collapse and crush injury
Incident Type: Structural damage and crush injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 13 May 2022 at approximately 1125 hours, a lead crane mechanic employed by Seatrax was struck and pinned by a falling boom mid-section while removing connector pins from an Aero-G15 crane on platform WD 74 F. The mechanic had struck a connector pin with a sledgehammer to remove it prior to rigging the mid-section to the bridle, contrary to proper procedure. The pin ejected unexpectedly, causing the boom sections to split and fall, pinning the mechanic's right leg between the boom and sledgehammer handle. The mechanic sustained a compound fracture and was evacuated by helicopter to hospital. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Tensioner ring disengagement from slip joint
Incident Type: Structural damage to lifting device
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drilling fluid, seawater
Summary: On August 14, 2014, at 2020 hours on the Noble Globetrotter drilling rig, the tensioner ring disengaged from the slip joint while the crew was scoping out the slip joint in preparation for diverter installation. The riser became unsupported, causing the Hands Free Goose Neck (HFGN) to separate from the tensioner ring. This resulted in extensive damage to the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP), numerous riser joints, tensioner ring, coflex housing, service lines, mux reels, hot line, and top-drive components. The root cause was the Sub-sea Engineer's failure to ensure that the locking dogs and secondary lock were fully engaged on the tensioner ring.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped Object
Incident Type: Dropped object - structural damage
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On June 25, 2019, a 162 lb packer setting tool detached from the wireline rope socket and free fell approximately 40 ft to the rig floor during well completion operations on the Deepwater Asgard Drillship. The incident was caused by a breakdown in communication between the winch operator and E-line operator during rigging down operations. The winch operator slacked off before the E-line operator, causing the lubricator weight to set down on the packer setting tool, which allowed the electric line to pull free from the rope socket. No injuries occurred; damage was limited to an indentation in the drill floor and a wooden plank.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment
Incident Type: Chemical leak in flying lead
Operating Phase: production
Materials: paraffin inhibitor, Nalco EC 6004A, naphthalene
Summary: On April 30, 2018, Murphy reported a subsea umbilical leak from a chemical injection line (Hydraulic Flying Lead) for the De Soto Canyon 4 subsea well. The leak was discovered by ROV during a subsea survey. Two leaks were identified: one on the HFL between the manifold and SUTA, and another on the pressure cap of the PLET. The chemical discharged was paraffin inhibitor containing naphthalene. The outer jacket of the hose was damaged due to chafing against the mounting bracket, compromising collapse resistance. Differential pressure across the inner sheath went negative, causing collapse and tearing of the internal pressure sheath. Maximum estimated volume discharged was 900 bbls, though BSEE calculated worst case discharge at 37,000 to 1.6 million gallons based on May 2, 2017 start date.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion
Incident Type: Explosion with personnel injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, pyrophoric iron (FeS), flammable liquids
Summary: On 24 June 2022 at approximately 1147 hours, two Gulf Island Services (GIS) employees were performing construction work on Main Pass 260 Platform P, a right-of-way platform owned by Destin Pipeline Company located 55 miles offshore in 300 feet of water. While installing a skillet to isolate the flare scrubber and flare, workers unbolted a 10-inch flange and determined the skillet did not fit. While waiting for the correct size skillet, a handheld gas detector alarmed, pressure was heard escaping from the flange, and an explosion occurred with a large plume of smoke. Both workers reported burning sensations and smoke inhalation. Both were transported to an onshore medical facility and released the same day without restrictions. No pollution or facility damage occurred. BSEE investigation identified three plausible causes: pyrophoric iron (FeS) combustion when exposed to oxygen through the opened flange (most likely), galvanic cell corrosion from a chain hoist generating electric current, or hydrate release within the piping.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Crane Boom Failure and Load Drop
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Product Tank contents (unspecified)
Summary: On September 21, 2016, at approximately 19:15 hours, a crane crew was repositioning Product Tanks on the deck of Motor Vessel Candy Barrel. The second P-Tank was lifted and repositioned alongside the first tank. The tank was initially placed at an angle. The Crane Operator picked the tank up and swung it over the water then back towards the boat. As the load swung starboard, the operator stopped it over the first tank. Vertical movement of the boat caused the bottom tank to strike the suspended tank multiple times, shock loading the crane. The wire rope parted, the suspended tank fell onto the tank below, the boom buckled and fell into the Gulf of Mexico. Estimated damage: $800,000.

Top Event: Casing weld failure and burst
Incident Type: Structural damage - equipment failure
Operating Phase: plug and abandonment
Materials: drilling fluid, crude oil
Summary: During milling operations on well A-6 with reverse circulation to unplug a rock bit, a weld failure occurred at the connection of the original 16" casing and a recently butt-welded 16" riser. The crew increased pump rate during reverse circulation, causing the rock bit to pack off and rapidly increase internal pressure in the 16" casing to over 2000 psi, exceeding the reduced burst pressure of the welded casing. The casing burst along the recently welded seams. No injuries or environmental spills occurred.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Generator Fire
Incident Type: fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel fuel, diesel engine oil
Summary: On 5-Jan-2014 at 17:30 a portable diesel generator caught fire on Platform A in the Gulf of Mexico. The generator had undergone maintenance earlier that day (09:30-10:45) with no leaks detected. The generator shut down at 17:30 and fire was observed. Platform personnel extinguished the fire using dry chemical extinguishers and cooled the unit with water. Investigation revealed a pinhole-sized leak in the secondary spin-on fuel filter, likely caused by improper installation with a strap wrench. The generator shutdown was initiated by a melted Temperature Safety Element (TSE) within the enclosure.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Crane boom collapse due to shock loading
Incident Type: Structural damage to crane
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: drill pipe, casing
Summary: During a rigless Plug and Abandonment operation at East Breaks 160 A on 01 December 2015 at approximately 2230 hours, a work string became lodged in the well casing while being lifted by the platform crane. After initial attempts to free the string using the crane failed, the crew attached a hydraulic jack system while leaving the crane still connected to the work string. When the hydraulic jack applied 275,000 pounds of pulling force, the work string suddenly released from the downhole bind, rebounded upward 4-6 feet, and then fell back into the hole. The abrupt release of energy threw the hydraulic jack's traveling slips out of the upper casing bowl, eliminating support for the work string. When the work string fell back down, its full weight was suddenly transferred to the crane's hoist system, shock loading the crane boom. The boom catastrophically buckled and collapsed onto the hydraulic jack work basket, folding in half with the jib end suspended approximately 15 feet above the manned Crane Operator's cab. No injuries, fire, or environmental pollution occurred. Damage exceeded $160,000.

Top Event: Crane shock load due to work string falling through slips
Incident Type: structural damage
Operating Phase: plug and abandonment
Materials: drill pipe, drilling fluid
Summary: On 9-December-2019 at 0700 hours, during a blind lift of 41 joints of 3-1/2" drill pipe with BHA and mechanical cutters at East Breaks 160 A platform, the work string fell through improperly seated DU-Regular slips after the crane lowered the drill pipe approximately four feet. The tool joint contacted the single joint elevators, causing a shock load to the crane. The root cause was failure to ensure personnel properly inspected slips and bowls and operated equipment per manufacturer's instructions. Contributing factors included incomplete seating of slips, inadequate lubrication, and JSA deficiencies.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire from overpressurization and rupture of fuel gas filter vessel
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, hydrocarbon fluids, glycol
Summary: On 9 May 2015 at approximately 1728 hours, the MAK-F960A Fuel Gas Filter vessel experienced catastrophic overpressurization during facility restart following a pressure upset. The vessel top was blown off, struck a nearby handrail creating sparks that ignited streaming fuel gas. The incident resulted in a fire that was extinguished by operators using portable fire extinguishers. Root causes included severe corrosion of vessel mounting tabs, failed pressure safety valves both upstream and downstream of the filter, unauthorized modifications to the filter vessels that removed differential gauges and altered level indicators, and a single-stage pressure reduction system that was inadequate for the operating conditions.

Top Event: Personnel struck by swivel socket during stud bolt replacement
Incident Type: Other Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 21 March 2015 at approximately 1500 hours, a contract worker was struck in the upper lip by a swivel socket attached to a Pneumatic Torque Unit (PTU) when it slipped off the nut of a stud bolt during production header maintenance at the Nansen SPAR. The worker sustained a laceration to the right side of the mouth, possible maxilla fracture, and dental trauma. The incident occurred because a Hydraulic Torque Unit (HTU) with a defective Emergency Shut Down was substituted with a PTU, which produces extreme pressure pulsations that can cause swivel sockets to release from nuts.

Top Event: Uncontrolled descent of suspended load
Incident Type: Equipment failure and human error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 13-Dec-2019 at approximately 16:40 hours at East Breaks 643 Boomvang SPAR, a 60-ton West crane was lifting a 33,700 lb crown section onto the derrick. As the crane operator began swinging the load to the right, the crane boom began to lower on its own. After descending approximately 15-20 feet, the operator regained control and placed the load on the elevated pipe rack. Investigation revealed debris in the boom hoist counterbalance valve orifice, which held the valve open and prevented the mechanical brake from setting. A contributing factor was the crane operator not fully releasing the joystick or setting the brakes.

Summary: At 13:50 hours on August 10, 2017, a welding flash fire occurred at the +12 level of EB-110A platform during hot work to prepare for temporary work platform installation. He was retrieved and transferred to UTMB Hospital. The fire self-extinguished. No environmental impact or additional injuries occurred.

recommendations: ["Sump pile pumped out and cleaned to eliminate hazardous vapors prior to hot work", "Construction Site Supervisor to personally verify Hot Work Permit controls adherence prior to authorization", "Safety stand down meeting to reiterate task-specific JSA use, Stop Work Authority, and communication", "Contracted construction supervisors to be personally involved in task planning, hazard identification, and mitigation verification", "Contracted Safety Representation assigned to support JSA, permit control, and hazard identification processes", "Incident awareness communicated to all similar operations", "Incident Safety Alert issued to all personnel including contractors", "Refresher training provided to Site Supervisors on Hot Work Permit controls", "Awareness training in hazard identification provided to contracted construction and cleaning services", "All contracted personnel trained and verified as competent in gas detection use"]

Top Event: Dropped Object - Lever Hoist Failure
Incident Type: Structural Damage / Dropped Object
Operating Phase: production
Materials: scaffolding, crane equipment, lever hoists
Summary: On 21-May-2025 at approximately 1:45 p.m., a crane incident occurred on the Nansen Spar in East Breaks 602 block. Contract scaffold builders were erecting scaffolding around the crane boom rest and used multiple 1/4-ton lever hoists as bracing, attaching them to both the scaffolding and the crane boom lattice without notifying the Lifting Authority. After a lunch break, the Crane Operator resumed lifting operations without conducting a pre-use inspection or checking for changes. When the operator lifted the boom out of the rest, one of the 1/4-ton lever hoists broke and fell approximately 30 feet to the flare boom mezzanine deck. The broken lever hoist weighed 0.5 pounds and caused minor property damage ($500). No personnel injuries were reported.

Summary: On 25-OCT-2014, during well completion operations on East Cameron 321A platform, a well U-Tubed forcing completion fluid out of the tubing. The fluid splashed on two crewmembers' eyes. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pressurized Oil Release
Incident Type: Pollution event with thermal burn injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hot pressurized oil
Summary: On June 23, 2014, at approximately 10:30 A.M., the Person in Charge (PIC) at Arena Offshore East Cameron 328 B facility was replacing a faulty pneumatic temperature controller (TC) on Heater Treater #2 (NBK-2770). The TC was installed directly into a thread-o-let within the liquid medium of the vessel instead of inside a thermo-well as recommended by the manufacturer. Prior to removal, the vessel was not depressurized and hot oil was not drained to a safe level. When the TC was removed, hot pressurized oil blew out of the one inch opening, striking and burning the PIC on his left bicep. Oil continued to flow across the deck, striking the line heater approximately 20 feet away. Operations personnel manually shut down the facility and installed a nipple and ball valve to isolate the leak. A total of 12 gallons of crude oil was discharged into offshore waters.

Top Event: Manway hatch structural failure and foot injury
Incident Type: Structural damage and lost time accident
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On October 19, 2017 at approximately 1911 hours, a construction employee severely injured his left foot when a 24-inch manway hatch from a water skimmer (MBM-7110) dropped and struck his foot. The hatch was being opened using a davit pole assembly for cleaning operations. The piping supporting the rod assembly failed due to severe corrosion of the davit and improper placement or detachment of the washer fastener. The employee suffered lacerations and multiple fractures to the left foot. Root causes included lack of pre-operation inspection of the davit and rod assembly condition, and inadequate Job Safety & Environmental Analysis (JSEA) that failed to capture hazards associated with the manway hatch removal.

Top Event: Finger Injury - Amputation
Incident Type: Crush Injury / Pinch Point
Operating Phase: production
Materials: casing joints, drilling fluid
Summary: An Ensco Floorhand sustained a severe finger injury on 14 February 2014 at 14:45 hours while assisting with picking up 7-inch casing joints at the v-door area on the Ensco 90 jack-up rig. The worker installed a lift nubbin in casing joint #2 and tightened it with a valve bar. When tightening the nubbin, casing joint #2 slid down the cat walk, pinning the worker's right index finger between the valve bar and the end of casing joint #3. The finger was nearly amputated at the first knuckle joint. The worker was evacuated to LSU Medical Center where surgical amputation of the distal phalanx was required. The probable cause was attributed to the casing joint not being supported by the back stop, allowing it to slip.

Operating Phase: production

Summary: On August 5, 2019 at approximately 0930 hours, an employee fractured his right ankle due to an awkward fall after coming in contact with a wheel valve handle at Cox Operating's Eugene Island 257 C platform. The employee was repairing a leaking Temperature Safety Element (TSE) located over the Flare Scrubber while standing on a ladder approximately 3 feet above the deck. When the generator shut down, the employee climbed down to assist in restarting it. While descending and looking downward, the employee's hardhat struck a wheel valve handle, forcing his head and neck back and causing his right knee to buckle. The employee fell and landed on his right leg, causing his ankle to turn in an awkward position. Pain and swelling developed on August 8, 2019, and medical evaluation confirmed a right ankle fracture.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in diesel generator exhaust system
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel fuel, crude oil
Summary: On October 21, 2020 at 0647 hours, Cox Operating operators at EI 259C observed a fire at unmanned platform EI 259A. The fire originated from the diesel generator exhaust. Investigation revealed that a governor actuator linkage failure caused engine overspeed, leading to turbo lockup and oil seal leakage. The air shut off valve failed to fully close due to oil gumming up an o-ring seal, allowing the engine to continue running and increasing exhaust temperature. Carbon buildup in the exhaust was ignited by high temperatures. A preventive maintenance inspection in January 2020 had noted suspected exhaust leak but it was not properly documented in the maintenance system.

Top Event: Loss of vessel station keeping
Incident Type: Equipment failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 1 October 2014 at approximately 17:00 hours, the offshore supply vessel (OSV) Endurance, a dynamically-positioning (DP) capable vessel, lost station keeping while conducting well operations on Well A002 at Eugene Island Block 355A platform. The vessel moved off location, causing the Well A002 production tree to be severed at the wellhead flange and fall overboard. A lubricator connected to the wireline unit fell onto the platform deck, damaging handrails and grating. An estimated 300 ft by 50 ft area of oil was spilled. Investigation revealed that a recently installed communications tower located within 1 foot of the DP system caused electrical interference, resulting in simultaneous failure of the DP system, gyro, and throttles. No injuries were reported.

Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 9 December 2021 at approximately 1030 hours, a Danos rigger (IE) was injured while assisting with laying down a 1,000-pound pulley using the platform crane on Cox Operating's EI 360-E facility. The crane operator intended to lower the pulley horizontally to the deck. However, a QCP rigger unhooked the strap while the pulley was still in vertical position and pushed it to drop horizontally. The IE moved forward with his left foot in the path of the falling pulley, resulting in contact above the steel tip of his boot. The employee sustained a fractured big toe and two dislocated toes, requiring surgery with partial amputation of the big toe. This was classified as a Lost Time Accident (>3 days) with required evacuation.

Top Event: Mechanic hand injury from rotating fan belt contact
Incident Type: Injury - Lost Time Accident (>3 days)
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, compressor oil
Summary: On 16 March 2021 at approximately 2030 hours, a mechanic suffered two fractured fingers on his left hand when he entered the compressor cooling fan housing to investigate a vibration alarm and placed his hand on a fan belt that was still rotating. The compressor had been shut down via E-stop relay, but residual energy in the fan belt was not properly isolated or dissipated. The incident was caused by human error (inattention to task) and failure to implement Stop Work Authority, Job Safety Analysis, and Lockout Tagout procedures.

Summary: On 12 February 2021 at approximately 0900 hours, a Quality Construction and Production crew was removing a heater tube from heat exchanger HBG-700 on Cox Operating's EI 360-E facility. During removal of a handrail, the third leg became stuck in the socket. The crew used a crane with a nylon strap to apply tension while attempting manual removal with hands and hammer. An employee positioned a 5-foot pry bar under the kick plate and stood up using his foot for leverage while another employee pushed the handrail. The handrail broke free under crane tension and struck the standing employee in the face, causing lacerations, fractured nose, and fractured orbital socket. The employee was transported offshore and admitted to hospital for orbital socket surgery on 16 February 2021.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in sleeping quarters
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 14-FEB-2019 at approximately 0930 hours, a fire was discovered in the upstairs sleeping quarters of Platform C in the Gulf of Mexico. Two Contract Compressor Mechanics detected smoke while replacing a piston. After approximately 30 minutes, the fire alarm sounded. Four personnel mustered and observed flames in the upper left corner of the sleeping quarters. Believing it to be an electrical fire from the window AC unit, they used a 30 lbs extinguisher followed by a 125 lbs extinguisher to suppress the fire. The mattress and surrounding items were removed through an emergency exit and the mattress was eventually tossed overboard. All personnel received medical treatment with no restrictions. The ignition source could not be determined due to discarded evidence including the mattress and an offshore bag.

Top Event: Fall from height
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On February 11, 2019 at 1640 hours, a helicopter mechanic fell while attempting to retrieve grease guns from the top of helicopter N438PH. The mechanic had recently purchased oversized work boots online that did not fit properly. While standing on a 2.5" x 4" helicopter fold-out step and holding a pitch change link, the mechanic's left foot slipped due to the boot folding inward. Top Event: Structural Damage - Crane Auxiliary Jib Detachment
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On August 20, 2023, at approximately 1000 hours, the auxiliary jib on the platform crane detached while attempting to relocate a construction supply box weighing 3,886 lbs. The crane operator failed to center the auxiliary line over the construction supply box, causing it to become lodged on a capping beam. Summary: On October 3, 2025, at approximately 1230 hours, a contract fitter sustained a severe injury to his right leg during decommissioning operations on the EI-259-C Facility. Summary: On 17 March 2025 at 2140 hours, a near miss dropped object incident occurred during well abandonment operations at Eugene Island Block 307A. Summary: On 17 July 2025 at 0752 hours during workover operations at Garden Banks Block 215, a 0.375-inch high-pressure hose ruptured while blowing down a coil tubing reel with nitrogen. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Structural failure and detachment of external walkway platform
Incident Type: Structural damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 16 July 2025 at 1810 hours, an external walkway platform on the third floor of the Temporary Living Quarters (TLQ) on Devils Tower SPAR platform unexpectedly detached from the TLQ structure by 12 to 18 inches while three Nabors employees were standing on it. The walkway remained partially secured by stanchions and stairway bolts. All personnel safely evacuated. Investigation revealed the TLQ, originally designed as temporary housing in 2002, had remained on site for 23 years. The welded tabs securing the walkway lacked sufficient welded surface area and were subject to fatigue and corrosion in the marine environment. Visual-only inspections in May 2024 failed to detect the deteriorated weld condition.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Casing lifting pin failure striking worker
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane Incident
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: 7-inch casing, crude oil
Summary: On July 12, 2025, at approximately 3:30 p.m., a casing lifting pin failed during well abandonment operations on the Vermilion Block 38M platform operated by Chevron U.S.A. Inc. The failed pin, which was bent into a U-shape, struck a Supreme Services employee in the face, causing a subdural hematoma, blunt trauma, an open fracture of the right side of the base of the skull, and multiple closed fractures of facial bones. The incident resulted in a required evacuation via medevac helicopter. Root causes included: (1) installation of a spool riser with an 11.00-inch inside diameter above a seal ring with an 11.44-inch outside diameter, causing the casing to become stuck; (2) the crane operator exceeding the safe working load limit of the casing lifting pin (60,000 pounds) while attempting to lift the stuck casing with the main hoist; and (3) incorrect installation of the two-part sling with D-rings not placed directly adjacent to the casing. Independent testing demonstrated that when D-rings were positioned 3-4 inches from the casing (as they were during the incident), the pin failed at 102,500 pounds, compared to successful performance at 150,000 pounds when D-rings were properly positioned adjacent to the casing.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Nitrogen bottle rupture and explosion
Incident Type: explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: nitrogen, jet fuel, diesel
Summary: On April 28, 2025, at approximately 10:30 a.m., a nitrogen bottle ruptured and exploded during refilling operations on the BW Pioneer FPSO in Walker Ridge Block 249. The bottle had been mistakenly assumed to be rated for 6,000 psi when it was only rated for 2,400 psi, and was recharged to more than double its maximum capacity. Additionally, an incorrect relief valve rated for 9,000 psi had been installed instead of the appropriate 2,400 psi valve. The explosion caused moderate to severe structural damage to the bottle rack, manifold, diesel fuel tank, and nearby equipment. A crane operator located approximately 18 feet away was struck by flying debris, sustaining minor bruises, a small neck lesion, and temporary hearing impairment. The root cause was over-pressurization due to equipment failure (incorrect relief valve) combined with human error (failure to identify correct pressure rating) and inadequate procedures and training.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Well Gas Release
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: well gas, grease, oil
Summary: On 27 April 2020 at 03:00 hours, while C&D Wireline LLC was conducting wireline operations to set Bottom Hole Pressure gauges, the wireline tool string parted at the Surface Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV) at 427 feet. The severed wire was pushed up and out the stuffing box, causing a small release of well gas through the stuffing box pack off assembly and high-pressure grease injection pack off hose. The influx of gas ruptured the small grease seal unit oil reservoir. The well was immediately secured with wireline BOPs and crown valve closure. No injuries or environmental damage occurred.

Top Event: Unintentional discharge of synthetic based mud
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: synthetic based mud, base oil
Summary: On November 16, 2020, approximately 458 barrels of synthetic based mud (247 barrels of base oil) were unintentionally discharged into the Gulf of Mexico during fluid transfer operations on the Nabors MODS 200 rig at Ewing Banks Block 873. The discharge resulted from improper valve alignment, specifically an overboard discharge line (dump line valve) left in the open position. Contributing factors included failure of personnel to follow procedures and verify valve alignment prior to pumping, and falsification of documentation indicating verification had been completed when it had not.

Top Event: Accidental shearing of drill pipe
Incident Type: Equipment failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid, cement
Summary: On March 11, 2015, the H&P 203 rig accidentally sheared a 5 7/8" drill pipe while setting a test plug. The Blind Shear Rams (BSR's) closed unexpectedly on the drill pipe after a contact switch in the rig floor panel for the close side of the shear ram function was found stuck in the closed position. A rig crew member pressed the 'Push to Operate' button intending to perform a different function, but due to the stuck contact switch, the BSR's fired automatically, shearing the pipe and leaving a test plug, pup joint, and 22.35 feet of drill pipe in the hole.

Top Event: Allision - Motor Vessel collision with Tension Leg Platform
Incident Type: Structural Damage / Crane / Other Lifting
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: pipeline riser segment
Summary: On 02 February 2020 at 1230 hours, a Motor Vessel engaged in pipeline riser disconnection and abandonment operations at the Ewing Banks 921-A tension leg platform experienced an allision with the platform. The vessel's 300-ton winch became overwhelmed by the pipeline weight, the shackle wedged into the TLP sheave, and despite 100% power application, the vessel was pulled toward the platform resulting in collision. A hold-back cable severed, increasing reverse tension on the deck winch cable. Total damage: $336,000 (TLP: $161K, M/V: $175K).

Top Event: Hose ejection striking worker
Incident Type: Injury from pressurized fluid discharge
Operating Phase: production
Materials: produced water, flowback fluids, crude oil
Summary: On 26-NOV-2017 at 0405 hours, during flowback operations on the Ewing Banks 834-A platform, a roustabout (R2) was struck in the right upper collarbone by a pressurized hose that ejected from an Auxiliary Mud Tank. The incident occurred when Roustabout 1 failed to communicate that pressurized air from a rig compressor airline remained connected to a 3-inch transfer hose. When R2 lifted the hatch to verify fluid receipt, approximately 125 psi of air and fluids discharged, causing the hose to strike him. Root causes included inadequate communication between roustabouts, failure to secure the hose, failure to follow operating procedures, and insufficient supervisor delegation. The injured person was evacuated and placed on restricted duty, with full duty release scheduled for 1/15/2018.

Top Event: Fire in engine room
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: diesel, SAE 15W40 motor oil, air filters
Summary: On 03-MAR-2022 at 0315 hours, the diesel engine for the #3 generator on the Nabors MODS 200 rig threw a piston rod through the engine block, allowing hot motor oil (220 gallons of SAE 15W40) to spray onto nearby stored air filters in the engine room. The filters ignited, causing a fire. The fire was extinguished with dry chemical extinguishers and water. No injuries were reported. Root cause was piston rod failure; contributing cause was improper storage of flammable materials (air filters) in the engine room due to lack of storage space.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment
Incident Type: Well Control and Pollution Event
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hydrocarbons, natural gas
Summary: On 10 January 2022, a well control and pollution event occurred at WD 80 D production platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident originated from the D-36 well where an alignment pin plug on the tubing head had been leaking gas since 11 January 2021. During a coiled tubing operation to set a plug and clean scale, the tool became stuck downhole and required mechanical extraction, which exacerbated tubing-to-casing communication. This caused the A Annulus pressure to rise from 50 psi to 2000 psi, resulting in excessive fluid returns to the wet oil tank. Approximately 107 gallons (2.5 barrels) of hydrocarbons were spilled. The platform was evacuated and remediation efforts continued through March 2022. Root causes included equipment failure (packer assembly design flaw, alignment pin corrosion due to inadequate coating maintenance), human performance error during coiled tubing operations, and organizational failures in communication and urgency to remediate.

The platform was evacuated and remediation efforts continued through March 2022. recommendations: ["Operators should maintain proper coatings on wellhead equipment in corrosive saltwater environments", "Well intervention plans should account for potential exacerbation of fluid returns and include adequate vessel and fluid handling capacity", "Timely reporting of well anomalies to regulatory authorities is critical for prompt remediation", "Scale and paraffin buildup should be managed proactively to enable safe well interventions", "Management communication systems should ensure field observations are escalated and acted upon promptly"]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - H2S Gas Release
Incident Type: H2S gas release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydrogen sulfide (H2S), sour gas, crude oil and gas liquids
Summary: On 29-May-2016 at 1310 hrs, a hydrogen sulfide (H2S) gas release of 400 ppm occurred on Main Pass 299-FP Platform during pigging operations on the flowline from MP 299-A to FP Platform. While the crew drained fluids from the pig trap, H2S sensors detected high concentrations of sour gas. The H2S alarm sounded and the platform was shut in. All personnel donned SCBAs and mustered. After an initial all-clear declaration, the H2S alarm sounded again. The response team located a gas leak in the 2-inch pipe outlet from the MBF-935 Buyback Gas Scrubber below the south side cellar deck. Investigation determined the leak was caused by extensive exterior corrosion that compromised the piping integrity. The root cause was identified as failure of the operator to perform routine corrosion assessment and maintenance on piping. The field was subsequently shut in indefinitely as continued operation was deemed uneconomical.

Top Event: Loss of Time Injury from Fall
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On February 7, 2024, at approximately 09:30 am, a loss time injury occurred when a Danos construction worker attempted to climb an out-of-service scaffolding ladder attached to the ExxonMobil Pipeline Company Motor Control Center/Control Building (ZZZ-006) on the Galveston A 244 JP platform. The ladder, which had been placed out of service on January 1, 2024 with the Self-Retracting Lanyard (SRL) removed and no visual identification tags, shifted when the worker reached the 3rd rung, causing him to jump to the deck and sustain injuries to both legs. The incident resulted from multiple failures including inadequate scaffolding construction, lack of proper out-of-service marking, missing fall protection equipment, inadequate work permits that did not address working at heights, poor communication between operators and contractors, and lack of coordination between multiple contractors on the platform.

Top Event: Explosion and Fire at Pig Launcher
Incident Type: Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, sales gas
Summary: On November 8, 2017 at 1:18 a.m., an explosion occurred at the 30-inch sales gas pipeline pig launcher on the Enchilada platform (GB Block 128) operated by Shell Offshore Inc. Two operators were preparing to launch a pig when the pig launcher closure door failed explosively, propelling the door and other items overboard. A fire ensued, fed by natural gas from the pipeline. One operator sustained concussion, back injuries, and second-degree burns; the other suffered minor injuries. All 46 platform personnel were safely evacuated. The root cause was incomplete closure of the clamp rings due to procedural failures in securing the closure door, combined with inadequate hazard analysis, improper management of change, and insufficient supervision.
