Top Event: Hand entrapment in winch sheave during wireline valve assembly hoisting
Incident Type: Serious hand injury - partial amputation
Operating Phase: well abandonment
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 14 March 2018 at 16:40 hours, a Helix Energy Solutions Roughneck sustained serious injuries to his left hand during well abandonment operations on the Helix Q4000 semisubmersible vessel. While hoisting a wireline valve assembly using a coiled tubing lift frame (CTLF) attached to a multipurpose tower, the worker observed the assembly beginning to shift and potentially contact the CTLF. In an attempt to reposition the assembly, the worker placed his left hand on the static line above the sheave on the CTLF main winch. His hand became trapped between the main winch sheave and the static line cable, resulting in partial amputation of the left thumb and severe laceration of the left ring finger. The worker was evacuated by helicopter and required surgery.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Base Mud Spill
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: synthetic base mud (SBM), 16.9 ppg drilling fluid
Summary: At approximately 23:00 hours on 23 May 2014, the Diamond Ocean Onyx semi-submersible drilling rig reported a loss of synthetic base mud (SBM) during abandonment operations on Well #1. Initial report of 107 bbl was revised to 61 bbl after strapping calculations. The spill occurred when mud line valves on the Schlumberger Cement Unit were left in the open position after cleaning operations and were not checked prior to the cement job. When the Cementer began pumping cement from Batch Mixing Tank #2, the tank overflowed, causing SBM to spill into secondary containment areas and then into drains leading to the overboard discharge line, which also had open valves. The Toolpusher closed the overboard drain line valves at 23:30 hours, preventing further discharge. No injuries or property damage occurred.

recommendations: ["BSEE Lafayette District recommends a Safety and Environmental Management Systems (SEMS) audit be conducted at the Shell Auger TLP rig"]

Top Event: Structural damage to drill pipe during pipe racker lifting operations
Incident Type: Equipment failure due to human error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: 3.5-inch drill pipe
Summary: On 10 December 2021 at 0030 hours, a lifting incident occurred during Well A019 tripping in the hole operations on H&P Rig 406 fixed on Shell's Auger TLP. A single joint of 3.5-inch drill pipe was damaged when the Lower Pipe Racker Operator (LPRO) pulled his end ahead of the Upper Pipe Racker Operator (UPRO), causing the top of the drill pipe stand to get under the elevators as the traveling block was coming down. The pipe bowed and threads were damaged. No injuries occurred. Root causes included LPRO inexperience (second operation, 8 months on rig), inadequate Job Safety Analysis, and inadequate Job By Design Template that failed to specify waiting until block is below fingerboard before removing next stand.

Top Event: Hand Crush Injury During BOP Maintenance
Incident Type: Serious Hand Injury - Crush/Entrapment
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid, subsea equipment
Summary: On 29 May 2017, a third-party SubC technician sustained a serious crush injury to his left hand while adjusting Belleville springs on a subsea BOP lower gripper assembly aboard the Noble Don Taylor drill ship. The technician placed his left hand in a gap between the connector plate and landing sleeve for anchoring while using a ratchet extension with his right hand. When the Noble Subsea Engineer actuated the gripper to unlatch and block positions, the connector plate fell and trapped the technician's hand against the landing sleeve. A hydraulic hand pump was used to free the hand. The technician was evacuated by helicopter and required surgery resulting in amputation of four fingers and part of his thumb.

Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drill pipe
Summary: On 28 September 2020 at approximately 0049 hours, while setting down single joint drill pipes onto the construction floor using the construction hoist block on the Noble Globetrotter I Drill Ship, the construction hoist block descended uncontrollably without input from the driller. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea BOP Seal Failure
Incident Type: Leak / Pollution
Operating Phase: exploration
Materials: synthetic oil base mud (SOBM), drilling fluid
Summary: On 7 June 2017, the Noble Don Taylor drillship under contract to Shell reported an unauthorized discharge of approximately 94.2 barrels of 15.1 ppg synthetic oil base mud during exploration operations on Well DC005 (Cardamom) in Garden Banks Block 427, Gulf of Mexico. The spill was caused by failure of the upper variable bore ram (VBR) aft bonnet door cartridge seal on the subsea blowout preventer. Contributing factors included buildup of debris in the upper VBR body, failure to conduct proper hole cleaning and flushing of the BOP as recommended in NOV Product Information Bulletin released 11 August 2015, and inadequate communication of known risk of loss of seal integrity.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Unauthorized discharge of synthetic oil base mud
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: synthetic oil base mud (SOBM), 15.3 ppg drilling fluid
Summary: On 23 March 2015 at 0505 hours, the Rowan Reliance drill ship under contract to Cobalt International Energy reported a discharge of approximately 2200 barrels of 15.3 ppg synthetic oil base mud (SOBM) during well operations on Well 002 (North Platte #2) in Garden Banks Block 958 at 4846 ft water depth. The primary cause was the failure to install a Seal Sub (Mud Pin) in the flange connection between the flex joint termination spool and the first riser joint (Joint #64). Contributing factors included inadequate procedures, insufficient crew handover, failure to recognize the SOBM buildup during ROV coverage, command structure issues, and under-torqued bolts (1250 psi instead of required 1600 psi). Of the 2200 bbl lost, 1557 bbl entered open water and 643 bbl were lost in open hole. Property damage totaled $151,353.

Top Event: Shallow Water Flow from Low-Pressure Wellhead Housing
Incident Type: Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: crude oil, seawater, drilling mud, cement
Summary: On March 28, 2021, at approximately 1921 hours, personnel observed a steady flow from the Lower Cement Port 1 on the 38-inch Low-Pressure Wellhead Housing during riserless drilling operations with the Transocean Deepwater Poseidon drillship at Garden Banks Block 962. The flow occurred approximately 7 hours after 22-inch casing cement was placed. Analysis revealed the flow originated from above the top of salt in a dirty salt section, migrating through a thin channel in the cement behind the 22-inch casing. Fluid samples contained trace hydrocarbons and high chlorides indicative of seawater. The well was temporarily abandoned on April 25, 2021.

Top Event: Loss of Hand Function - Finger Amputation and Crushing Injury
Incident Type: Crush Injury / Lost Time Accident
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, drilling fluid
Summary: While removing a gearbox coupling and mud agitator shaft for repair on the T.O. Discoverer India, the injured person (IP) and mechanic deviated from the written risk assessment (WRA) procedure. The IP installed support beams out of sequence and attempted to adjust a safety clamp without first securing it properly. While hammering an Allen wrench onto a corroded bolt head on the agitator shaft, the 750-pound shaft and coupling suddenly separated from the gearbox output shaft and fell, crushing the IP's fourth and fifth fingers between the wrench and support beams. The incident was preceded by three prior unexpected separations of the agitator shaft from the gearbox output shaft (2010, 2014, 2015), but no additional precautionary measures were implemented.

recommendations: ["Update procedures and WRAs to include hazards associated with unexpected coupling failure and separation of agitator shaft from gearbox output shaft", "Implement additional precautionary measures to mitigate the hazard of unexpected coupling failure", "Enforce company policy requiring all participating members of a job task to individually sign the WRA", "Ensure personnel wear appropriate personal protective equipment (impact gloves) during repair tasks"]

Top Event: Gas Release
Incident Type: Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: well abandonment
Materials: natural gas, hydrocarbon, calcium bromide brine (CaBr2), nitrogen
Summary: On 15 November 2024 at 0837 hours, a gas release occurred in the shaker house aboard the Valaris DS-16 drillship during well abandonment operations. While displacing the kill line and landing string back to calcium bromide brine after nitrogen displacement, hydrocarbons were released into the shaker house atmosphere. The incident was triggered by a compromised liquid leg in the mud gas separator, allowing hydrocarbon gas to bypass the separator and enter the shaker house. A hydrocarbon alarm activated, prompting evacuation and crew muster. Gas levels returned to zero and operations resumed without further issues.

Top Event: Drill pipe parting and loss of drill string
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Structural Damage
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: crude oil, drilling fluid, salt formation
Summary: On 03 August 2025 at 1934 hours, during drilling of the 26-inch top-hole open water section of Well SP202 at Garden Banks Block 959, a torque spike of 71.8 kft-lbf occurred while drilling salt at 9699 feet depth. This caused a 410 kips reduction in hook load, full loss of torque, loss of hook load, and loss of pump pressure. The drill string separated below the water line. Approximately 4128 feet of 6.625-inch drill pipe fell to the seafloor, with an additional 5350 feet of bottom hole assembly and drill pipe left in the wellbore. The ROV tether was trapped in at least three locations by the fallen pipe. No injuries were reported and no personnel were in the red zone at the time of incident.

Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid, hydraulic fluid, diesel
Summary: On 22-November-2019 at Garden Banks 491, during tripping in operations with an 11-7/8" Easy Drill Subsurface Valve, a loss of power to drilling equipment occurred. Personnel received an alarm indicating smoke or fire in the Port Switch Gear Room. Investigation revealed that condensation accumulated on top of the Drilling Drive DC Bar cabinet from the Air Handling Unit ducting vent and ran inside the cabinet, causing an Arc Flash fire. The fire was extinguished by mechanics using a handheld fire extinguisher. Damage to the Drilling Drive DC Bar Cabinet, AHU ductwork, vent, and insulation totaled $65,000.

Top Event: Amputation of fingers due to falling wireline tool and pack-off assembly
Incident Type: Traumatic injury - amputation
Operating Phase: production
Materials: wireline, electric line, hydraulic fluid
Summary: On August 13, 2020, a Floorhand working in a riding belt on the H&P 100 rig was hoisted to guide wireline tool string out of a lubricator. Summary: On 16-OCT-2014 at 2250 hours, a blown fuse on the #4 generator aboard the Ensco 8502 caused loss of four of eight thrusters. Approximately 150 barrels of SBM were released from the riser and choke/kill hoses. No injuries occurred. The well remained secure and undamaged.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Uncontrolled descent of traveling block and top drive
Incident Type: Equipment failure and human error
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drilling fluid, hydraulic fluid
Summary: On June 9, 2014, while troubleshooting a brake fault alarm on the drawworks system during pipe transfer operations, the Chief E.T. performed a hard reset of drive #4 without confirming that the drawworks brakes were set. Due to miscommunication between the Toolpusher and Chief E.T., the reset interrupted motor control while the top drive was still being held up, causing the traveling block to freefall. The Assistant Driller activated the emergency hydraulic dump, which caused the brakes to slam shut but failed to prevent impact with the rig floor. Damage occurred to drill pipe, the Driller's shack, and the traveling assembly. Root cause analysis identified sticking hydraulic solenoid valves due to debris accumulation and improper hydraulic pump installation.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, air, glycol
Summary: On 23-DEC-2018 at approximately 1800 hours, an explosion occurred inside Cooler HAL-4160B on the Allegheny Sea platform (OCS-G 7049, Green Canyon 254-A) operated by Eni US Operating Co. Inc. The explosion was caused by ignition of a lower explosive limit (LEL) mixture of air and natural gas within the process piping. The root cause was that instrument air (instead of instrument gas as configured by the manufacturer) was connected to the temporary rental Booster Compressor CAE-5140A. This allowed continuous air from a Kimray pressure control valve to enter the inlet suction scrubber and mix with gas from the vent system through an open Blowdown Valve (BDV). The explosion separated cooler end caps, damaging the cooler module, PSV, discharge piping, ESD station #7, main deck south guardrail, and other equipment. The ESD station shut in the facility, but the compressor engine continued running until manually shut down by the mechanic after the emergency stop button failed. All personnel mustered safely with no injuries. Contributing factors included incomplete Management of Change (MOC) process, lack of pre-startup review, unavailable operating procedures, and installation not performed as recommended by the manufacturer.

Top Event: Hand Crush Injury from Pinch Point
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall - Hand Crush
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 04-JUN-2021 at 0930 hours, an ROV Deckman inadvertently placed his left hand in a pinch point between the two cantilever support areas (A-Frame and lower pedestal) of the ROV lifting frame during ROV launch operations on the Seadrill West Neptune. The Deckman immediately contacted the Winch Operator by radio to stop and retract the A-Frame. The Deckman was medivaced to shore for evaluation. Investigation found that the TBRA did not identify the pinch point hazard, the red zone was unidentified, and the Deckman had become complacent during a routine operation.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Crane sheave failure with dropped objects
Incident Type: Structural damage - crane incident
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling mud
Summary: On September 16, 2019, at approximately 1700 hours, a sheave failure occurred during a critical lift of Mud Tank #1 module (92.5k pounds, approximately 96% of crane capacity) using the port side crane main hoist at Green Canyon Block 338 Platform A. The main line tip Nylatron idler sheave shattered, resulting in dropped objects that struck two injured persons (IPs) attending tag lines. One IP was struck on the right forearm and right boot tip; the other on left middle and ring fingers. Both received first aid and returned to full duty. Investigation revealed corrosion of the outer bearing race and mild side loading during the heavy lift as probable causes. The roller bearing had not been replaced in 17 years, had exterior corrosion, missing lubrication seals, and surface corrosion on the outer race.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - LP Pipe Rupture
Incident Type: Pipe Rupture / Hydrocarbon Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, scale inhibitor, demulsifier
Summary: On 16-OCT-2015 at 2015 hours, an 18-inch LP pipe ruptured on the Front Runner spar at GC 338A, releasing approximately 25 barrels of oil and undetermined amount of gas. The rupture occurred at the chemical injection point where the line penetrated the production deck. Root cause was severe internal corrosion (metal loss up to 50.4%) caused by acidic chemical injection without proper distribution via quills. Contributing factors included failure to use quills for chemical distribution and inadequate NDT testing that did not cover injection point areas. One employee was injured. Total estimated damage: $1.5 million.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unauthorized discharge of Zinc Bromide due to Emergency Disconnect Sequence activation
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: Zinc Bromide (ZnBr2), drilling fluid
Summary: On January 15, 2022, at approximately 16:10 hours, the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse experienced a Dynamic Position (DP) system failure due to misalignment of Thruster-2 (180 degrees out of alignment) during completion operations at Green Canyon Block 389. High winds and sudden wind direction changes caused the misaligned thruster to push the drillship in the same direction as the wind, compromising DP position into red alert status. The Emergency Disconnect Sequence was initiated, unlatching the Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP) from the Blow Out Preventers (BOP). This resulted in an unauthorized discharge of approximately 420-558 barrels of 15.1 PPG Zinc Bromide (ZnBr2) into the Gulf of Mexico. The incident was contributed to by the operator's failure to complete the submitted Well Suspension Procedure in a safe and timely manner despite knowing inclement weather was approaching.

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid, crude oil
Summary: On May 10, 2018, a fire occurred in the mud pit room of the drillship Transocean Deepwater Asgard while commissioning a Precision Fluid Injection (PFI) System. The fire was caused by incorrect wiring of temperature control modules to the wrong junction box, resulting in false temperature readings that prevented the heater safety shut-off from activating. The heater continued to heat the drilling fluid beyond safe limits, causing the discharge hose to ignite. The fire was detected by flame, heat, and smoke detectors and extinguished with portable fire extinguishers. No injuries were reported.

Top Event: Dropped casing joint
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane
Operating Phase: production
Materials: casing
Summary: On April 7, 2017, a joint of 9 7/8" casing was dropped by the Pipe Deck Pipe Handler (PDPH) crane on the Seadrill Sevan Louisiana while being transferred between pipe bays. A leaking hydraulic seal on the aft pipe adapter cylinder caused the gripper to lose closing pressure, allowing the casing to slip during the lift. The crane operator had to periodically re-initialize the casing settings due to the hydraulic leak but did not identify this as a system malfunction. All personnel were in safe zones and no injuries or equipment damage resulted.

Top Event: Fire in Flash Gas Compressor skid
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydrocarbons, condensate, crude oil
Summary: At approximately 2350 hours on May 7, 2014, a fire occurred in the skid of the Flash Gas Compressor-B on the Marco Polo TLP. Two Platform Operators drained condensate from the 1st Stage Suction Scrubber into the compressor skid in an attempt to restart the compressor. Hydrocarbons were released and vapors reached an unknown ignition source, possibly static charge, causing a fire that spread to CLX cable trays throughout the facility. The fire was eventually controlled with AFFF and BC fire suppressants after the firewater system proved ineffective on the secondary fire.

Top Event: Loss of Load Control
Incident Type: Dropped Object
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: 14-inch casing, drilling fluid
Summary: On December 20, 2020, a 4,725-pound joint of 14-inch casing fell across the rig floor of the Transocean Deepwater Invictus drillship after the clamp type elevators (CTE) unlatched when the joint was lowered to the rig floor during troubleshooting of casing slips. The RBC panel operator prematurely functioned the CTE to 'slips up' position during troubleshooting, which allowed the elevators to open and release the casing when weight was removed. Top Event: Dropped Object - Sheared Bolts and Falling Actuator Plates
Incident Type: Structural Damage / Dropped Object
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid
Summary: On January 18, 2015, during drilling operations at 2140 hours, the PS-1000 drill pipe slips set unexpectedly while the driller was rotating at 180 RPM after making a connection. The sudden stop of the drill string created immense torque that was transferred to the MDDM (Modular Derrick Drilling Machine), causing bolts on two of the I-BOP's actuator plates to be sheared. One plate fell approximately 30 feet to the bridge-racker walkway, and the other fell approximately 130 feet to the rig floor. Each plate weighed 19 pounds. No personnel were in the impact areas and no injuries occurred. The root cause was a combination of poor weather conditions that prevented rig repositioning and equipment failure where downward force from the drill string overcame the hydraulic pressure holding the slips open.

Top Event: Dropped Load - Riser Joint

Operating Phase: production

Summary: On April 11, 2017, while picking up riser to begin well 003 in Green Canyon Block 627, the riser gantry crane (RGC) on the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet dropped a joint of riser approximately 19 feet in the riser bay. The RGC operator received a false 'Green Light' signal indicating proper capture when the hooks were not fully engaged. When the remote console was powered down for reboot, the aft crane hook lost hydraulics and released the riser joint, which fell approximately 15 feet and struck additional riser joints before falling an additional 4 feet to the bottom of the riser bay. No personnel were injured due to barricading of the work area.

Top Event: Arc flash in electrical cabinet DBU 7
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, drilling fluid
Summary: On May 26, 2017, smoke alarms in the port 690 V switchboard room of the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet drillship triggered a required muster. An arc flash occurred in Dynamic Braking Unit (DBU) 7 due to blown fuses that had not been replaced since May 10, 2017. Carbon buildup in the fuses created a path for electrical current, enabling arcing through an IGBT switch. The arcing destroyed the fuse housing and jumped to the cabinet frame, causing an arc flash that destroyed the cabinet and activated smoke alarms. Estimated damage was $50,000.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in Pressurized Welding Enclosure
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: welding materials, Panther felt, electrical wiring
Summary: On June 27, 2022, at approximately 1755 hours, a fire occurred inside a Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) on the Northeast side of Green Canyon Block 645 Platform A (Holstein). The fire was caused by improperly wrapped Panther felt on Zone 4 deluge wiring, which allowed slag from hot work to smolder inside the wrapping. The fire was discovered when the firewater pump activated and deluge Zone 4 was triggered by the burning wires that controlled the deluge system. Contributing factors included only one Firewatch being present during the 30-minute cool-down period instead of the required two, and fire and smoke detection systems being disabled when the hot work control panel was turned off.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Equipment Overboard
Incident Type: Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drill pipe
Summary: On February 3, 2021, at 02:00 hours, a Nabors Offshore night Crane Operator operating the West knuckle boom crane on the Holstein Spar Rig was transferring a single joint of 5.5-inch drill pipe (31.5 feet long, 810 pounds) from the pipe conveyor to the pipe rack. The Crane Operator gripped the pipe after only 2 seconds instead of the manufacturer-required 5 seconds, and the gripper feet were not in full contact with the pipe before gripping. When the operator abruptly stopped the boom swing, the pipe gripper shook and bounced erratically, causing the drill pipe to slip out and fall approximately 13 feet onto the pipe rack. The pipe then bounced and slid over the handrail into the Gulf of Mexico. No injuries were reported. Investigation determined the cause was operator error: failure to follow manufacturer's operating instructions regarding grip time and proper gripper foot contact.

Summary: On February 14, 2021 at 0330 hours, a stand of 5.5-inch drill pipe fell approximately 4 feet from the Pipe Racking System (PRS) gripper to the rotary table during drilling operations on the Holstein Spar Rig. Top Event: Lateral Support Guide Frame Collapse
Incident Type: Structural Damage / Fall from Height
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On May 19, 2018, while rigging down blow out preventers (BOPs) and high pressure riser on the A-8 well aboard the Holstein Spar Rig, a lateral support guide frame (LSG) fell approximately 17 feet after two employees removed bolts securing the frame to the riser. The crew removed all nuts from sixteen studs contrary to the planned procedure and the Driller's instructions. The Toolpusher, newly promoted and unfamiliar with the procedure, instructed the crew to remove the bolts before the LSG frame was properly supported. Both employees were injured and evacuated by medivac.

Top Event: Thermal burn injury from ruptured sight glass
Incident Type: Other Injury - Burns to the body
Operating Phase: production
Materials: sewage, hot fluids
Summary: On November 7, 2015, an Engine Room Operator sustained first and second degree burns on approximately 40% of his body when an inspection glass on a Marine Sanitation Device (MSD) vacuum pump ruptured, ejecting hot fluids and fractured glass. The operator was troubleshooting an alarm on the MSD when the incident occurred. The direct cause was excessive scale buildup in the discharge line causing overpressurization of the pump, as no pressure relief valve or rupture disk was installed to prevent overpressuring.

Top Event: Dropped Objects from Casing Elevators
Incident Type: Structural Damage
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: 9-7/8" x 10-7/8" tieback casing, drilling mud
Summary: On January 15, 2021, at 15:26 hours during drilling operations on the Seadrill West Vela in 6,534 feet of water, an 8-pound leveling cone and a 104-pound bell guide insert fell approximately 90 feet from Frank's International spider type casing elevators to the rig floor. The incident occurred when the casing became wedged between the VersaFlo tool and the bell guide due to slight misalignment. The elevators tilted on the Top Drive System bails, causing the leveling cone to shear off and the bell guide insert to dislodge. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by misalignment between the VersaFlo tool and bell guide, combined with inadequate secondary retention devices and excessive weight on the elevators.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unintentional discharge of Synthetic Oil Based Mud
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM)
Summary: On September 13, 2017, the Mad Dog Spar Rig unintentionally discharged 71 barrels of Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM) into the Gulf of Mexico due to improper valve alignment while cleaning the mud pill pit. The Derrickman and Floorhand used a valve line-up checklist to verify valve positions, but an error occurred. When the overboard discharge valve was opened and the pump activated, SBM was pumped overboard for approximately eight minutes before the pump was turned off and the valve closed. A leaking splitter valve may have been a contributing factor but was not the primary cause.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unintentional discharge of synthetic oil-based mud into the Gulf of Mexico
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: synthetic oil-based mud (SOBM), wash water
Summary: On February 27, 2021, approximately 13 barrels of synthetic oil-based mud (SOBM) containing 6.24 barrels of synthetic oil were inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico during a wash water overboard transfer operation on the Diamond Ocean Black Hornet drilling rig. The discharge resulted from improper valve alignment at the mud mix manifold combined with inadequate communication during a tour change. The #1 mud mixing pump was left running while circulating mud in the #11 and #13 active mud pits, but this critical information was not properly communicated to the incoming crew. When the overboard discharge valve was opened, mud was discharged instead of wash water. The incident was detected when the Driller noticed volume loss in the active mud pits and immediately notified the Derrickhands, who shut down the pump and closed the valve.

Top Event: Personnel struck by suspended manual tongs
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall - Struck by Object
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On February 10, 2017 at 0223 hours, a Rowan Floorhand was struck in the back by manual tongs while assisting with pipe tripping operations on the drillship Rowan Resolute in Green Canyon Block 859. A hydraulic hose on the hydra-tong had ruptured, requiring the crew to switch to manual tongs. The manual tongs were suspended by a hoist line and secured by a half-inch tie back rope. As the driller lowered the top drive to latch onto pipe, a clamp on the kelly hose caught the hoist line, hoisting the tongs higher. When the driller stopped the top drive, the tie back rope broke and the tongs swung toward the rotary table, striking the Floorhand who was standing in the permanent Red Zone facing away from the suspended load. The Floorhand sustained a bruise but no broken bones or internal injuries.

Top Event: Structural damage to marine riser and LMRP during hurricane evacuation
Incident Type: Equipment failure - weather related
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: synthetic based mud, seawater
Summary: On October 28, 2020, the Transocean Deepwater Asgard drillship was forced to evacuate location due to Hurricane Zeta. Despite initial forecasts indicating the storm would pass to the east, the hurricane's actual path brought it directly over the vessel. The decision to remain connected to the well until the last moment, combined with inaccurate weather forecasting, resulted in the drillship being pushed off location by extreme winds (peak gust 152 mph). During evacuation, the marine riser impacted the hull and the LMRP struck the seafloor twice, causing approximately $5.7 million in damage to the riser, LMRP, telescopic joint, mux cables, and subsea pods.

recommendations: ["BOE to recommend well securing, riser displacement, disconnection, and relocation to safe zone for storms projected to reach Category 1 Hurricane sustained winds or greater", "Modify rig contract requirements to mandate riser, BOP, and tensioner system analysis for Category 1 Hurricane conditions between June 1 and December 1", "BOE to compare projected versus actual weather conditions with weather service provider to eliminate conflicting information", "Transocean to revise Extreme Weather Evacuation Plan (EWEP) to ensure compliance with operational limits", "Transocean to request bathymetry maps to identify hazards in safe zone transit routes", "BOE to modify Hurricane Evacuation Plan to include risk assessment and barrier evaluation considering time constraints"]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Uncontrolled descent of man rider during personnel lift operation
Incident Type: Personnel handling incident - man rider malfunction
Operating Phase: workover
Materials: hydraulic fluid
Summary: On February 17, 2025, an Expro Americas Inc. employee (AP) was lifted approximately 100 feet on the forward man rider to tighten a leaking hydraulic hose on the Coil Tubing Lift Frame (CTLF) aboard the Noble Globetrotter drillship. After completing the task and signaling for descent, the man rider began descending at an accelerated rate. The hoist operator attempted to control the descent by moving the control to hoist position and attempting to activate the emergency stop, but AP reached the rig floor before intervention could be effective. Investigation revealed that the Original Equipment Manufacturer's Programming and Control Logic (PCL) had been altered from "Default" to "Warning" settings, allowing system faults to be overridden and preventing brake engagement. Contributing factors included lack of preventive maintenance, environmental degradation of equipment, inadequate pre-use inspections, and administrative deficiencies in the Control of Work permit.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped Load - Steel Plate Striking Personnel
Incident Type: Lifting Equipment Failure / Struck-By Incident
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: steel plate, pipe collars
Summary: On December 25, 2024, a 340 lbs steel plate disengaged from a magnetic heavy lifting device on the Noble Drillship Blackhornet while being lowered to the deck. The plate fell 47 inches and struck both a Noble welder (IP1) and a Noble roustabout (IP2) on the top of the left foot. Both personnel sustained swelling and contusions and were evacuated to University Hospital. Investigation determined the magnetic heavy lifting device was defective with damaged pole shoes causing air gaps that reduced lifting capacity. Contributing factors included inadequate job safety analysis, lack of timeout for safety (TOFS), failure to review hazards, and work environment constraints that created time pressure.

Top Event: Unsecured pipe section rolled off storage rack and struck personnel
Incident Type: Lifting incident with personnel injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Hawse piping
Summary: On 08-MAY-2025 at 0430 hours, two Transocean employees (2nd Engineer and 3rd Engineer/IP) were transferring nine sections of Hawse piping from deck to a three-tier storage rack using chain falls on the Deepwater Invictus drillship. After placing the eighth pipe section on the top tier, the 2E left to retrieve a U-bolt for securing the pipe while the IP knelt to rig the next section. The unsecured eighth pipe section rolled off the rack, fell approximately 3 feet, and struck the IP's right middle finger, resulting in amputation of the fingertip at the DIP joint. The IP was evacuated by medivac helicopter and treated at University Hospital. Root causes identified include flawed rack design, inadequate hazard analysis in the HIPC, lack of written job procedure, and failure to secure pipe before removing chain falls.

Root causes identified include flawed rack design, inadequate hazard analysis in the HIPC, lack of written job procedure, and failure to secure pipe before removing chain falls.
recommendations: ["BSEE Office of Safety Management to evaluate the Hazard ID Prompt (HIPC) card used by Transocean", "Install additional restraints on storage rack such as chalks, bumpers, or grooves", "Increase angle of rack supports", "Implement written job procedure for pipe storage task", "Enhance hazard identification to specifically identify unsecured pipe rolling hazard and crush points", "Establish requirement to secure piping with U-bolts before removing chain falls"]

Top Event: BOP/LMRP broke away from riser
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, seawater, calcium chloride
Summary: On January 14, 2019, during temporary abandonment operations on well SS002 aboard the Seadrill Sevan Louisiana, the riser adapter failed and allowed the BOP stack to fall to the seafloor. The incident occurred when the Subsea Supervisor reduced riser tension to 440 KIPS instead of the agreed 650 KIPS while unlatching the wellhead connector. This reduced tension caused the BOP stack to tilt approximately 45 degrees, placing excessive bending loads on the riser adapter. The riser adapter partially ruptured immediately and fully failed 17 hours later, causing the BOP stack to fall. The well was already secured with multiple plugs and cement, preventing pollution.

Top Event: Loss of load control - riser joint dropped
Incident Type: Structural damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: riser joint, drilling fluid
Summary: On January 9, 2019 at 0220 hours, a 35,000 pound slick riser joint fell across the rig floor of the drillship Noble Don Taylor while being hoisted using a hydraulic riser running tool (RRT) suspended from the top drive. The RRT was not properly seated in the riser and the secondary manual lock was not engaged. A crew member narrowly avoided being struck. The riser joint crushed the drops shed. No injuries were reported. Estimated damage was $184,000.

Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: At approximately 2040 hours on 06-SEP-2018, a high gas alarm on the main deck gas compression skid tripped on the Stampede floating production platform (OCS-G 26313, Green Canyon 468-A). Investigation revealed the gas release was caused by four loose fastening bolts on the sight glass of the Flash Gas Compressor 2-A Gas Filter Coalescer (MAJ-1950A). The loose bolts allowed the gasket to cut out under normal operating pressure (~300 psi), resulting in gas release and platform emergency shutdown. No injuries were reported.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unintended Emergency Disconnect Sequence Activation
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, drilling fluid
Summary: On September 11, 2016, at 0826 hours, the Senior Dynamic Positioning Officer (SDPO) aboard the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet mistakenly activated the Emergency Disconnect Sequence (EDS) while the Blow Out Preventer (BOP) stack was suspended approximately 1,550 feet below the water line during a planned pull to surface. The EDS activation caused the BOP stack to fall approximately 2,330 feet to the sea floor. The incident resulted from human error triggered by confusion between similar acronyms (EDS vs ESD), similar personnel names, lack of pre-job planning, and informal EDS testing practices. The BOP stack landed safely in the designated safe zone approximately 484 feet from the wellhead with no damage to the rig, no personnel injury, and no environmental impact.

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: fuel gas
Summary: On March 21, 2017, at approximately 10:00 a.m., a fire occurred on the Green Canyon 680-A Constitution spar platform. The Flash Gas Compressor No.1 shut in on a Level Safety High High alarm on the 1st Stage Scrubber. During restart attempts, the operator discovered a fire between the engine block and starter. The fire was caused by fuel gas escaping from a cracked starter signal return line, which ignited upon contact with a spark possibly caused by the Bendix on the starter engaging the fly-wheel. The operator shut in the platform, activated the general alarm, and extinguished the fire with a fire hose. No reportable injuries occurred. The damaged signal line, starter, flex piping, and associated hoses/piping were replaced.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Buoyancy - Well A-1 Dry Tree and Riser Descent
Incident Type: Structural Damage / Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, nitrogen, seawater
Summary: On December 27, 2017, the Well A-1 riser on the Constitution Spar (GC 680-A) dropped 3-4 feet onto the workover deck grating. On December 31, 2017, during air-up operations on Buoyancy Can Chamber #2, an air leak was detected. The Well A-1 dry tree subsequently descended 28 feet onto the spar deck. Root cause investigation revealed severe corrosion of the air fill-up lines in Chambers #2 and #3, allowing nitrogen to escape and seawater to enter the chambers. Loss of buoyancy in these chambers caused the weight of the dry tree and riser to exceed the buoyancy capacity of the remaining chambers, resulting in structural collapse of the workover deck grating and descent of the wellhead assembly. Estimated damage: $4,700,000.

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: engine coolant, natural gas
Summary: On April 29, 2017, at approximately 1:12 a.m., a fire occurred on the Green Canyon 680-A Constitution spar platform in the Gulf of Mexico. Vibration from the Flash Gas Compressor No. 2 engine caused stainless steel coolant tubing to rub against a retaining bolt head, creating a hole. Coolant sprayed onto the engine exhaust piping, which was covered with protective heat blankets. Once the blankets became saturated with coolant, the hot exhaust ignited the coolant, causing a fire. Operators responded immediately, shut in the platform, activated the general alarm, and extinguished the fire with a handheld portable fire extinguisher. No reportable injuries occurred.

Top Event: Crush injury to left hand
Incident Type: Structural Damage / Crane Lifting Device Incident
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: production tubing, piping
Summary: On 29-OCT-2015 at 1740 hours, the Deck Coordinator sustained severe crush injuries to his left hand while attempting to relocate two joints of damaged production tubing on the Diamond Ocean Blackhornet. The tubing was being set on an uneven bundle of 6 5/8 inch piping in the pipe bay area. Despite warnings from two Roustabouts that the surface was not level and the tubing would roll, the Deck Coordinator continued the operation. When the tubing began to roll, the Deck Coordinator positioned himself between the tubing and a cargo box and attempted to wedge angle iron under the tubing. The weight of the tubing caused the angle iron to flip, crushing his left hand. The tubing continued to roll, pinning him between the cargo box and tubing until the crane lifted the tubing to free him.

Top Event: High pressure gas release
Incident Type: Gas Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: sales gas, natural gas
Summary: On May 8, 2018 at approximately 2115 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred on sales gas meter #3 at the Atlantis platform in Green Canyon Block 787-A. The release was discovered by third party personnel and lasted approximately 30 minutes. The root cause was the installation of incorrect Teflon seals instead of required Viton O-rings (Green Tweed 938 elastomer) during a transmitter replacement 18 days prior. The seal failed at approximately 2100 psi. This was the fourth gas release incident on BP facilities since 2008 and the third on Atlantis, all due to failed Teflon seals. Contributing factors included lack of knowledge by the technician, poor oversight, inadequate training, bypassed gas detectors, insufficient detector placement, and deficient emergency response procedures.

Top Event: Flash fire in waste oil incinerator tank
Incident Type: fire
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: waste oil
Summary: On December 10, 2016, at 05:40 a.m., a flash fire occurred inside the waste oil incinerator tank on the Pacific Drilling Sharav drillship operating for Chevron in Green Canyon block 807. The fire was caused by failure of the waste oil level switch to shut down power to the internal heating element when the waste oil level became too low. The exposed heating element ignited fumes inside the tank, causing a flash fire that distorted and ballooned the tank. One isolation valve was damaged and approximately 1 gallon of waste oil was released to secondary containment. No injuries or environmental threat occurred.

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: seawater, lube oil, cooling water
Summary: On December 3, 2017 at 2215 hours, a fire occurred at the Fieldwood Energy LLC Grand Isle 116-A platform. During normal operations, operators observed a flame coming from the #2 seawater lift pump electric motor. The fire was caused by a bearing failure in the pump motor that resulted in excessive heat buildup, igniting lube oil in the motor housing. The fire was contained using a purple K fire extinguisher and the platform fire water system. Root cause was identified as inadequate preventive maintenance and monitoring of the seawater pump motors, combined with a lube oil leak that caused the reservoir level to drop.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Leak from Tubing Connection
Incident Type: Pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 16 July 2019 at 0700 hours, approximately 4.8 barrels of oil leaked from a 3/4" broken tubing connection (Swagelok fitting) on the gas lift bleed ring of the casing valve at Well R-22 on the unmanned GI 22-R platform. The leak was discovered when an operator on nearby GI 22-L observed a sheen north of the platform. The platform had been shut-in since 28 June 2019 for maintenance, but pressure remained on the gas lift line and well casing. High seas from Hurricane Barry (14-16 ft with occasional 21 ft seas) caused well movement that sheared the tubing. Contributing factors included inadequate tubing support, improperly functioning check valves in gas lift mandrels, incorrect installation of a Flow Safety Valve upstream rather than downstream of the tubing connection, and cleanup procedures that exacerbated pollution to a wildlife refuge.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment
Incident Type: Unauthorized discharge of pollutants into offshore waters
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, produced water
Summary: On January 26, 2018, at the GI 43 AP Platform in the Gulf of Mexico, a loss of containment of hydrocarbons and gas releases occurred. Control Room Operators experienced abnormal operating conditions due to sand and sludge buildup and chemical flocking affecting level sensors. Safety devices were placed in bypass to prevent equipment shutdown while operators flushed level bridles. A hole was discovered in the Gas Vent Header Piping at 1:18 am leaking hydrocarbons. The leak was temporarily sealed with duct tape and absorbent pad. Due to communication failures and personnel falling asleep, the platform continued flowing until 6:45 am when a sheen was observed and a field-wide shut-in was initiated. The incident resulted in a 10-mile sheen and discharge from 5 locations on the platform. Root causes included sludge buildup, collapsed internal packing, failed sump pumps, safety device bypass, and failure to use Stop Work Authority.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pipeline Leak
Incident Type: Pollution Event
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hydrocarbons
Summary: On 25 August 2018 at 0730 hours, a 3/8-inch hole developed in a 12-inch departing pipeline (KAH-101, segment #8217) at Fieldwood Energy's Grand Isle 76-A platform, discharging approximately 8.35 barrels (352.27 gallons) of hydrocarbons into the Gulf of Mexico. The Pressure Safety Low sensor detected the leak and shut in the platform. Investigation revealed the pipeline had been converted from gas sales/condensate service to bulk oil service without BSEE approval. Contributing factors included improper Pressure Safety High (PSH) set point at 1200 psi (too close to MAOP of 1203 psi) and improper Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) set point at 1323 psi, both failing to provide adequate overpressure protection. The pipeline pumps' Maximum Discharge Pressure of 1800 psi exceeded the pipeline's MAOP, creating overpressure risk.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pressurized Gas Release
Incident Type: Struck by Object / Pressure Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 25 April 2025 at approximately 1330 hours, a Louisiana Safety Systems, Inc. Valve Technician was performing maintenance on a control valve located on a 6-inch gas line at GI 47 AP platform. While disassembling the valve, a sudden pressurized gas release occurred, causing the valve bonnet flange assembly (approximately 50 pounds) to strike the technician in the left chest and abdomen area. The technician was knocked backwards onto an elevated scaffolding platform and possibly lost consciousness. The technician sustained a laceration behind the ear and an anterior rib fracture of the 8th left rib. The incident resulted from multiple failures including inadequate communication between the operator and contractor, failure to obtain required work permits and PIC approval, failure to verify zero pressure on the gas line through proper energy isolation procedures, use of unapproved scaffolding without fall protection, and non-adherence to manufacturer recommendations for valve disassembly.

Top Event: Dropped load - drill pipe and elevators falling from v-door to catwalk
Incident Type: Structural damage; required evacuation; lost time accident
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drill pipe
Summary: During drill pipe lay-down operations on the Hercules 264 rig, a 3.5 inch drill pipe joint and single joint elevators fell from the v-door to the catwalk, striking two rig personnel (Floor Man and Roustabout) on the left shoulder/arm. The failure was caused by an improper-sized cotter pin on a 4-point shackle that came loose. Root causes included failure to properly rig up elevators, use correct cotter pin size, develop a rigging checklist, conduct pre-job rigging inspection, and incorporate crane crew in the Job Safety Analysis.

Operating Phase: production

Summary: On 22-Jan-2015 at approximately 1454 hours, the United States flagged Motor Vessel MAERSK KENTUCKY (container ship) collided with Energy XXI's High Island 154 'B' platform. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Flash fire at crane engine air intake
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, diesel fuel
Summary: On 11-April-2018 at 17:15 hours, a flash fire occurred at the crane engine air intake blower during fuel system bleed-down operations at platform HI 22 A. Two Shamrock Energy Solution Mechanics were performing crane engine overhaul. After exhausting stored air pressure, facility Operators opened a valve sending natural gas to the fuel gas system to supply additional pressure for the starter. On the third cranking of the starter, a flash fire occurred at the engine air intake blower. One Mechanic sustained 1st and 2nd degree burns to hands, arms, face, and ears. The root cause was failure to vent the crane engine starter discharge to a safe area, combined with lack of communication that the starting system utilized natural gas instead of compressed air, and absence of a Management of Change procedure when the facility's air system was modified to a gas system.

Top Event: Battery explosion with chemical burn injury
Incident Type: Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel fuel, hydrogen gas, battery acid
Summary: On 06-AUG-2014 at 0200 hours, the main platform generator at Energy XXI's High Island A368 'A' platform shut down due to severed belts. Two contract operators attempted to start the backup diesel generator. When the 'A' Operator removed the cover from the second battery box to check terminal connections, sparks from a loose battery terminal ignited accumulated hydrogen gas in the battery box, causing an explosion. Battery acid was blown into the operator's face and eyes. The operator was evacuated by helicopter and treated for chemical burns.

Top Event: Fire in #2 Diesel Generator
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel, electrical winding
Summary: On 20 April 2015 at approximately 1230 hours, a minor fire occurred at W&T Offshore's HI A379 'B' facility in the Gulf of Mexico. Production operators noticed platform lights flickering and discovered smoke and flames emanating from the electrical winding end of the #2 diesel generator. The generator was immediately shut down and extinguished with dry chemical. Investigation determined the fire was caused by arc flash in the electrical winding due to corrosion and/or moisture contamination, resulting in $50,000 in damage. No injuries or pollution events occurred.

Top Event: Generator Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel fuel, dry chemical fire suppressant, water
Summary: On 3-Jan-2014 at 05:15, a fire was detected in the exhaust piping of a rental 1000 KW generator located on the drill deck. The Contract Night Operator spotted flames, announced the fire, and pulled the engine ESD, which shut down the engine and shut in the platform. A second operator used dry chemical extinguishers and water to suppress the fire. Root cause was determined to be a collapsed/clogged right bank air intake filter that caused incomplete fuel combustion in the cylinders, leading to fuel burning in the exhaust and overheating of the turbo. No injuries or pollution resulted.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of pipe string down wellbore
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: work string, 3-1/2 inch pipe, cement retainer
Summary: During Plug and Abandonment operations at High Island A 446 A on 4-DEC-2019 at 0704 hours, a crew member unscrewed the top joint of a 3-1/2 inch work string. When the crane operator lifted the unscrewed joint, it became caught on the last thread section, causing the bowls and slips to rise and unseat. Upon release, the remaining work string fell back down at an angle. The safety clamp broke loose during impact, allowing the bowls to open and the remaining work string (approximately 346 feet) to fall down the hole. The work string impacted the cement retainer 72 feet below mudline. Root cause was failure to verify complete separation of the joint before signaling the crane operator to lift. Contributing factors included failure to document blind lifts in the JSA, failure to secure bowls to the Pacman plate, failure to secure slip handles, crew distraction during the lift, and inadequate procedures addressing blind lifts.

Top Event: Personnel basket struck crane boom cradle and personnel fell from basket
Incident Type: Crane/Material-Handling Equipment Failure with Personnel Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On June 12, 2015 at 20:00 hours at platform HI A 537 B, during evacuation due to approaching tropical storm, a crane operator picked up a Billy Pugh personnel basket carrying four personnel. While swinging the basket over the helideck and lowering it toward a boat, the crane boom continued to swing northward despite the operator attempting to stop it with the swing brake. The crane was operating at 75 percent power instead of full throttle as designed. The operator raised the basket to place personnel back on deck, but the basket struck the crane boom cradle and became entangled. One person fell approximately 10-12 feet onto a Marine Portable Tank, landing on face and stomach and losing consciousness. A second person fell on the boom cradle sustaining minor injuries. Two remaining personnel stayed on basket and climbed down using boom cradle.

Top Event: Laceration from grinder wheel kickback
Incident Type: Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 06-Jun-2014, a contract employee of Infinity Valve working for McMoRan was tasked with swapping out a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) by removing mounting bolts. A grinder with a cutting wheel was used in a small work space with limited visibility instead of a reciprocating saw. The grinder was not discussed in the Before Job Safety Analysis (BJSA). The employee was not comfortable using the grinder but did not invoke Stop Work Authority to complete the job quicker. The cutting wheel became bound in the confined space, causing the grinder to kick back and shatter the wheel. The blade contacted the back of the employee's left hand below the ring finger, causing a 4-inch laceration. The employee received 30 stitches and is expected to make a full recovery.

Top Event: Worker struck by make-up tongs during tripping operations
Incident Type: LTA (>3 days)
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drill pipe
Summary: On May 10, 2015, during back-reaming while tripping out of the hole operations on the Hercules 300 jackup rig, a Floorhand was struck on his right arm by a set of make-up tongs which failed to remain secured to the drill pipe. The Floorhand was medically evacuated and later determined to have a radial fracture on his right arm. Root causes included inadequate Job Safety Analysis, lack of established safe standing zones, inefficient crew communication, absence of required supervision, and equipment failure of the Top Drive grabber dies.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Backfire/Explosion
Incident Type: Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: fuel gas, crude oil
Summary: On 16 March 2015 at approximately 14:48 hours, a backfire/explosion occurred at Chevron USA's High Island A582 'C' facility during platform startup after monthly Emergency Shut Down testing. Production Operators were bringing the platform back on line when the purge timer on Ambitrol Heater EAL-1510 was observed not counting down. Investigation revealed an inadequate purge of air within the Ambitrol Heater Cabin before ignition, allowing an explosive fuel mixture to accumulate. The air/fuel ratio valve linkage was initially disconnected, and the Programmable Logic Controller allowed excess fuel introduction during ignition. The unit backfired and dislodged the heater cover. No personnel injury, environmental incident, or fire resulted.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Consciousness in Confined Space
Incident Type: Confined Space Entry - Near Fatality
Operating Phase: production
Materials: heater treater fluids, sludge, hydrogen sulfide, hydrocarbons
Summary: On September 4, 2023, at approximately 09:15 hours, an injured person (IP) entered the NBK-1070 Heater Treater vessel on platform HIA 379 B without a Supplied Air Respirator (SAR), wearing only an Air Purifying Respirator (APR). The IP was overcome by toxic fumes and passed out face down in the vessel fluids and sludge. A pump operator noticed the IP missing and alerted the cleaning crew supervisor. The supervisor entered without a respirator, was also overcome, and had to exit. A construction supervisor then entered without respiratory protection, extracted the IP, and CPR was administered for approximately 20 minutes before the IP began breathing. The IP was evacuated by helicopter and treated in intensive care for three days. The incident resulted from failure to follow the confined space entry plan, including no tri-gas testing, no hole watch, improper PPE, and no rescue equipment setup.

summary: At 0955 hours on 2 October 2019, a Pipeline Technician sustained a bicep tendon tear while manually lifting a one-inch high pressure hose from a suspended 4'x4'x3' metal basket on the High Island A 474 A facility. The basket was suspended by crane approximately two to three feet off the deck and positioned half on another basket and half over the deck. Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, wet gas
Summary: On May 26, 2022, BSEE inspectors observed a sheen approximately 30 yards by 200 yards in Gulf of Mexico waters originating from Cox Operating's High Island A 582 C facility. Investigation determined hydrocarbons were discharging from the incoming KAQ 2700 high pressure 8-inch wet gas pipeline segment number 13380 at the plus 10 deck level. The source was identified as a one-quarter inch diameter hole in the pipe due to corrosion. An estimated total of 129.145 gallons of oil was discharged during the bleed down process. Top Event: Structural failure of Billy Pugh Derrick Basket during personnel lift
Incident Type: Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 14-OCT-2014 at 01:05 hours, during preventive maintenance on the Aft Pipe Racker System, a Short Service Employee was hoisted in a Billy Pugh Derrick Basket to a height of approximately 110 feet. The basket hung up on an unknown part of the derrick/PRS causing it to tilt. Despite radio communication of an 'all stop' command, the basket continued to climb 3-4 inches before stopping. A loud 'pop' was heard and the rider discovered major damage to the single main support arm and two lower arm braces had separated. The rider used the anchor line for support and called for emergency descent. When the basket reached the rig floor, the hanging arm completely separated from the basket.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Casing Shoe Failure
Incident Type: Pollution - Unauthorized discharge of drilling mud
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: Synthetic Based Drilling Mud (SBM), Nitrogen foamed cement, Seawater
Summary: On 1 April 2015, the SeaDrill West Vela drill ship under contract to BP reported a dark area identified by ROV approximately 400-500 feet from the wellhead at Well 001 (Tiber #3) in Keathley Canyon Block 147. Samples from the seafloor showed gelatinous material resembling filter cake with 2% oil content, consistent with Synthetic Based Drilling Mud used during the 16.5 inch hole section drilling. The incident resulted from a 22 inch casing shoe failure at 8630 ft md/tvd set in a salt formation. From 18-22 January 2015, approximately 500 barrels of 14.4 ppg synthetic oil base mud were discharged into offshore waters during 14 inch intermediate casing running and cementing operations. Total mud losses during casing operations reached approximately 4802 barrels.

Top Event: Dropped drill pipe from aft pipe transfer conveyor
Incident Type: Falling object / Structural damage
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drill pipe
Summary: At approximately 08:10 hours on 6 January 2016, a joint of drill pipe fell from the aft pipe transfer conveyor onto the aft pipe deck of the Transocean Discoverer India drill ship during drilling operations for Chevron U.S.A. in Keathley Canyon Block 770. The drill pipe became trapped under a utility cart during loading arm operations, causing it to roll and fall over handrails, creating a 5-inch diameter hole in the aft deck. No personnel injuries occurred due to area barricading. The incident resulted from failure to follow work risk assessment procedures, inadequate safety time-outs, and lack of hazard assessment for utility cart positioning.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Base Mud Spill
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: synthetic base mud, synthetic oil
Summary: At 20:20 hours on 30 August 2014, the Transocean Discoverer Clear Leader drill ship under contract to Chevron was conducting drilling operations at Keathley Canyon Block 829 when a loss of synthetic base mud was observed from a telescopic slip joint packer failure. Approximately 12 barrels of 14.5 ppg SBM (6.12 gallons synthetic oil) was spilled into the Gulf of Mexico but was restricted to the moon pool area. Root cause was a leaking stainless steel JIC connection for the air hose at the bottom of the J-plate, with contributing factors including improper hose support, ocean currents causing vibration, and submersion of hose bundle below water line.

Top Event: Eye injury from ejected indicator rod
Incident Type: Personnel injury - eye trauma
Operating Phase: production
Materials: subsea blowout preventer equipment, hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 7 August 2019 at 04:30 hours, a Seadrill subsea technician sustained an eye injury while working on the hands-free gooseneck (HFGN) during well operations. While attempting to retract the kill line HFGN locking pins, an indicator rod was ejected by trapped air and water pressure from within the operator housing, striking the technician in the right eye. The technician was diagnosed with orbital fractures and bruising. The incident resulted from operation of out-of-compliance equipment with known defects, failure to follow OEM guidelines, creation of unsafe workaround procedures, and failure to account for trapped hydrostatic pressure.

Top Event: Electrical fire in drilling switchboard room
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: electrical equipment, wellbore fluid
Summary: On 28 May 2019 at 21:00 hours, smoke detectors activated in the Starboard 690V Drilling Switchboard Room aboard the Diamond Offshore Ocean Blackhornet drillship. An electrical fire occurred in the Aux Drawworks B (ADWB) motor drive cabinet, causing severe damage to two Inverter Modules and two Variable Frequency Drives. The general alarm was activated and all personnel reported to muster stations. No injuries were reported. The assessed damage was approximately $180,000. The root cause could not be definitively determined, but possible causes included a faulty Inverter Module, cooling water leakage, or improperly torqued DC fuses.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: An explosion occurred on 14 February 2019 at 0115 on the Mississippi Canyon 109 A (Amberjack) Platform. The explosion originated from a 12 Volt battery located inside a Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) Panel used for gas sales measurement and communication. A rechargeable battery had been overcharged due to improper replacement and installation in a non-vented enclosure, causing hydrogen gas accumulation and ignition of electrical components. The panel door was blown off and debris landed 45 feet away. No injuries or pollution occurred. The root cause was flawed equipment design and installation of non-rated electrical components in a hazardous area, compounded by inadequate personnel training on battery hydrogen venting and lack of hazard analysis during design changes.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Overboard Discharge of Synthetic Based Mud
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Based Mud (SBM), seawater
Summary: On 02 July 2015 at 0400 hours, the Rowan Relentless drill ship inadvertently discharged 368 barrels of Synthetic Based Mud (SBM) containing 67 percent synthetic oil into Gulf of Mexico waters. The discharge occurred when the Derrick Man, without verifying valve alignment, pumped displacement seawater overboard from pits #12 and #14. A manually operated splitter valve separating reserve pit #1 from these pits was left halfway open, allowing SBM to flow into the overboard discharge line. Root causes included poor communication between parties, failure to verify valve alignment prior to pumping, and lack of awareness of company policy regarding sensitive valve lockout procedures.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Overboard Discharge of Drilling Fluid
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM)
Summary: At approximately 0055 hrs on 1 April 2019, LLOG Exploration Offshore LLC experienced a spill of 61 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) on the Seadrill West Neptune Drillship during drilling operations at Mississippi Canyon Block 199. The spill resulted from a misalignment of trip tank valve positions. While the Derrickman was transferring mud between trip tanks per driller request, the master dump valve for the Trip Tanks was found to be locked in the open position, allowing 61 bbls of SBM to flow into the overboard discharge line into the Gulf of Mexico. The pit cleaning crew had possibly locked open the master dump valve during cleaning operations and never returned it to its normally closed and locked position. Total pollution was 37.8 bbls (61 bbls at 62% synthetic make-up).

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Communication and Catastrophic Failure of Satellite Umbilical Cobra Head Termination
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 12 November 2019 at 1200 hours, a loss of communication to MC 208/209 SS001 well occurred at Murphy Exploration & Production Company's MC 254 A Delta House platform. This resulted in catastrophic failure of the satellite umbilical cobra head termination with rupture of one HP and two LP hydraulic supply lines. The root cause was heavy weight and center of gravity offset from the UTH assembly resulting in high shear and bending moment on the MQC plate beyond its reported capacity, with collapse of bolts on the MQC bracket. Contributing factors included temporary buoyancy modules left on cobra heads that were not designed for long-term subsea use, and EFL grounding to the UTH. Approximately 1,000 gallons of hydraulic fluid was released, below EPA reportable threshold. The fail-safe shutdown sequence successfully shut in associated wells.

Top Event: Crane boom structural damage
Incident Type: Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 9-JAN-2015 at approximately 0600 hours, during a routine lift operation on the NABORS MODS 200 crane at Platform B SIMBA in MC Block 21, the crane boom struck the boom pad causing structural damage to the boom heel. Investigation revealed that the high angle kick-out (boom hoist high limiter) safety device was adjusted all the way in, rendering it non-operational. This device is designed to prevent the crane boom from striking the boom pad. Both day and night crane operators had initialed Daily/Pre-Use Crane Inspection forms from 7-JAN-15 through 13-JAN-15 verifying the high angle boom kick-out was checked for proper operation, despite the device being inoperable.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Mud Discharge Overboard
Incident Type: Pollution - Overboard Discharge
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Base Mud (SBM), 13.5 ppg drilling fluid
Summary: On 12 FEB 2014 between 1630 and 1959 hours, air pressure was lost to the primary Telescopic Joint (TJ) Packer resulting in a 128 barrel discharge of 13.5 ppg Synthetic Base Mud into offshore waters during 14" casing running operations. The air supply line to the TJ packer had ruptured. The air pressure alarm for the TJ packer was disabled with alarm set points at 0-psi for both high and low limits. No automatic redundant back-up system was in place; the back-up hydraulic system required manual engagement.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Partial Loss of Power
Incident Type: Power Loss
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 6 May 2019 at 0748 hours, Shell Offshore Inc. MC 437-A (Appomattox) platform experienced a partial loss of primary generator power during a fuel swap operation from diesel to natural gas. The Uninterrupted Power Supply (UPS-3451) failed to provide backup power due to shorted capacitors and undersized fuses on the inverter capacitor bank. This caused shutdown of the fire and gas processor, preventing emergency generator startup. The UPS was bypassed and power restored. A second power loss occurred at 2147 hours but the emergency generator restored power within 30 seconds with the UPS in bypass. Root cause was identified as turbine generator trip combined with UPS-3451 failure. No injuries or pollution resulted. The facility had not commenced first oil production at the time of the incident.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in turbine generator enclosure
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel fuel, natural gas
Summary: On 30 November 2021 at 1917 hours, a fire occurred in the ZAN-700 turbine generator enclosure at the Appomattox semi-submersible production platform. Flame detectors alerted the control room, and a deck operator confirmed smoke and sparks from the generator housing. The generator ESD was activated, platform ESD initiated, and full muster sounded. Fire teams extinguished the fire with water. Investigation determined that vibration caused loss of fastener preload in the exhaust hatch bolts, allowing hot exhaust to escape and ignite Visqueen (plastic tarp) left by painters, which then melted cables. No injuries, explosions, or environmental harm occurred.

Top Event: Unplanned gas release
Incident Type: Gas release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 15 July 2020 at 1411 hours, the Appomattox platform (MC 437 A) experienced an unplanned gas release while attempting to start up Bulk Gas Compressor #1. Multiple gas detectors alarmed at 2:19 pm, triggering an automatic Process Shut Down. A deck operator detected the odor of gas and the control room operator manually activated the Emergency Shut Down and Muster Alarm. Investigation revealed that level bridle drain valves on the BGC #1 Gas Scrubber had been left open. Approximately 1838 kg of gas was released over a 25-minute period. No injuries or damages occurred. The facility safety system responded in accordance with applicable regulations.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unplanned mooring chain payout
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: mooring chain, hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 29 January 2020 at 1400 hours, a Roller Ram Winch (RRW) mooring chain incident occurred at Shell Offshore Inc's Appomattox platform in Mississippi Canyon Block 437. During maintenance activities on mooring line L-11, the chain links suddenly began paying out from the chain locker. The guide roller broke free from its bearing cradle, and 22 chain links paid out outboard. The chain stopper cylinders, angle transmitter, and Slack Chain Hang-off Unit (SCHU) stopper were forced out of frame. The root cause was identified as human performance error: the maintenance crew assumed the mooring chain was in lock position without visually confirming it. Contributing factors included flawed equipment design lacking proximity switches and programmed interlocks to confirm SCHU stopper extension. Total repair and recovery costs were $5,750,000.

The guide roller broke free from its bearing cradle, and 22 chain links paid out outboard. The chain stopper cylinders, angle transmitter, and Slack Chain Hang-off Unit (SCHU) stopper were forced out of frame. The root cause was identified as human performance error: the maintenance crew assumed the mooring chain was in lock position without visually confirming it. Contributing factors included flawed equipment design lacking proximity switches and programmed interlocks to confirm SCHU stopper extension. Total repair and recovery costs were $5,750,000.
recommendations: ["BSEE to provide training on mooring for large platforms", "Update operating manual to identify how and when load is transferred to SCHU", "Strengthen and clarify roles and responsibilities in maintenance procedures", "Install additional proximity switches and programmed interlocks to confirm SCHU stoppers are fully extended", "Permanently reinstate gripper used during installation/commissioning as secondary barrier to prevent unplanned chain payout"]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Chemical exposure to personnel
Incident Type: Lost Time Accident (LTA > 3 days)
Operating Phase: production
Materials: asphaltene inhibitor
Summary: On 17 April 2021 at 1415 hours, a stack operator was contacted with asphaltene inhibitor on the arms and face while attempting to open chemical injection valves to Well AC-006 on the Appomattox platform. The incident was caused by a loose tubing fitting between an isolation valve and flow element FE 4220-H1, which resulted in chemical spray from the weep hole. The operator was wearing inadequate PPE (no chemical resistant gloves or face shield). The injured person was initially released to full duty by the medic but experienced eye irritation the next day and was evacuated. An onshore physician diagnosed chemical conjunctivitis and ordered 7 days off work, resulting in an LTA > 3 days classification.

Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: methanol, crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On 27 June 2020 at 1725 hours, a subsea methanol leak was observed by an ROV operator on the Mississippi Canyon Block 503, Lease OCS-G 27277 SS002 subsea well (E1). The leak originated from the Methanol Injection Tree Valve (MITV) due to internal seal failure. Approximately 107 gallons of methanol was lost during diagnostic testing. LLOG mobilized repair operations, and on 2 December 2020, after completing repairs, a blind flange was installed to isolate the leaking line and the well was returned to service.

Top Event: Dropped Object
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane Other Lifting
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid, riser
Summary: On October 31, 2020 at approximately 17:58 hours, a spider dog safety door (grating) fell through the rotary table to the top of the Blow Out Preventers (BOP) on the SeaDrill West Neptune drillship at Mississippi Canyon 503, Well #007, injuring two floor hands. The safety door was dislodged when spider dogs were functioned to the open position while back plates remained in the down position, causing bolts securing the grating to shear. The inadequate secondary retention chain failed to prevent the grating from falling into the well center. One injured person sustained facial lacerations and the other a fractured rib.

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On 14 July 2019 at 21:50, a fire occurred on LLOG's MC 547 A platform when a 1/2" isolation valve on Pipeline Pump A's discharge piping developed a crack. Hydrocarbons sprayed onto the hot turbo exhaust system and ignited. The TSE activated the ESD system, all production shut in, and firewater pumps activated. A 3-man fire team extinguished the fire in 16 minutes. No injuries occurred, but pollution was detected on deck and structural supports. The root cause was improper installation of the isolation valve without adequate support, causing fatigue damage from high vibration service.

Top Event: Hand laceration and finger fracture from pinch point during pump installation
Incident Type: Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 13 September 2019 at 1530 hours, an injured person sustained a laceration and fracture to the left index finger while assisting with installation of a flow assurance pump on Deck 7 of the Gulfstar 1 platform. The injury occurred when an air-operated chain hoist jerked upward while the IP was using an alignment tool to position piping flanges, causing the IP's hand to be pinched between the piping and alignment bar. The incident was attributed to inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis, congested workspace, poor hand placement, and the jerking motion of the air hoist.

Top Event: Unauthorized discharge of synthetic oil based mud overboard
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM), salt water, seawater
Summary: On 13 February 2015 at 1115 hours, a 260 barrel discharge of 15 ppg Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM) occurred at MC 762 on the Stena Forth. The Derrickman intended to transfer salt water from active pit #2 to active pit #4 but was unaware that SOBM from equalized active pits #1 and #3 had flowed into active pit #2 due to failure of the air actuator for active pit #2 and the suction valve for active pit #3. Poor communication between the Derrickman and Mud Loggers, combined with equipment failure and lack of continuous pit monitoring, resulted in the unauthorized discharge.
