incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Well Control Incident - Unintentional Shearing of Fluid Loss Control Devices
Incident Type: Underground blowout
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: completion fluid, packer fluid, crude oil, calcium carbonate
Summary: On January 9, 2021, at approximately 06:00 hours, Kosmos Energy experienced a well control incident on Seadrill West Neptune during completion operations on the SS003 well. While displacing wellbore completion fluid to packer fluid, the downhole Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD) unintentionally sheared the upper and lower Fluid Loss Control Devices (FLCD), resulting in cross flow between completed intervals and fluid losses of 600 barrels per hour. The well was subsequently shut in and killed over a multi-day operation. The incident was attributed to inadequate risk recognition, poor communication, insufficient execution and supervision, and incomplete procedures.

Top Event: Structural damage to high pressure gas lift piping during mezzanine deck installation
Incident Type: External damage - mechanical lifting
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, high pressure gas lift fluid
Summary: On 6 March 2015 at 1615 hrs, during installation of the West Gas Lift Mezzanine Deck (6,500 pounds) on Murphy's MC 736 A Platform, the load shifted and jammed between high pressure (1300 psi) gas lift piping and a 4" isolation valve. The incident resulted from failure to follow engineering design specifications for lifting equipment placement, non-use of hold back hoist and tag lines, and non-installation of stabbing guides. Attempts to unjam the load caused the gas lift piping to bow and create a 5/16" gouge in the isolation valve body. No injuries or hydrocarbon releases occurred. Property damage exceeded $50,000.

Top Event: Fire in Pressurized Welding Enclosure
Incident Type: fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 15 September 2015 at 0510 hours, a fire occurred inside Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) #39 at Murphy Exploration MC 736-A platform during hot work operations. Hot slag from torch welding rolled under the Velcro portion of the PWE wall, heated up and ignited. The fire was detected and extinguished by SafeZone personnel using a water fire extinguisher. Contributing factors included improper PWE installation with gaps, lack of readily available fire protection equipment, use of incorrect fire extinguisher type, failure to follow hot work procedures, and delayed communication to control room.

Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: N/A
Summary: During casing installation operations on the Noble Bully I drilling rig, an injured person (IP) placed his hand into a hinged section of a 1,250 ton Rotary Mounted Slips (RMS) component while attempting to stop its rotation. top_event: Tubing hanger handling tool separation and falling load strike
incident_type: Structural damage and personnel injury from dropped load
summary: On 7 July 2014 at 1940 hours during workover operations on the Seadrill West Vela platform in MC Block 776, a tubing hanger handling tool (THHT) separated from a tubing hanger at approximately 35 degrees angle while being transported. The hanger fell approximately 15 feet to the pipe skate, striking one employee and causing another to fall while avoiding the falling load. Two Seadrill employees were injured: one with lacerations to elbow and bruising; the second with two fractured ribs and lacerated spleen. The probable cause was either mechanical failure of the THHT or improper installation, though this remained undetermined at time of investigation.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge to Sea
Incident Type: Pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, workover fluids
Summary: On 13 July 2020 at 1055 hours, a pollution event occurred at BP's Thunder Horse platform (MC 778-A) in the Gulf of Mexico. While ramping up production following pigging and flowback operations, production operators observed a sheen covering 90% of a 100-yard area. BP's failure to adequately flush workover fluids from subsea well MC 822 TD003 S45 before placing it into production resulted in abnormal emulsion and foaming in process vessels. This caused an estimated 3 barrels of oil to be discharged into the Gulf of Mexico through the overboard produced water handling system. The incident was not reported to the National Response Center or BSEE until 23 July 2020, 10 days after occurrence, violating reporting requirements.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Gas Release
Incident Type: Gas Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On 2 August 2020 at 1925 hours, BP production operators were preparing to load a pigging device into the oil export pig launcher KAH-4190 at Thunder Horse platform in the Gulf of Mexico. When operators began cracking open the launcher door, a gas cloud was observed emanating from the top of the launcher. The operators immediately shut the door. The combustible gas detector ASH 962-102, located 3 feet above the launcher, activated and reached 88% of the Low Explosive Limits (LEL). The gas cloud dissipated to 0% LEL within 2 minutes. The incident was caused by oil residue in the pig trap generating a hazardous atmosphere after the launcher had been isolated for 5 days. The launcher was not purged with nitrogen immediately before opening the door, contrary to standard operating procedures. All safety systems responded as designed and no injuries occurred.

Top Event: Crush injury from falling metal sheets
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall - Crush Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: aluminum sheet, channel iron, metal sheets
Summary: On 22-JAN-2015 at 2235 hours, a Roustabout (Injured Person) sustained crush injuries to both feet when approximately 2,150-lbs of metal sheets shifted forward and pinned the worker. The incident occurred while the Crane Operator and Roustabouts were retrieving a 38-lb aluminum sheet from a storage rack. Channel iron supporting the metal sheets shifted, causing the load to slide forward. The injured person sustained at least one fracture to a bone in the left foot.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Produced Water Discharge
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, water clarifier chemical
Summary: A pollution event occurred on 4 May 2021 at Thunder Horse platform (MC 778 A) when approximately 5 barrels of hydrocarbons were released into the Gulf of Mexico from the produced water outfall. The incident was caused by operator error leaving the water clarifier injection quill to Float Cell #2 open, allowing 286 gallons of water clarifier to gravity drain into the vessel. This excess clarifier caused a lack of oil/water separation. The issue was prolonged because a faulty level control valve in Float Cell #1 prevented switching to the backup produced water train. The sheen was first detected via satellite imagery on 4 May 2021 and the discharge continued through 9 May 2021.

Top Event: Structural damage to Hydra-Racker and Top-Drive systems
Incident Type: Equipment failure due to human error
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drill pipe, drilling fluid
Summary: On August 7, 2014 at 0525 hours during pipe tripping operations, the Assistant Driller retracted the Hydra-Racker with elevators still closed and top drive extended. Without communicating with the Auxiliary Driller, the Assistant Driller lowered the top drive with drill pipe still connected to elevators and block. The top drive struck the drill pipe, causing it to bow and damage multiple top drive components. The drill pipe broke out of the Hydra-Racker heads, shearing mounting bolts and causing a 500 lb lower guide arm to fall 12 feet to the rig floor. The incident occurred in the last 30 minutes of the last shift prior to crew change when employees were not focused on the task.

Top Event: Overpressurization of Lower Annular Operating Chamber
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: pressurized fluid
Summary: On August 17, 2014, during surface pressure testing of a lower annular operating chamber on the Seadrill Sevan Louisiana rig, the test pump was left running unattended by the Subsea Engineer while he put away tools. The lower annular, rated for a maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP) of 3000 psi, was overpressurized to at least 10,000 psi, causing significant structural damage including sheared inner cylinder studs, blown out pusher plate, broken shuttle valve, and dislodged support ring and inner piston.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of marine riser and LMRP during hurricane evasion
Incident Type: structural damage, lost object, pollution event
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: crude oil, drilling fluid, lube oil, hydraulic oil
Summary: The Noble Globetrotter II Drillship was conducting Temporary Abandonment operations on well P012 in MC Block 809 when Hurricane Ida approached. Despite T-time calculations indicating insufficient time to safely secure the well and evacuate, the operator and contractor proceeded with operations. A crew change delayed the temporary abandonment operation by several hours. The drillship unlatched the LMRP and began pulling riser but encountered over-torqued bolts and hydraulic torque wrench breakdowns. At approximately 7:00 pm on August 28th, the Captain initiated Stop Work with 12 riser joints and LMRP still hanging from the rotary. The drillship attempted to evade the hurricane at speeds of 1-3.5 knots but was unsuccessful against Category 2+ hurricane force winds and high seas. The riser broke below the rotary, sending approximately 11 joints and the LMRP to the seafloor. The incident resulted in approximately 3,700 gallons of miscellaneous oil spilled, one loss-time injury, over $1 million in vessel and equipment damage, and 115 personnel exposed to extreme weather conditions.

Top Event: Hand caught between sheave and belt during pump maintenance
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall - Hand Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: methanol
Summary: On May 7, 2014 at 19:30, an AGI Mechanic contractor working on a Crosby Methanol Pump (PZZ-802) on the Shell Ursa TLP had his left hand caught between the sheave and belt while adjusting the motor with a ratchet and checking belt tension manually instead of using a tension tester. Top Event: Loss of Containment - Gas Release
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, methanol
Summary: On 9 December 2020 at approximately 1806 hours, a gas detector inside the Recycle Gas Compressor (RGC) enclosure detected a gas release from a valve flange on a 1-inch methanol injection line. A nut had backed off one of four bolts holding the valve flange, allowing gas to leak through the O-ring. The gas migrated into the RGC enclosure because the enclosure is not sealed and the flange was within 3 feet of the building. The alarm triggered an automatic shutdown of the RGC unit and platform. Emergency response crews successfully located, isolated, and secured the leak. No injuries, equipment damage, or pollution occurred.

Top Event: Pressure Relief Valve Failure and Projectile Impact Injury
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drilling mud, crude oil
Summary: On 4 July 2015 at 2120 hours, an Assistant Driller was struck in the left shoulder by the end cap of a pressure relief valve from mud pump #4 on the Noble Globetrotter Drillship. The incident occurred when the Driller failed to verify proper alignment of mud pump #4 to the boost line. The pump was lined up on the #1 standpipe instead, creating a closed system. When the pump was staged to 40 strokes per minute, pressure rapidly increased to 7,919 psi, exceeding the relief valve setting of 7,100 psi. The relief valve actuator housing failed, projecting an 8.5-inch diameter, 1-inch thick, 14-pound end cap that struck the injured person. Investigation revealed no scheduled maintenance had been performed on the pressure relief valves as recommended by the manufacturer.

Top Event: Hand Crush Injury
Incident Type: Serious Hand Injury - Amputation
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 6 June 2015 at 2151 hours, a Floorhand (Injured Person) suffered a serious crush injury to both hands while attempting to free a jammed insert carrier lodged in a casing wear bushing. The casing wear bushing unexpectedly dislodged and fell approximately 1.0 to 1.5 feet onto a wooden block, trapping the IP's hands between the bushing and block. The IP's left hand was amputated just below the wrist, and the right hand lost one full finger and two partial fingers. The incident was caused by poor hand placement, failure to recognize hazards associated with changed conditions, and lack of a written Job Safety Analysis.

Top Event: Unplanned Lower Marine Riser Package (LMRP) Disconnect
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On June 7, 2019, during BOP wet verification (stack hopping) operations on the Vito VA001 well, the drill team misinterpreted Step 21 of procedure CG1-OPS-CSP-01-59 Rev. 15. The team applied 100,000 lbs weight to the BOP connector instead of the LMRP connector, resulting in insufficient weight on the LMRP connector. When the Riser Connector was unlatched via ROV flying lead, the LMRP lifted off the BOP while pod receptacles remained energized, causing approximately $500,000 in equipment damage to pod stingers, receptacles, and related tubing/hoses. No spill was recorded.

recommendations: ["Update BOP Wet Verification Procedure to include sign-offs by responsible persons", "Include absolute weights at critical steps in procedure", "Include formulas for calculating weights at steps with weight changes", "Re-emphasize expectations to follow existing work processes such as handovers and Control of Work (COW)", "Consider developing a hard barrier procedure to prevent procedural control of this function", "Issue a Safety Alert"]

Top Event: Loss of Containment - O-Ring Failure on Aceco Compact Valve
Incident Type: Leak / Pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: methanol, crude oil, nitrogen
Summary: On 16-FEB-2015 at 12:40 hours, the Titan Platform (OCS-G 16661) experienced an O-Ring failure on a 1" 15,000 psig Aceco compact valve on the Telemark (AT 63 No. 4) incoming flowline. The valve was being used during methanol flow-back operations following hydrate remediation. Approximately 438 barrels of methanol with trace amounts of crude oil leaked into the Gulf of Mexico, creating a barely-visible sheen over a 1,320' by 10' area. Root causes included improper installation by a Shamrock technician: flange cap screws were not properly torqued, an incorrect outlet flange was installed, and the face seal was not properly verified. No injuries or fatalities occurred.

Top Event: Projectile strike injury from pipe drift (rabbit)
Incident Type: Other Injury
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drill pipe, drilling fluid
Summary: On 30-Nov-2015 at 0915 hours aboard the Maersk Developer semi-submersible drilling rig, an injured person was struck in the face by a Teflon pipe drift (rabbit) that became dislodged from a vertical drill pipe joint. The incident occurred while making up stands of 5-inch heavy weight drill pipe. The injured person removed the thread protector and, when the rabbit did not exit, signaled the tugger operator to raise the pipe approximately two feet. While looking up into the pipe, the rabbit came free and struck the injured person in the face, causing numerous lacerations and fractures requiring surgery and emergency evacuation.

Top Event: Firewater system piping rupture and dropped object
Incident Type: Equipment failure and structural damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: seawater, diesel fuel, Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF)
Summary: On 09 May 2025 at 1530 hours, an Emergency Shutdown event occurred at MC 127 A SPAR platform when EnerMech technicians accidentally initiated a pressure loss in the Temperature Safety Element loop while troubleshooting an air compressor. During recovery from the ESD, multiple procedural failures and equipment issues led to air entering the firewater system. When the Fire Water Pumps were restarted, a water hammer effect ruptured multiple sections of FiberBond firewater piping, causing extensive damage. A metal support clamp weighing 1.5 pounds fell 30 feet from the ruptured piping and landed 5-6 feet from a person near Lifeboat 4. No injuries occurred, but the firewater system was damaged and unable to provide adequate firefighting capability.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Load Drop
Incident Type: Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid, nylon slings
Summary: On 21 October 2023 at 13:04 hours at Mississippi Canyon 127 A, Horn Mountain platform, personnel attempted a heavy lift of 8,300 pounds using the platform's West Crane. When the lift was 15 feet above the platform deck, two nylon choker slings failed due to contact with a sharp edge on the hydraulic chain jack cylinder, causing the load to fall onto two cargo baskets. No personnel injuries occurred. The root cause was the decision to use nylon choker slings wrapped around the cylinder body instead of the manufacturer-recommended lifting pad-eyes, combined with inadequate hazard analysis that failed to identify the sharp edge as a hazard.

Top Event: Loss of Dynamic Positioning and Unplanned Emergency Disconnect Sequence Activation
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: 10.9 ppg calcium chloride brine, 12.8 ppg calcium bromide brine, diesel fuel
Summary: On 28 MAR 2025 at 1643 hours, an Able Body Seaman 1 (AB1) conducting routine accommodation fire dampener testing alone mistakenly operated the wrong switch panel in the Forward Emergency Gear Locker, inadvertently actuating emergency fuel shut off valves. This caused sequential diesel generator failures, loss of dynamic positioning capability, and drillship drift off station. The rig floor manually activated EDS, shearing the drill pipe and detaching the LMRP, resulting in discharge of 2436 barrels of brine into the Gulf of America and $2.6M in equipment damage.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Station-Keeping and Emergency Disconnect Sequence
Incident Type: Weather-Related Loss of Position / Structural Damage
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: calcium chloride brine, drill pipe, drilling fluid
Summary: On May 29, 2025, at approximately 10:46 a.m., the Noble Stanley Lafosse drillship experienced an unexpected and severe weather event during cement preparation operations. Forecasts had predicted winds not exceeding 10 knots; however, a thunderstorm developed producing a sudden 60-knot wind gust from the north that shifted to the west. The vessel experienced a 14-meter excursion while hanging off 5-7/8" drill pipe, triggering escalation from yellow alert to red alert. The crew initiated an Emergency Disconnect Sequence, successfully closing blind shear rams and securing the well. Approximately 417 barrels of 10.9 ppg calcium chloride were released to the sea. No injuries were reported. The incident was caused by rapid and variable wind conditions that exceeded the rig's station-keeping capacity.

Summary: On March 5, 2024, at 04:15 hrs, the Transocean Deepwater Poseidon drillship experienced extreme unexpected changes in wind speed and direction due to strong thunderstorms while drilling the AW003 well in MC 391. Wind speeds escalated from 30 kts to 83 kts with rapid directional shifts that were not forecasted. The vessel excursion triggered an Emergency Disconnect Sequence when the vessel approached the red watch circle limit (90.3 ft). During the EDS, three joints of 6-5/8 in drill pipe and an MPD bearing assembly (13,200 lbs) fell from the derrick and struck the drill shack protective barrier. Approximately 2,875 bbls of synthetic based mud (1,667 bbls base oil) was released to the seafloor. Total estimated damages: $1,000,000.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Pollution event - discharge of oil into Gulf of America waters
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, emulsion breaker chemical, oil and grease
Summary: On 08 February 2025, BP personnel at Na Kika platform began injecting a trial emulsion breaker chemical into the produced water treatment system. The new chemical caused frothing and foaming within production vessels, leading to faulty level readings and liquid carryover. This resulted in approximately 89 barrels of oil and grease discharging from the produced water overboard system into the Gulf of America from 08-15 February 2025. The discharge went undetected for 7 days due to the discharge point being located 80 feet below the water surface. Once BP identified the chemical trial as the cause, they discontinued the trial and returned to the original formula, successfully ending the pollution event.

Summary: On 07-JUN-2023 at 11:00 hours, a 43-foot cleanout assembly fell approximately 40 feet onto the pipe skate during drilling operations on the Diamond Ocean BlackHornet Drillship at MC 520. Top Event: Fire on rental air compressor
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, compressed air, oil separator fluid
Summary: On 24 December 2023 at 0743 hours, a fire occurred on a rental air compressor being used for painting and blasting operations on the FPU 'Who Dat' in MC 547 A, approximately 39 miles offshore in 3280 feet of water. The fire was caused by failure of a 90° elbow on the oil separator piping, which allowed oil to spray into the enclosure and ignite on hot surfaces. The platform's fire detection system and Emergency Shut Down system functioned as designed. The fire was safely extinguished by the platform's Fire Team using water. No injuries, environmental impact, or platform damage occurred. Damage was limited to the rental air compressor and minor charring of adjacent generator enclosure wiring. Cost of repairs/replacement: $31,969.09.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped Object - Cable Roller
Incident Type: Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 16 April 2025 at approximately 1500 hours, a 15-pound cable roller measuring 66 inches long and one inch in diameter broke off from the boom of an East Crane (700L-140) on the Medusa SPAR platform and fell 50 feet to the deck below. The cable roller struck a hydraulic hose on the deck, damaging it beyond use. No injuries occurred. The root causes were inadequate preventive maintenance (failure to repair corroded cable rollers identified in October 2022 inspection) and human error (failure to barricade the area beneath the crane to prevent personnel access to the drop zone).

Top Event: Dropped riser joint onto conveyor skate
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: development/production
Materials: riser joint, hydraulic fluid
Summary: On March 19, 2024, at 13:30 hrs, during BOP deployment/riser running operations on the Valaris DS-18 drillship in Mississippi Canyon Block 607, riser joint #31 was dropped onto the forward conveyor skate. The incident occurred when the riser cart shuttle brake valve wire was loose, causing a fuse to blow and preventing the cart from freewheeling. As the driller continued to hoist the riser joint, one end came out of the conveyor bucket and the Cameron Hydraulic Riser Running Tool (HRRT) mandrel parted, allowing the riser joint to fall approximately 28 inches onto the conveyor. The incident resulted in damage to the riser skate tailing arms, skate lift bucket, HRRT, and riser joint floatation, with estimated property damage of $418,000. No injuries occurred.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Rupture of nitrogen cylinder
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: workover
Materials: nitrogen
Summary: On June 5, 2024, at approximately 15:38 hours, a nitrogen cylinder ruptured inside a twelve-cylinder bottle rack aboard the Helix Q5000 during workover operations. The rupture caused damage to other bottles in the rack and scattered them across the main deck. Three personnel sustained minor injuries from shrapnel. The root cause was corrosion of the cylinder wall due to inadequate paint coating protection in the marine environment, combined with design deficiencies including lack of protective barriers between cylinders and rack, and inadequate drainage. Visual pre-fill inspections prior to offshore deployment did not identify the corrosion because the rack was not disassembled for thorough inspection of individual cylinders.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Well Leak
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid, methanol, calcium bromide completion brine, synthetic based oil
Summary: On January 6, 2023, at 13:00 hours, Shell Offshore Inc. experienced a pollution event at Mississippi Canyon 943 where subsea well PN003-ST2-BP1 released approximately 16.61 barrels of fluid mixture (hydraulic fluid, methanol, calcium bromide completion brine, and synthetic based oil) to the environment. The leak originated from loss of integrity in the Surface-Controlled Subsurface Safety Valve (SCSSV) control lines, with fluid being released through the High Pressure vent on the subsea tree. The leak was detected and stopped by closing control valves SV1 and SV2 via ROV. The well was secured with 2 barriers and remains shut in with continuous pressure monitoring.

Top Event: Laceration injury from modified wrench
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall - Laceration
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 24 February 2023 at approximately 1115 hours, a Mechanic employed by Helmerich & Payne was attempting to install a hydraulic line on the Pipe Handling Machine (PHM) at MC 807A (Mars TLP). When the combination wrench being used was determined to be too long to access the fitting, the Mechanic decided to modify the wrench by cutting it shorter using a manual hack saw. The wrench was placed in a vise to stabilize it during cutting. As the final cut was made, the Mechanic's weight shifted, causing his hand to come down onto the freshly cut sharp edge of the wrench, resulting in a laceration between the ring and little finger. The Mechanic received first aid onsite and was evacuated to an onshore medical facility where sutures were applied. The Mechanic returned to the platform on 26 February 2023.

Top Event: Laceration injury from contact with jagged I-beam edge
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall with Lost Time Injury (>3 days)
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 23 February 2025 at 0830 hours, a SafeZone worker slipped while securing felt and cleaning inside a Pressurized Welding Enclosure (PWE) on the Ursa platform at MC 809. The worker lost his footing on an elevated working surface, fell 12-18 inches, and his right leg contacted a jagged edge of an I-beam, resulting in a 3-to-4-inch laceration. The worker was medically evacuated and received 11 staples and 4 stitches. Investigation found that jagged edges and uneven surfaces were not identified in the initial JSA hazard assessment, and supervision was inadequate during the pre-job hazard identification process.

Top Event: Mooring Line Failure
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, polyester rope, steel chain, Grade RQ4 chain link
Summary: On 15 June 2023, during a scheduled underwater inspection of the Titan SPAR platform mooring system, abnormal slack was observed on Mooring Line #9 from the Port Column. Investigation revealed a significant length of the line was resting on the seabed. The 5-inch Grade RQ4 chain link subsequently failed due to fatigue initiating at the crown where stresses are high, with corrosion as a contributing factor. The failure was likely initiated by surface cracks on the crown. The incident resulted in production shutdown and a replacement cost estimate of $5,000,000.

Summary: On July 11, 2018, at 1200 hours, an estimated 17,098 gallons of Mono Ethylene Glycol (MEG) was discharged subsea from the Na Kika Platform (MC 474-A) following repair work on PZZ 2591/92 MEG pumps. Top Event: Partial Loss of Power
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On May 4, 2019, at 1351 hours, a partial loss of power occurred at BP Nakika Platform (MC 474-A) in the Gulf of Mexico. The root cause was rainwater ingress into the ESS-2 safety panel due to an unprotected warehouse roof under repair. The rainwater disrupted the ESS-2 processor, which handles critical safety functions including electrical power management. The emergency generator failed to start automatically due to a faulty lube oil temperature switch. Crew bypassed the switch and manually started the generator. All production safety systems functioned as designed. No evacuations, injuries, or pollution occurred. Power was restored at 1431 hours.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unauthorized discharge of pollutants into offshore waters
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, methanol, Low-Dose Hydrate Inhibitor (LDHI), produced water
Summary: On May 16, 2018, a pollution event occurred at Noble Energy's Mississippi Canyon 736 A (Thunder Hawk) Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The incident involved the discharge of approximately 14.77 barrels of oil from the facility's overboard water line. The pollution event was preceded by three separate shut-in and start-up events between May 13-16, 2018. During production ramp-ups, operators pumped methanol and hydrate inhibitor into flowlines, which increased demand on the overboard water treatment system. Level control issues in process vessels, combined with inadequate operational procedures for handling increased water production (6,000 BPD increase since end of 2017), resulted in oily water discharge. The root cause was identified as inadequate Management of Change procedures and inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences.

The root cause was identified as inadequate Management of Change procedures and inadequate operational procedures for well start-up sequences.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Power
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, methanol
Summary: On September 18, 2017, at 1100 hours, the Thunder Horse Platform (MC 778-A) experienced a complete loss of electrical power including emergency power. The incident was triggered by a UPS-B inverter failure on September 17, followed by a static bypass switch opening due to frequency synchronization tolerance mismatch between the Emergency Generator and Turbine Generators. The facility's Vessel Management System incorrectly maintained an abandon platform alarm state when rebooted, causing repeated power losses. A carbon monoxide gas release was detected in the UPS-B battery room. The incident resulted in the evacuation of 265 personnel and took approximately 24 hours to restore emergency power and several days to fully restore facility operations.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Hydraulic Fluid and Well Fluid Release
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Transaqua Hydraulic Control fluid, hydrocarbon, crude oil
Summary: On 22 October 2020 at 13:59, MC 778 A (Thunder Horse) experienced an unplanned power outage when line-of-sight gas detectors near the air compressor header activated, triggering a Process Shut Down and subsequent Emergency Platform Shut Down. Upon restart and troubleshooting, loss of hydraulic fluid was discovered at Subsea Well MC 776 TG002, caused by leaking SCSSV rod piston seals. An estimated 3 to 50 barrels of Transaqua Hydraulic Control fluid and/or hydrocarbon entered the Gulf of Mexico. The SCSSV was re-opened and hydraulic control lines were re-pressurized to prevent further loss of containment. ROV inspection confirmed no fluid flowing from the SCSSV vent. A function test on 21 November 2020 confirmed the SCSSV failed to function open. The well is currently secure with subsea tree valves in the closed position.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Well Control Event
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM), natural gas
Summary: On June 2, 2019 at 14:30 hours, the Seadrill West Capricorn Semi-Submersible Drilling Rig encountered a well control event while drilling the Taggert #3 Well in Mississippi Canyon Block 816. The drill crew observed increased return flow with increased gas at the surface (max gas=1386 units). After closing the BOP upper annular and diverter bag, approximately 4 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SBM) were diverted to the Gulf of Mexico waters. No injuries occurred. The probable cause was inadequate mud weight to balance pore pressure in the newly drilled hole section.

Top Event: Uncontrolled gas release and eye injury
Incident Type: Loss of Containment with Personnel Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 24-Nov-2014 at approximately 0830, an operator at Fieldwood Energy's Matagorda Island 622 'C' platform was injured when investigating a flow rate indication problem on the #4 Production Separator. While attempting to verify orifice plate installation, the operator failed to close the equalizer valve between the upper and lower chambers of the Senior Orifice Fitting prior to loosening clamp bar bolts. A plugged blow-down valve created a false impression of zero pressure. When the operator loosened the second clamp bar bolt, approximately 915 PSIG of natural gas and debris was released, striking the operator's left eye and face. The operator was transported by helicopter to hospital where corneal surgeries were performed. Full recovery is expected.

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: diesel fuel, engine oil, gel sweep
Summary: On March 14, 2017 at approximately 2330 hours, a diesel-driven cement pump on the Lift Boat Jamie G Eymard experienced engine over-speed during decommissioning operations, causing fire to erupt from exhaust stacks. The right bank turbo charger had failed, allowing engine oil to be injected into the air intake. Despite activation of the Emergency Shutdown Device, the engine continued to run due to a flapper seal failure that allowed continued air feed. Crew successfully extinguished the fire using portable extinguishers and the vessel's fire water system. No injuries or pollution resulted. Property damage estimated at $100,000.

Top Event: Flash Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: flammable gas, crude oil
Summary: On October 2, 2015, at approximately 20:25 hours, a flash fire occurred during decommissioning activities on Matagorda Island 685, Platform A, OCS-G-04548. Tetra Offshore welding personnel were cutting slots in the B-1 conductor for installation of lifting plates when an Oxylance burning bar ignited flammable gas trapped inside the 13 3/8 inch x 9 5/8 inch casing annulus at the plus ten deck. Initial combustible gas checks at the cellar deck level and wellhead detected 0% LEL, but failed to detect trapped gas in the annulus. The flash fire burned for approximately 30 seconds before self-extinguishing. No injuries, pollution, or property damage were reported.

Top Event: Dropped Load - Tote Tank Fall
Incident Type: Structural Damage / Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel, crude oil
Summary: On May 5, 2018, at 0745 hours, a crane incident occurred at Cox Operating LLC's MO Block 872 A platform. During the second lift, a 4000-pound tote tank containing 550 gallons of diesel was being offloaded from M/V Diamond to the platform. While lowering the tote tank into a containment skid, one of the four sling legs came loose at the swage ferrule due to a manufacturing defect. The wire rope parted, causing shock loading on the remaining three sling legs, which also failed. The tote tank fell to the deck, spilling a small amount of diesel into the containment skid. No injuries or pollution to the Gulf of Mexico occurred. Investigation revealed the sling had a manufacturing defect: a 3/8 inch ferrule was installed on 5/16 inch wire rope when 3/8 inch wire rope was specified.

Top Event: Load handling injury - crate slid off dolly striking worker's foot
Incident Type: Lifting incident with injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 8 May 2021 at 1100 hours at MP 283 A, two personnel (mechanic and electrician) were moving a 100-pound crate 30 feet across deck grating using a hand dolly to load it into a cargo box. Due to the crate's rectangular shape and size, it could not be balanced equally on the dolly. As personnel pulled the crate across the deck and encountered a 4-inch step-up 2 feet from the cargo box opening, the crate slid off the dolly. The injured person (mechanic) lost grip on the dolly handle and it impacted his right foot just behind the steel toe, causing a contusion. The worker was evacuated by helicopter and placed on light duty for greater than 3 days.

Top Event: Flash Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, antifreeze, glycol, triethylene glycol
Summary: On 2-FEB-2015 at 0230 hours, a flash fire occurred on Stone Energy's OCS-G 01665 Main Pass 288-A Platform in the Gulf of Mexico. The Kimray Temperature Controller on the BAP-1500 Line Heater malfunctioned, causing excessive temperature rise above the normal 150 degrees setting. The Temperature Safety High (TSH) sensor failed to shut off fuel supply and inflow of combustible fluids. This caused Glycol and Antifreeze mixture to boil over and spill onto the lower deck, dripping onto the Compressor exhaust and igniting. The fire was extinguished by operators using a fire extinguisher and water hose. Contributing factors included a metal plate blocking the burner sight glass port, preventing visual inspection for abnormal conditions, and the Pressure Safety High (PSH) sensor also failing to function.

Summary: On September 22, 2019, at 06:55 hours, an Electric Line Operator (ELO) at McMoRan's Main Pass 299 A facility sustained puncture wounds to his right-hand fingers, left forearm, and left torso when a Green Detonator blasting cap exploded during continuity testing. Top Event: Fire in compressor-generator building
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, lube oil
Summary: On 21 September 2021 at approximately 1550 hours, an Emergency Shut Down occurred at MP 42 D/L/M facility. A leak in the ESD/TSE loop caused loss of main power and rendered both primary and secondary seal oil pumps inoperable. Combined with a liquid carryover event in the first stage suction scrubber, produced gas bypassed the wet seals and entered the lube oil tank. The overpressurized tank lid blew off, spraying lube oil and natural gas onto the hot turbine engine, causing ignition and rapid fire spread. All personnel were safely evacuated with no injuries or pollution.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, oxygen
Summary: On 9 October 2021 at 0910 hours, the reciprocating gas compressor (CBA-0900) at MP 64 A #1 experienced an explosion in the 3rd stage discharge piping between the discharge bottle and heat exchanger during startup after shutdown. The explosion was caused by oxygen intrusion into the compressor inlet suction stream through two leaking valve cover gaskets on the 1st stage compression suction cylinder. The oxygen mixed with process gas and was auto-ignited by high temperature discharge gas from the 3rd stage cylinder. The operator mistakenly interpreted an initial ignition noise as an engine backfire and restarted the compressor rather than shutting down for mechanical diagnosis. External damage was limited to a 3-inch piping section and insulation. No injuries occurred and no personnel mustered.

Summary: On 03 May 2025 at 0830 hours, during a blind lift operation to install an Electric Submersible Fire Water Pump (ESFWP) at MP 153 B platform, a Fiberbond pipe flange contacted an I-beam beneath the top deck. Incident Type: Burn injury - chemical/thermal
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Triethylene Glycol
Summary: On 3 July 2023 at 1545 hours, an Island Operating Company employee was testing the Level Safety High/Low (LSHL) on the Glycol Reboiler (EBC 2151) at MP 270 Platform B when a blockage in the bridle occurred. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Rupture of pressurized gas lift injection tubing with personnel injury
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, stainless steel tubing, crude oil
Summary: On 12 March 2023 at 1633 hours, two Louisiana Safety Systems technicians were injured on MP 311 B platform while performing valve maintenance on well QAY B-18ST. A 1-inch stainless steel gas lift injection tubing, rated at approximately 3100 psi, ruptured due to 4330 psi of unexpected pressure trapped in the tubing. One technician suffered a serious injury resulting in lost time greater than 3 days; the other sustained minor injuries. The incident was caused by: (1) failure to monitor casing pressure, (2) failure to perform pre-job hazard walkthrough, (3) failure to perform required casing diagnostic, and (4) discrepancies between approved drawings and actual field installation. Root cause was downhole well integrity issues that resulted in casing pressure buildup following a grease zerk repair in July 2021.

Top Event: Nitrogen bottle explosion
Incident Type: explosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: nitrogen
Summary: On 27 April 2025 at 1520 hours, a 12-bottle nitrogen rack on the top deck of MP 42 Delta platform experienced an explosion. One nitrogen bottle with corrosion at its base ruptured, causing the 'daisy-chained' tubing and isolation valves to be ripped from the remaining bottles, launching them across the platform. Four bottles went overboard into the Gulf; seven were scattered on deck. One bottle remained severely damaged in the aluminum rack. No personnel were injured. Damage included the aluminum rack, welding machine door, and section of solid decking. The bottles were at least 1 year overdue for inspection.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Crane boom structural damage and loss of load control
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 15 October 2019 at 0755 hours, a crane boom dropped from a 45-degree angle onto the boom rest at Talos Petroleum's Main Pass 288 A platform during unscheduled maintenance. The boom was being lowered using an unapproved emergency load lowering procedure with an improper brake release tool (a 1/4" threaded bolt instead of the required fixed-head nut tool). The spiral lock ring in the brake actuator failed, causing loss of load control and the boom to fall approximately 30 feet, resulting in severe structural damage to the outermost 20-foot boom mid-section. No injuries or damage to process equipment or platform structure occurred. Root causes included inadequate communication between operator and contractor, lack of proper documentation and approval for the procedure used, and mechanical failures in the brake assembly.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel fuel, oil
Summary: On 25-JUN-2015 at 2100 hours, lighting failed at platform MU A 111 A due to a tripped electrical breaker. Upon investigation, the P&A Supervisor discovered a small flash fire on the generator engine exhaust manifold insulation. The fire was caused by vibration of metal rings used to adjoin heat wrap pieces together, which created a pinhole in the underlying blow tubing. Oil from the blow tube was blown onto the heat wrap by the engine fan, igniting the insulation. The fire was immediately extinguished and the generator shut down. Damage was limited to a small burned area of heat wrap insulation, with estimated property damage of $500.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Natural Gas Release
Incident Type: Gas Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On July 4, 2014 at 1300 hours, construction workers on the main deck heard an unusual noise from the cellar deck. Natural gas was observed leaking from the upstream flange of the Flow Safety Valve (FSV) on the FA-2 flow line segment of the B-001 well. The leak was caused by multiple valve deficiencies: the Manual Master Valve, Surface Safety Valve, and Wing Valve were all leaking, allowing shut-in tubing pressure of 3200 PSIG to reach the out-of-service production header, causing gasket failure on the FSV upstream flange. These valve deficiencies had been previously identified on August 16, 2013 but were not mitigated by management. The facility was evacuated and the well was isolated.

Top Event: Structural damage to riser skate from dropped spanner joint assembly
Incident Type: Equipment failure and human error
Operating Phase: completion
Materials: spanner joint assembly, tubing hanger assembly
Summary: On July 22, 2022, at 2203 hours, a spanner joint assembly being lifted by tandem lift (topdrive and crane) on the drillship Noble Stanley Lafosse experienced failure of the nylon sling tail line. The assembly dropped approximately 6 feet onto the riser skate deck. Investigation revealed the crane operator failed to create a new Permit to Work and Lift Plan, instead reusing an expired permit for chrome tubing. The nylon sling used was rated for only 5,100 pounds in choker arrangement but was required to lift approximately 15,000 pounds. No injuries occurred due to Red Zone Management and personnel positioning.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pipeline Rupture
Incident Type: Pollution - Leak
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 30 June 2022 at 2346 hrs, while conducting subsea pipeline modification operations on PSN 11015 in the Gulf of Mexico (205 ft water depth, 41 miles offshore), a diamond wire saw cut through an 18-inch diameter pipeline. At approximately 65% completion of the cut, the pipeline suddenly separated due to unknown stored energy, causing each end to move in opposite directions. This unexpected torsion/tension caused the temporary 8-inch bypass piping to shear at a flange connection, releasing approximately 90 barrels of crude oil into the Gulf of Mexico over a 3-day period. The incident was caused by personnel not being aware of the amount of stored energy within the pipeline, and was contributed to by lack of communication with a pipeline contributor prior to performing the cut.

Top Event: Fire in Heater Treater
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: At approximately 1000 hours on 8/01/2021, a fire occurred on Talos Energy South Pelto Block 23 Platform D in the Heater Treater (NBK-2900). A Production Operator noticed black smoke from the stack and investigated, discovering a small fire. The operator and PIC shut in the heater treater, turned off gas, opened the front cover hatch, and used a dry chemical extinguisher to extinguish the incipient fire. Upon inspection, a pinhole leak was found in the fire tube. Investigation determined the fire tube was operating at higher temperatures (110-120°F) than designed (maximum 105°F), and paraffin solids had accumulated on the fire tube due to insufficient heat transfer, contributing to the failure. A pattern of similar fires had occurred in 2016 and 2018. Post-incident modifications included installation of a new fire tube, burner, heat shroud, and updated operating procedures.

Top Event: Fire and overpressure in Heater Treater burner system
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, fuel oil
Summary: On September 23, 2021 at 1010 hours, a Burner Safety Low alarm occurred on the NBK-2900 Heater Treater at Talos Energy's South Pelto Platform. Two operators responded: one at the burner location and one at the master panel. After resetting the fuel shutdown valve relay and relighting the burners, a pressure release occurred when the West side burner was lit. The root cause was identified as restricted airflow in the stack arrestor from a previous fire tube fire on August 1, 2021, combined with insufficient purge time of residual gas before reignition. A second incident occurred on September 30, 2021 during restart, revealing charred wiring on the igniter and ground wires. The burner section was permanently taken out of service.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in Heater Treater Fire Tube
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, CO2, phosphoric acid, paraffin inhibitor, emulsion breaker
Summary: On 29-FEB-2016 at 1930 hours, a fire occurred inside the 24-inch fire tube of the Heater Treater (NBK-2900) on the South Pelto PL 23-D platform. The fire was caused by thermo-galvanic corrosion of the A-106 Grade B schedule 20 fire tube material. Multiple corrosion-accelerating parameters were present: low pH produced water (2.95), high chloride concentration (25,028 mg/l), temperature gradient on the metal surface, and scale deposition. The phosphoric acid in the emulsion breaker contributed to the low pH. The calculated pitting corrosion rate was 450 mils/year, an extremely high rate requiring thermo-galvanic corrosion as the driving force. The fire was quickly extinguished with no injuries or evacuation. The platform remained shut in for repairs until 18-MAR-2016.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Hydrogen Sulfide Release
Incident Type: H2S release and muster
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: crude oil, condensate, water, hydrogen sulfide
Summary: At approximately 1730 hours on 25-June-2018, an H2S release occurred on the South Pelto 5-B platform during abandonment operations involving flushing of eight platforms and fifteen pipelines. A worker detected an odor of H2S at an open top flow back tank. An H2S detector recorded 100 ppm of gas. All operations were stopped and personnel mustered for safety. H2S readings steadily decreased from 100 ppm to 0 ppm over nine hours. No personnel were injured. Investigation determined that water left in pipelines for over two years prior to abandonment operations allowed high concentrations of bacteria growth, which led to small pockets of H2S gas formation.

Summary: On 26-Nov-2015 at approximately 1030 hours, a fire was observed on the unmanned, shut-in Padre North 975 A platform during an overflight. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire from unattended cutting torch
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: oxygen, acetylene, crude oil
Summary: On 20 August 2022 at 1007 hours, a worker discovered a small flame emanating from an unattended oxygen/acetylene cutting torch hanging from a bulkhead in a Safe Welding Area on the Ursa TLP. The worker immediately extinguished the flame using a nearby portable fire extinguisher and notified the Control Room. A general alarm was sounded, full muster conducted, and Fire Team dispatched. No flame, smoke, or damage was observed by the Fire Team. The incident was caused by construction personnel failing to close torch bottle valves, depressurize lines, and fully tighten the torch head valve after switching to a plasma cutter. Sparks from the plasma cutter subsequently ignited acetylene gas leaking from the improperly secured torch. No injuries or pollution resulted.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Subsea Well Leak
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil
Summary: On 8 May 2022 at 1130 hours, a sheen of unknown origin was observed on the water at Mississippi Canyon Block 940, OCS-G 31534. The leak originated from Subsea Well MC 940 (Vito) VA007, a completed well not yet tied back via pipeline to Shell's Vito facility. The source was identified as a tubing string parting at a coupling in the 5.5" tubing at approximately 5,122 feet below mudline. The leak was stopped within 4 days by closing the SV2 needle valve on the subsea tree. An estimated 7 barrels of oil were released into the Gulf of Mexico between 8 May and 12 May 2022. Shell's response and reporting were found to be in compliance with federal regulations.

Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On February 25, 2014 at approximately 1500 hours, a contract employee fell approximately 90 feet from the rear of a crane into the Gulf of Mexico. The employee was cleaning oil from a skid when he lost his footing on a slippery beam due to humid and foggy conditions. During the fall, he struck a safety chain that was meant to barricade an opening to a ladder. The u-bolt connecting the safety chain to a stainless steel fixture broke under the impact, allowing the employee to fall the full 90 feet. The employee was rescued after grabbing an emergency escape rope on the north side of the facility and suffered severe injuries including a forehead laceration, broken nose, and broken tailbone.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Structural damage to crane boom heel section
Incident Type: Equipment failure and human error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 7 September 2022, damage to the boom heel section of the SM 192A platform crane was discovered during a monthly facility inspection. The damage consisted of bent vertical lacings and uniform bends in both upper chords of the boom heel section. Investigation revealed that the high-angle boom kick-out safety device had failed to function, allowing the boom to contact the boom stops and sustain damage. The root causes were identified as: (1) inoperable safety device due to incorrect mounted position, and (2) human performance error where crane operators failed to function test the high-angle kick-out despite checking it off on pre-use inspection forms on multiple occasions. The crane was de-rated and placed out of service.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pipeline Leak
Incident Type: Leak
Operating Phase: production
Materials: condensate
Summary: On December 3, 2014, a sheen was reported in the vicinity of South Marsh Island Area Block 27. Pipeline Segment Number 3334 operated by Sea Robin Pipeline Company was identified as the source. A hole measuring 1/2" by 3/4" was located at the 6 o'clock position at the bottom of the line at 93 feet water depth. The spill volume was initially reported as 15 barrels but later revised to 0.7 barrels of condensate. The leak was secured on December 5, 2014, and repairs were completed by December 12, 2014. Investigation findings indicated no corrosion, no third-party damage, and normal cathodic protection readings, suggesting the probable cause was a manufacturing defect or isolated pit in the pipe.

Top Event: Fall from height into water
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic oil
Summary: On July 7, 2018 at approximately 1115 hours, a contract crane mechanic fell approximately 81 feet from a crane skid into the water while attempting to replace an anti-two block control valve at Fieldwood Energy's South Marsh Island 105 A platform. The mechanic had removed hydraulic hoses the previous day without properly tightening them, causing hydraulic oil to spill onto the crane skid. When returning to replace the valve, the mechanic climbed a 10-foot ladder placed between the crane and handrail, placed both feet on the crane skid (over 7 feet above deck), and fell backwards over the handrail when attempting to reposition his foot on the ladder. The mechanic may have slipped due to hydraulic oil. He was rescued approximately 45 minutes later and suffered fractures to the C-7 and lower lumbar vertebrae.

Top Event: Crane Boom Structural Damage
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 25-FEB-2024 at 0930 hours, a contract construction crew attempted to raise the elevation of an I-beam support by approximately 1 inch on the Talos OCS-G02280 SM 130 B production facility. After unsuccessful attempts with come-along winches, the crew attached the crane's main line to the fixed I-beam and applied tension. The crane operator exceeded the maximum load capacity of 21,500 lbs at the 55-degree boom angle by applying approximately 30,000 lbs of tension, resulting in damage to the crane boom heel section and lattice. Estimated repair cost: $400,000. No injuries occurred.

Top Event: Crane Boom Collapse
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: construction equipment, grating rack
Summary: On May 12, 2023, at approximately 1605 hours, a crane boom collapse occurred on the Talos ERT LLC OCS-G02280 South Marsh Island 130 B Facility. While attempting to lower an 11,000 lb grating rack to the top deck, the boom cable broke causing the boom and load to collapse onto the deck. The boom cable failed due to fatigue from numerous cycles through the bridle sheaves, exacerbated by frequent operation at 60-to-75-degree boom angles and inadequate lubrication penetration. Total damage was $730,000 ($330,000 to deck and production equipment, $370,000 to crane). No injuries occurred.

Summary: On 17 February 2020 at 07:55 hours, a Warrior Energy Services Electric Line Helper sustained a foot injury when a 1.25-inch tubular pipe slipped through single joint elevators during hoisting operations on a Fieldwood Energy platform at South Marsh Island Block 149D. Top Event: Fire from compressor piston failure
Incident Type: Equipment failure with fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, compressed gas
Summary: On January 16, 2024, at 0252 hours, the C1 compressor at Arena Offshore's SM-128B facility experienced a piston failure. The piston rod nut loosened due to component fault or wear, causing the two bottom connecting rod bolts to break. This allowed the piston rod nut to contact the head end of the cylinder and separate from the frame. As the housing broke, gas ignited. The vibration switch detected the abnormal vibration and tripped, but failed to initiate shutdown at the panel due to moisture in the output line that froze in the 17°F ambient temperature. The night operator discovered the fire, activated the fire alarm and ESD, alerted personnel, and the fire was extinguished without injuries.

Top Event: Pipeline rupture and fire
Incident Type: Equipment failure - severe external corrosion
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On December 15, 2023, at 0520 hours, an 8-inch bi-directional gas pipeline (Segment 5114) containing 1220 psi ruptured on the Talos ERT LLC SM 130 B facility in the Gulf of Mexico. The rupture resulted in a fire that traveled approximately 80 feet from below the +10 level to the production deck. A 3-foot section of pipeline from the +4 to +7 elevation was missing. The fire lasted 5-7 minutes before gas pressure bled down. Life saving equipment, wiring, and minor structural damage occurred near the +10 level. No injuries were reported. Favorable wind conditions (28 mph blowing away from platform) prevented more severe damage. Root cause was determined to be severe external corrosion of the pipeline flange driven by high chlorine concentrations (12,000 ppm) in an oxygen-containing environment, combined with inadequate maintenance of protective coating and failure to complete required mechanical integrity inspection.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Uncontrolled descent of topdrive and traveling block
Incident Type: Equipment failure
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: 7.625-inch casing
Summary: On 11 September 2019 at approximately 13:00 hours, while making up for a casing run on the Enterprise 263 jack-up rig, the driller's-side drawworks brake became non-responsive. The topdrive and traveling block began to descend uncontrollably. The driller used the off-side drawworks brake to slow the descent. The topdrive struck the top of the 7.625-inch casing in the hole and the traveling block fell against the casing stabbing board. Investigation revealed a non-OEM stainless steel retention roll pin had broken, causing brake band separation. Post-incident investigation found incorrect retaining roll pins installed in both drawworks brake band assemblies instead of required OEM cotter pins. No injuries occurred.

Top Event: Fall through grating
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 08 September 2020 at 1330 hours, a contract mechanic fell through corroded steel bar deck grating on Cox Operating LLC's South Pass 49 Platform A while walking near the fire water pump to retrieve a serial number. The grating, secured only with clips and not welded, gave way beneath the mechanic, creating an open hole. The mechanic fell chest deep but caught himself and pulled to safety. Initial injury assessment reported bruises to the upper left rib area, but subsequent medical evaluation revealed a meniscus tear in the left knee requiring surgery, resulting in a lost time accident greater than 3 days. Investigation revealed extensive corrosion of deck grating throughout the platform, with prior BSEE inspections having documented corrosion issues that had not been adequately addressed by the operator.

Top Event: Personnel basket struck vessel railing and platform structure during transfer
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane
Operating Phase: production
Materials: personnel
Summary: During personnel transfer from M/V Patience Lab to SP 93 B Platform using a 4-Man Billy Pugh basket, a sudden wind gust caused the crane boom to swing. The crane operator attempted to lock the swing brake but it failed to hold. The hydraulic swing gear motor (slew drive system) had experienced structural failure due to mounting bolts backing out, resulting in complete loss of boom swing control. The basket struck the vessel railing and platform structure, injuring three personnel. The fourth rider was suspended in the basket for approximately 15 minutes before being lowered back to the vessel.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in galley/diner building
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On 09-OCT-2015 at 0015 hours, a fire ignited inside the galley/diner on Platform SP 93 B in the Gulf of Mexico. The fire burned for over 8 hours, with at least 4 hours of contained burning with both doors closed. Two operators on the platform detected the fire via alarm and attempted initial containment using the platform's Fire Water Cooling System. The Person In Charge shut in gas and oil pipelines and made emergency distress calls. A nearby vessel (M/V Canyon Runner) responded and assisted in extinguishing the fire using its water cannon. The fire caused extensive structural damage to the galley/diner. Root cause investigation identified improper installation of an electric stove/oven with insufficient clearance from combustible materials in the walls, allowing heat transfer to ignite wood paneling, insulation, and wiring. No personnel injuries occurred.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, lube oil
Summary: On October 20, 2014 at 1000 hours, a fire occurred on the Energy XXI Facility, Lease OCS-G 1619, South Pass 93-A platform. The Lead Operator was loading the #4 Arial Compressors after a shutdown. After restarting the compressor and switching the governor from manual to automatic mode, a loud popping sound was heard from the turbine area followed by a small flash fire approximately 6 inches long. The fire was extinguished using a handheld fire extinguisher with no injuries reported. Investigation revealed that minor metal fragments from previous repairs of the right bank #6 cylinder went unnoticed during installation, causing coolant to enter the cylinder and leading to a cascade failure involving lube oil sealing system damage and combustion.

Top Event: Uncontrolled release of mechanical energy resulting in hand entrapment and amputation
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall - Hand Entrapment
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: mechanical casing cutter, casing jack, slip bowls, grease, debris
Summary: On 30 July 2025 at 13:10 CDT, a Supreme Services crew contracted by W&T Offshore was laying down a 12-inch mechanical casing cutter from Well C005 into a shipping basket on South Pass 49 C platform. While guiding the tool into the basket, the outermost casing bowl (approximately 250 pounds) unexpectedly separated and slid down the tool due to a 'grease lock' created by heavy buildup of grease and debris from prior cutting operations. A Supreme operator's left hand was caught between the bowl and tool, resulting in amputation of one finger and traumatic crush injuries to two others. The operator was evacuated and underwent medical treatment resulting in permanent dismemberment of three fingers.

Top Event: Gas Release from Corroded Piping
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Gas Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On 03 November 2025 at 1205 hours, a gas release was detected on SP 62 C platform during a BSEE annual production inspection. A BSEE Inspector observed suspected gas vapors coming from a corroded area on an 8-inch gas vent line. Platform operators confirmed the gas release using a portable gas detector. The platform was manually shut in and the corroded gas line was replaced. The incident resulted in no injuries or pollution. Root causes were identified as inadequate equipment inspection and inadequate equipment maintenance, with contributing factors related to inadequate job procedures for routine corrosion inspections.

Top Event: Laceration injury from falling PIG launcher cap
Incident Type: Other Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 21 September 2023 at 1505 hours, an injured person (IP) suffered a laceration to the right hand while assisting two other operators in reinstalling a PIG launcher cap onto gas pipeline KZZ-602 on the cellar deck of unmanned Platform A, SP Block 83. The three-person crew removed the 12-inch cap for pipeline flushing operations. During removal, the lever arm assembly became damaged, forcing manual reinstallation. The crew mistakenly believed the cap was securely threaded after rotating it, and when they lessened their hold, the cap fell. The sharp edge of the lever arm shaft penetrated the IP's cut-resistant gloves, causing a laceration. The IP was evacuated by helicopter and treated at an onshore medical facility with eleven sutures.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On August 2, 2017, a fire occurred on Ship Shoal Block 177 Platform A during decommissioning operations of well A4. A cutting crew was removing a casing valve using a cutting torch when a fire ignited at a weep hole on well A5's Surface Safety Valve (SSV). The fire was extinguished in approximately 3 seconds by the Fire Watch using a portable fire extinguisher. Investigation revealed that well A5 had a leaking diaphragm in its SSV which allowed gas to escape and be ignited by slag from the cutting torch. The primary cause was the failure to shut-in well A5 prior to commencing hot work, compounded by lack of communication between the cutting crew and the Production Operator.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, air
Summary: On July 16, 2017, at approximately 4:30 pm, a Waukesha Reciprocating Compressor on the Ship Shoal 198-J platform experienced an internal explosion during start-up operations. The compressor had undergone maintenance on July 12-15, 2017, during which internal components were exposed to atmosphere. During restart, the compressor was not purged or loaded before running idle for approximately ten minutes with the blowdown valve open. An LEL mixture of air and natural gas ignited within the process piping, causing the explosion and damaging the 3rd stage cooler and associated piping. The compressor mechanic experienced migraines and dizziness five days later. Probable causes were identified as failure to purge and inadequate job safety analysis.

recommendations: ["Retrain all personnel on recognition of hazard analysis and proper system purging requirements following any repairs that expose combustible equipment to atmospheric conditions", "Develop and implement a general purging procedure", "Improve employee competency testing on purging techniques", "Enhance hazard analysis processes to identify purging as a key step in job safety analysis"]

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Spill
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, fuel gas
Summary: On February 23, 2018, operators discovered oil discharging from the overboard water discharge line of the Sump Tank (ABH-1000) on SS Block 224-D platform. Investigation revealed that the manual dump valve for the Fuel Gas Scrubber (MBF-2020) had been left in the open position overnight, allowing oil and gas to flow unchecked to the sump tank. Oil contamination in the supply system also rendered the Sump Tank Level Safety High (LSH) inoperable, preventing automatic platform shutdown. Total oil released: 4.7 barrels.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Crane boom structural failure during load offloading
Incident Type: Equipment failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: equipment, cargo
Summary: On June 18, 2015 at approximately 1300 hours, the platform crane boom on Ship Shoal 229 A suffered major structural failure while offloading equipment onto an offshore vessel. The crane operator was lowering a load approximately 10-20 feet above the vessel deck when boom swing control became inactive. The operator heard what sounded like failure of boom hydraulic pistons and the boom and load began lowering uncontrollably. When the operator attempted to raise the boom, it collapsed in the middle, dropping the load onto the starboard side and deck of the vessel. The root cause was that the crane operator used an assumed load weight of 12,000-14,000 pounds rather than verifying the actual weight of 20,300 pounds, which exceeded the maximum rated capacity of 15,000 pounds at a 70-degree boom angle.

Top Event: Falling equipment striking personnel
Incident Type: Structural damage to lifting device with personnel injury
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: N/A
Summary: On September 25, 2014, at 1350 hours, an employee working on the deck of Weatherford's Hydraulic Pulling Unit #4 was struck on the right arm by a Pipe Stop Arm (PSA) that fell 24 feet from the derrick. The PSA, weighing approximately 165 pounds and measuring 8 feet in length, broke off during the process of lowering it while racking back pipe from the A004 well. The injured person sustained no fractures or broken bones and was released to full duty the following day. Root cause analysis identified incomplete weld joint penetration and shallow/thin welding along the fractured weld, combined with overload conditions (tension, bending, and/or torsional).

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire resulting in evacuation
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, produced water, basic sediment and water
Summary: On January 5, 2017, at approximately 2:00 a.m., a fire occurred on Ship Shoal 266-A platform when oil discharged from a failed interface sight glass on the Heater Treater (NBK-1700) saturated protective insulation and contacted the hot exhaust manifold of Pipeline Pump #1 (PAX-4100), located 14 feet away. The sight glass failure was exacerbated by missing ball checks in the gauge cock valves, which allowed continuous oil discharge. All four crew members were evacuated and rescued. The fire was extinguished at approximately 5:50 a.m. Total property damage was estimated at $2.7 million.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Gas migration during cementing with well control incident
Incident Type: Well Control - Gas Migration
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, cement, water based mud
Summary: On October 23, 2014, while performing drilling operations on the C-22 well, the Ensco 87 drilling rig experienced a well control incident during cementing operations. A gas bubble from the formation entered the well during the cement job and migrated up the well before the cement had time to cure. At approximately 10:15am, fluid began flowing from the casing valve at 15 barrels per hour. The crew cleared the well and closed the Diverter Annular at 11:00am, after which fluid discharged to a height of approximately 20 feet above the rig floor. For approximately 30 minutes, the well intermittently discharged a mixture of gas, mud, and cement from the Diverter line. The Emergency Shut Down was initiated and 62 personnel were evacuated. No injuries, equipment damage, or pollution were sustained.

Top Event: Structural Damage to Crane Boom
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drinking water
Summary: On February 22, 2020, at approximately 15:30 hours, a Seatrax S100P temporary crane operator was transferring a 6-by-6-by-6-foot Conex box containing drinking water (approximately 3,000 pounds) from supply vessel John Harville to the platform at Ship Shoal Block 358. During the boom-down operation, the right side pendant line fell outside the boom frame and snagged on a bolt in the flange connecting the heel and mid-section of the boom. As the crane operator continued to boom down, the pendant line twisted the boom, causing significant structural damage. The crane was tagged out of service pending boom replacement. No injuries were reported.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pressure Safety Valve Dislodgement and Flash Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, glycol
Summary: A 2-inch line inside a reboiler ruptured, causing vessel overpressure. The pressure safety valve (PSV) was forcibly dislodged due to improper sizing and poor threading alignment, releasing liquids into the atmosphere. These liquids contacted the heater treater, igniting a flash fire. The ESD was activated and the source isolated. No injuries occurred. Two Incidents of Noncompliance were issued for equipment maintenance and PSV sizing deficiencies.

Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, production fluids, fuel gas, corrosive chemicals
Summary: On December 23, 2016, at approximately 1550 hours, personnel on the E-Auxiliary platform at Ship Shoal Block 154 noticed smoke coming from the exhaust stack of the Heater Treater (NBK-1800). After shutting off fuel gas, a small fire was observed inside the fire tube. The fire was extinguished with a handheld fire extinguisher. Investigation revealed corrosion and pitting in the welds of the fire tube, likely caused by caustic chemicals introduced into the production system that damaged the metal over time.

Top Event: Uncontrolled release of energy and tool ejection from wellbore
Incident Type: Equipment failure
Operating Phase: plug and abandonment
Materials: seawater, cement
Summary: On January 31, 2023, during temporary abandonment of the E-4 well at Ship Shoal 224 E platform, a wireline casing cutter operation resulted in uncontrolled release of trapped pressure in the annulus below a subsea wellhead tieback system. The tool string and fluids were ejected from the wellbore, damaging grating on the cellar and wellbay decks. No injuries were reported. The root cause was that personnel were unaware of trapped pressure in the 10-3/8" and 7-5/8" annulus due to lack of communication above and below the subsea wellhead system and absence of pressure control equipment.

Top Event: Loss of Load - Crane Wire Rope Failure
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: air compressor
Summary: On December 13, 2021 at 11:15 hours, the auxiliary wire rope of the SS 72 OF crane failed during offloading operations at Sanare Energy Partners' Ship Shoal Block 72 Platform. A 4,000 lbs rental air compressor being offloaded from motor vessel Leader fell approximately 20-30 feet into offshore waters when the aux cable parted above the hook. The compressor was recovered five days later. Root cause was determined to be failure to lubricate the wire rope, allowing corrosion and disintegration. Contributing factors included inadequate maintenance, failure to follow manufacturer recommendations, and failure to take the crane out of service despite identified deficiencies.

Top Event: Flash fire during pipeline pump startup
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, fuel gas, crude oil
Summary: On March 7, 2025, at 08:15 hours, a flash fire occurred at W&T Offshore's production facility at Ship Shoal Block 80, Platform A while attempting to bring Pipeline Pump #2 (PAX-360) online. A W&T Mechanic and Contractor Electrician sustained burn injuries to head and hands. The fire resulted from a loose fitting on the fuel gas starter exhaust piping that allowed gas to accumulate near the pump. When the engine backfired during startup, the accumulated gas ignited. Both injured personnel were evacuated and treated; the Electrician was released to return to work on April 9, 2025, while the Mechanic continued treatment. The incident was exacerbated by personnel not wearing required PPE (gloves) and wind direction that pushed flames toward one worker.

Top Event: Personnel Injury During Hoisting Operation
Incident Type: Injury requiring evacuation
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid
Summary: On August 25, 2018 at 0345 hours, a Floorhand employed by Helmerich & Payne was severely injured while being hoisted in the derrick of rig H&P 107 to inspect the top drive assembly following a jarring operation. The Floorhand's secondary safety static line became snagged on a monkey board finger while he was being hoisted upward. The opposing forces created by the moving hoist line and the snagged safety line caused the Floorhand to be pulled in opposite directions, resulting in severe injury. The Toolpusher operating the man rider winch had obstructed line of sight due to racked drill pipe and was not using a flagger as required by the JSA and Pre-Job Checklist. The Floorhand was evacuated via helicopter and transported to hospital for emergency medical treatment.

Top Event: Fire in Heater Treater
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, caustic chemicals, Scaletreat SD 8580, Biotreat 4663, Phasetreat 220, Scaletreat 194
Summary: On March 22, 2018, a fire occurred in the Heater Treater skid underneath the flame arrestor on the Ship Shoal 72 J platform. Operators discovered the fire during routine rounds and successfully extinguished it using a CO2 fire extinguisher while activating the fire suppression system and shutting off fuel gas supply. Investigation revealed corrosion on fire tube welds caused by caustic chemicals with low pH used in the production system. Combustible liquids entered the fire tube through corroded weld holes, causing hydrocarbon ignition. No injuries or major damages were reported.
