Top Event: Crane boom structural damage
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On May 7, 2021 at 17:00 hours at South Timbalier Block 100, the crane's boom control lever became stuck in the upright position during backloading operations. The boom pulled up into the boom stops causing significant structural damage. The crane operator attempted to reverse the control lever without success, then kicked it forward to stop the boom. Investigation revealed multiple failures: corroded control valve and linkage, failed High Angle Kick Out (HAKO) system with bent actuation valve and heavy corrosion, and failed Emergency Shut Down (ESD) system. The crane was permanently removed from service.

Operating Phase: production
Materials: drill pipe
Summary: On November 26, 2015 at 01:31 hours, a Floor Hand on the Rowan Gorilla IV suffered a laceration to his left hand and wrist when a stand of 4" drill pipe swung uncontrolled and crushed his hand between the pipe and an iron roughneck. Summary: On May 2, 2021, at approximately 10:11 hours, a fire occurred on the unmanned, out-of-service ST-26 A/A-QTR fixed platform in South Timbalier Block 26, Lease OCS-G 01361, operated by Cox Operating, L.L.C. The fire was caused by lightning striking the Communications Tower and Communications Building during severe thunderstorms. The fire was intensified by hydrocarbons released from a nearby Dry Oil Tank that was damaged by intense heat, causing process piping to separate at welds. Multiple response vessels conducted water deluge operations for approximately 18 hours. The fire was completely extinguished by May 3, 2021. No injuries were reported. Total property damage was estimated at $2,000,000.

Summary: On June 10, 2017, at approximately 3:15 pm during normal startup operations on South Timbalier 26-A Platform, a Cooper gas compressor backfired after two failed restart attempts and adjustments to the magnetic pickup and flywheel. top_event: Engine backfire and fire during compressor startup
summary: On June 10, 2017, at approximately 3:15 pm during normal startup operations on South Timbalier 26-A Platform, a Cooper gas compressor backfired after two failed restart attempts and adjustments to the magnetic pickup and flywheel. The backfire sent the scavenger box and debris airborne, igniting paper filters in the filter housing. A mechanic standing nearby was injured by flying debris to the chin and upper body. The probable cause was determined to be poor or improper gas purging techniques during compressor startup operations.

Top Event: Vessel collision with unmanned platform resulting in structural destruction and fire
Incident Type: collision
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, produced water
Summary: At approximately 0430 hours on 23-January-2015, an offshore supply vessel struck an unmanned Energy XXI production platform at OCSG 1443, South Timbalier 27 IA. Ninety percent of the structure was destroyed as a result of the collision and subsequent fire. The platform had three producing gaslift wells with average daily production of 92 barrels of oil, 93 barrels of water, and 115 million cubic feet of gas per day.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire on Gas Compressor No. 1
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On October 11, 2017, at 0015 hours, a fire occurred on top of Gas Compressor No. 1 on the South Timbalier 28 PRD Platform. The fire was caused by a failure/rupture of the third stage cooler tube resulting in a gas release, with static charge as the probable ignition source. The fire melted a fusible plug in the platform fire loop system, initiating a process shut-in alarm that alerted operating personnel. The fire was extinguished immediately and the compressor was secured.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Flash fire from ignited gas during welding operations
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On May 17, 2014, at 0830 hours, while performing abandonment operations on Walter Oil & Gas's #001 well on the Rowan Gorilla IV, three employees were burned by a flash fire. The crew was welding pad eyes to the inside of a 36-inch casing to enable removal. Burning slag fell inside the wellbore, igniting gas that had migrated from the reservoir after the casing was cut. The three employees suffered first and second degree burns to their upper bodies. Investigation revealed gas was present at approximately 40 feet depth inside the casing but not at the surface where employees were working.

recommendations: ["Prior to performing any welding operations on casing, the operator should take measures to ensure that not only the immediate area is clear of all hydrocarbons, but that there is no gas present inside the casing as well."]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire from uncontrolled hydrocarbon release
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: LP Gas, hydrocarbons
Summary: At approximately 1200 hours on January 23, 2020, at South Timbalier Block 311 Platform A, an operator inadvertently opened a ball valve connected to the bottom of an in-service LP Gas Compressor 3rd stage scrubber LSH with his foot/shin while exiting the area. This released approximately 1100 psi through a 3/8" stainless steel tubing drain line approximately 7 feet in length. The pressure release caused a whipping action in the drain tubing striking the operator on the side of the face/hardhat. Hydrocarbons from the 3rd stage suction scrubber vented toward the in-service LP gas compressor engine and ignited. Personnel activated the manual ESD station, signaled the fire alarm, and all personnel mustered. An attempt to use a nearby 125 lb. wheeled fire extinguisher unit failed to discharge. Fire water was used to extinguish the fire. Personnel reported first aid injuries (scraps, bumps, and skin redness). The fire caused damage to the compressor engine electrical components/wiring and the 3rd stage discharge PSV.

Top Event: Hand Crush Injury During Load Transfer
Incident Type: Slip/Trip/Fall - Hand Crush
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hoses
Summary: During the second transfer of two air hoisting work baskets from the boat landing of Platform A to the deck of the liftboat Raimy Eymard, a paint crew member's right hand was caught between a guardrail and a tag line, causing fractures to his index, middle, and pinky fingers. The incident occurred because the liftboat auxiliary load block was not centered over the load due to platform deck outreach constraints, causing load swing. The injured person attempted to control the swing using a tag line wrapped around the guardrail, but his hand was caught during the lift.

Top Event: Load Block Failure and Dropped Object
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane Incident
Operating Phase: production
Materials: wire rope, load block assembly, sheave bearings, grease lubricant
Summary: On May 9, 2025, at approximately 0900 hours, a crane load block failure occurred during offloading operations at Platform A in Block 229, South Timbalier Area, approximately 50 miles offshore in the Gulf of America. While lowering a Job Safety Analysis document in a trash bag attached to the load block hook, the crane cable lost tension and fell approximately 65 feet onto the aft deck of a motor vessel. The load block sheave was ejected upward and fell onto the vessel deck, striking a grocery box. A deckhand sustained a lumbar muscle strain while evading the falling components. Root cause analysis revealed inadequate lubrication of the main block sheave bearings due to internal debris obstruction in the lubrication passage, leading to bearing failure, sheared dowel pin, and sheave pin dislodgement. Contributing factors included operator inattention to distress indicators, inadequate training on early mechanical failure signs, and unsafe work practices involving maintaining the suspended load at excessive height above the vessel.
top_event: Load Block Failure and Dropped Object
region: Gulf of America, South Timbalier Area
summary: On May 9, 2025, at approximately 0900 hours, a crane load block failure occurred during offloading operations at Platform A in Block 229, South Timbalier Area, approximately 50 miles offshore in the Gulf of America. While lowering a Job Safety Analysis document in a trash bag attached to the load block hook, the crane cable lost tension and fell approximately 65 feet onto the aft deck of a motor vessel. The load block sheave was ejected upward and fell onto the vessel deck, striking a grocery box. A deckhand sustained a lumbar muscle strain while evading the falling components. Root cause analysis revealed inadequate lubrication of the main block sheave bearings due to internal debris obstruction in the lubrication passage, leading to bearing failure, sheared dowel pin, and sheave pin dislodgement. Contributing factors included operator inattention to distress indicators, inadequate training on early mechanical failure signs, and unsafe work practices involving maintaining the suspended load at excessive height above the vessel.
recommendations: ["BSEE Houma District recommends the Office of Incident Investigations issue a Safety Alert", "Implement revised Block and Hook Inspection Forms requiring explicit verification of lubrication points serviced", "Enhance Crane Pre-Use Inspections to include visual checks of all load block pins, retainers, and hardware", "Require flushing and verification of all lubrication pathways during annual, quarterly, and monthly servicing", "Implement photographic documentation during annual inspections", "Strengthen operator training on early mechanical distress indicators including block rocking, grinding/popping, and reduced wire-rope tension", "Establish protocol to remove crane from service immediately upon detection of abnormalities", "Apply safe work practice of lowering loads to lowest and safest height above water before swinging over vessel", "Conduct hazard analysis to identify and mitigate consequences of suspended loads at height"]

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire on Glycol Reboiler vent line
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: glycol, natural gas
Summary: On January 29, 2024 at approximately 1745 hours, a fire occurred on the Glycol Reboiler vent line at Cantium LLC facility in South Timbalier Block 23. The facility had shut in due to a Level Safety Low alarm on the Glycol Surge Tank. While operators were adding glycol to the system, a flame was observed coming from the Pressure Safety Valve vent line when the valve relieved pressure. The fire was extinguished using potable water. The probable cause was determined to be human error during reassembly of the still column following October 2023 maintenance, where the packing section was not reinstalled properly, removing the ability to preheat glycol and allow entrained gases to be released.

recommendations: ["Send still column back for thorough cleaning", "Replace perforated metal plate with expanded metal in packing section to improve flow"]

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Unintentional Discharge of Drilling Fluid
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: synthetic based mud (SBM), drilling fluid
Summary: On September 3, 2018, approximately 517 barrels of synthetic based mud (SBM) were inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico during a fluid transfer operation from the work boat 'Esplanade' to the H&P 100 platform rig. The discharge was caused by a gasket failure in a 4-inch butterfly valve combined with the absence of a secondary TODO Dry Break valve that had been removed but not reinstalled. The night crew failed to reinstall the TODO valve despite communication from the Deck Foreman that it needed to be in place prior to fluid transfer.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Crane boom structural failure and collapse
Incident Type: Structural damage to crane
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On September 15, 2017, during a boom rest operation on the South Timbalier 34B Platform, the crane boom experienced structural failure. The two bottom bolts connecting the midsection and heel section failed, causing excessive stress on the top bolts. The top section of the boom collapsed (jackknifed) to the right and dropped, eventually hanging by the main and auxiliary cables on the west side of the platform. The probable cause was a recent boom/boom stop contact that damaged the lower bolts. Heavy corrosion may have contributed to weakening the bolts. No reportable injuries occurred.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Crane boom failure and free fall
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On December 1, 2021, at approximately 19:30 hours, the west crane boom on the H&P 100 rig at Viosca Knoll 989 A experienced a catastrophic failure and free fell. The boom damaged a 45-person life capsule and platform handrails. No personnel were in the crane cab at the time. Investigation revealed that the ratchet and pawl locking assembly was not properly adjusted after boom winch replacement on October 14, 2021, causing failure of the load-holding mechanism. Contributing factors included motor overheating, sprag clutch slipping, and inadequate hydraulic system pressure.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, pipeline fluid
Summary: On 22 December 2020 at 15:30 hours, a small fire occurred at Viosca Knoll (VK) 900 Platform A production platform operated by Cox Operating, LLC. A flame was noticed on the pipeline pump's exhaust insulation. Operators extinguished the fire using a handheld fire extinguisher in under a minute. The root cause was a 6 to 8 inch crack in the expansion joint that allowed hot exhaust to contact the engine's left bank air filter housing, causing a plastic dust cover to melt and drip onto the exhaust blanket, resulting in a small flame. No injuries, pollution, or property damage occurred. One pipeline was taken out of service.

recommendations: ["Incorporate more frequent and thorough inspection of expansion joints and equipment to identify flaws before escalation", "Look into the possibility of finding a dust cover that can withstand higher temperatures than previous and current plastic dust covers"]

Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, pipeline fluid
Summary: On 28 March 2021 at approximately 1115 hours, a fire occurred on Pipeline Pump #2 exhaust piping at Viosca Knoll Block 900 A platform. The fire was caused by corrosion-induced perforation in the exhaust muffler inlet piping, which allowed excessive heat to ignite the piping insulation blanket. The fire occurred in a Class 1 Division 2 area. Operators nearby immediately extinguished the fire using a potable water hose. No injuries, major damage, or pollution resulted. The incident was attributed to inadequate preventative maintenance.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Overboard Discharge
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SOBM), drilling fluid
Summary: On 22-Jan-2018 at approximately 2330 hours onboard the drillship Diamond Ocean Blackhawk, 80 barrels of Synthetic Oil-Based Mud (SOBM) with 57.5% base oil content was inadvertently discharged into Gulf of Mexico waters during a Coriolis Meter flushing operation. The discharge occurred because shakers were not turned on before the mixing pump was started, allowing SOBM to flow over the shakers to the cuttings auger and dryer, then down the cuttings overboard discharge line into the Gulf of Mexico.

Top Event: Laceration injury from portable bandsaw
Incident Type: Other Injury
Operating Phase: production
Materials: steel tubing, methanol
Summary: On 15 January 2020 at 0715 hours at VK 956 A (Ram Powell) platform, a construction supervisor used a portable bandsaw to cut one inch steel tubing. The supervisor held the saw with one hand and the tubing with the other hand. The tubing moved and the bandsaw blade slipped onto the supervisor's left hand, causing a deep laceration to the left index finger just below the first knuckle despite the supervisor wearing cut resistant gloves. The supervisor received 7 stitches and was released to full duty with full range of motion.

Operating Phase: production

Summary: On November 21, 2020 at 1945 hrs, a gas leak occurred on the A-Ram Powell platform in Viosca Knoll Block 956. Top Event: Fire and platform evacuation
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: diesel, natural gas
Summary: On 22 July 2023 at approximately 1240 hours, a fire occurred on VK 900 A Platform when diesel from a tote tank spilled onto the intake housing of the #1 turbine compressor. The fire was initiated by diesel contacting the hot turbine exhaust and/or an exposed extension cord. Personnel attempted firefighting but the fire spread to the 8-man sleeper and became uncontrollable. All 6 personnel were safely evacuated. The fire was extinguished by responding vessels using water cannons. Property damage was estimated at $1,900,000.

Top Event: Gas Release
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 24 November 2024 at 1710 hours, a 3/4 inch pipe nipple on the sight glass level bridle of Compressor #1 Suction Scrubber (MBF P630A-1) cracked and broke, releasing gas from the platform. The gas alarm activated, alerting the Control Room Operator who witnessed the release and initiated platform shutdown via the Emergency Shutdown System. A platform muster was conducted with no injuries reported. The broken nipple was replaced and the facility returned to production within 30 minutes. The incident was caused by fatigue cracking of the nipple due to extended compressor vibrations, over-tightening during original installation, possible loosening causing excessive vibration, and excessive weight from the sight glass level bridle support.

Top Event: Vessel allision with offshore platform
Incident Type: Allision
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On 04 May 2025 at 0445 hours, motor vessel Adriatic (178-foot supply boat) struck the Marlin Tension Leg Platform at Viosca Knoll Block 915. The vessel's bow contacted the Northwest pontoon and stern contacted the Southwest pontoon, resulting in damage to production risers (Dorado and Crown and Anchor), internal gas lift line, and firewater piping. No injuries, environmental pollution, or stability issues were reported. The allision was caused by the vessel captain's inattention to navigation while performing administrative tasks and the absence of a second watch stander on the bridge.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Casing Failure
Incident Type: Casing Failure with Oil and Gas Release
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On 19-June-2015 at 0605 hours, a production operator on platform VR-279 discovered gas and oil leaking from well A3/A3D through a hole in the 20 inch drive pipe at the +10 bell guide and from the 20 inch x 10 3/4 inch annulus below the wellhead base plate. Approximately 0.67 gallons of oil leaked into the Gulf of Mexico. Root cause analysis determined that a gas compressor failure on 16-June-2015 caused production shutdown, allowing reservoir shut-in pressure to build. This pressure caused pump through plugs to fail, allowing gas and oil to enter deteriorated short string tubing, then the 7 5/8 inch production casing, and finally the 10 3/4 inch surface casing, which had a leak approximately 10 feet below the wellhead. The surface casing leak allowed pressurized fluids to enter the non-pressure-containing 20 inch drive pipe, creating open leak paths to the atmosphere.

Materials: Zinc Bromide completion fluid
Summary: On 13-Nov-2014, the Hercules 300 was completing well A5 when approximately 66 barrels of 16.6 ppg Zinc Bromide completion fluid was discharged overboard. The incident occurred because the valve on the cement unit from the fluid delivery line to the mud pits was left open, allowing Zinc Bromide to enter the displacement tank of the cement unit. The drain valve on the displacement tank was also in the open position, allowing the fluid to discharge overboard. The Derrickman failed to confirm that valves isolating the cement unit were closed prior to beginning the fluid transfer to the filtration unit.

Summary: On June 24, 2022, at approximately 12:30 pm during completion operations at VR-369 platform A, the wire rope guard assembly for the main load line idler sheave carrier fell 103 feet to the platform deck. Top Event: Fire in Heater Treater
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water, natural gas, acid-based de-emulsifier (Gyptron TA-21)
Summary: On April 18, 2014 at approximately 1900 hours, a fire occurred in the Heater Treater (NBK-2100) on the VR369A platform operated by Black Elk Energy Offshore Operating LLC in the Gulf of Mexico. The fire was caused by a pin hole that developed in the fire tube due to pit corrosion from frequent high-dosage batch treatments with acid-based de-emulsifier (Gyptron TA-21). Produced oil leaked through the pin hole into the fire tube and sprayed near the main burner, providing a steady fuel source. The operator responded to a Burner Safety Low alarm, isolated gas supply manually, activated the ESD station, and alerted personnel. The fire was successfully extinguished using handheld fire extinguishers. The incident resulted in $15,000 in property damage to the fire tube.

Top Event: Crane shock loading due to packer shear ring failure
Incident Type: Equipment Failure / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, cement
Summary: On February 10, 2020, at 5:45 am, a shock loading incident occurred with the platform crane at Vermilion Block 371 while setting a tension packer during plug and abandonment operations. The packer's shear ring failed at 26,000 pounds of pull (below its 40,000 pound rating) due to the ring not being changed after previous settings per manufacturer instructions. The sudden load reduction from 26,000 to 9,000 pounds shock loaded the crane, damaging the 9/16 inch diameter Dyform 6 boom cable. Root causes included failure to follow API RP 2D guidelines, use of incorrect Job Safety Analysis, crew misunderstanding of shear ring rating (believed 50,000 pounds instead of 40,000 pounds), and non-compliance with manufacturer instructions to change shear ring after each setting.

Top Event: Struck by rotating spinning wrench
Incident Type: Struck by / Contact with object
Operating Phase: production
Materials: drilling fluid, crude oil
Summary: On July 3, 2023, at approximately 3:00 pm, an Assistant Driller on the Enterprise 264 drilling rig was struck in the head by a spinning wrench that rotated clockwise approximately 30 inches while he was standing at the wrench controls. The wrench was not installed according to manufacturer specifications; only one back-up line was attached instead of the required two, allowing excessive rotational movement. The Assistant Driller was evacuated on July 5, 2023 and diagnosed with a concussion. The incident resulted in a required evacuation and injury lasting more than 3 days.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Explosion and Fire
Incident Type: Equipment Failure and Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, water, natural gas, diesel
Summary: On August 11, 2024, at approximately 7:10 p.m., an explosion and fires occurred on the deck of the liftboat Brazos at Vermilion Block 170 A during a well cleanup and test operation. Two Production Technology & Services Inc. (PTS) workers sustained second-degree burns and required evacuation by USCG. The incident resulted from improperly latched hatches on a 500-barrel tank allowing gas to escape, which accumulated near a rental diesel generator. The generator's faulty electronic control module failed to shut down the engine when it overran due to gas ingestion, causing ignition of the gas plume. Contributing factors included lack of written procedures, inadequate hazard analysis, poor maintenance of tank seals, and failure of the generator's automatic emergency shutdown system.

Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pressurized Gas Release
Incident Type: Injury - Required Evacuation
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, natural gas
Summary: On April 4, 2024, at approximately 10:30 PM, an employee of Cajun Cutters Inc. was struck in the left eye and face by a pressurized gas release from the open top of the tree of well A-15 while reconfiguring a tree cap connection. The injured person had been on duty for 15.5 hours. The incident resulted in evacuation, emergency medical treatment, partial permanent hearing loss, and ongoing evaluation for future eye surgeries. Root causes included failure to implement proper Lockout/Tagout procedures, inadequate written procedures, designation of an inexperienced operator for the overnight shift, and lack of safe working surfaces.

Top Event: Crane boom structural damage
Incident Type: Equipment failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On 16-DEC-2015 at approximately 1845 hours, a crane incident occurred at Fieldwood Energy's Vermillion 261-A-Aux facility during boom positioning activities. The boom hoist limit device failed to function, allowing the boom to exceed its maximum angle height and contact the boom stops, resulting in bent boom chords and bent boom stops. Contributing factors included failure of pre-use inspection to identify a loose and bent extension shaft on the boom hoist limit device, a boom angle indicator obscured by paint overspray and grime, and failure of the job safety analysis to identify hazards associated with close proximity of load to crane's minimum boom radius and low lighting conditions.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Hydraulic boom cylinder rod separation from cylinder rod eye
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: A hydraulic box boom crane at Castex Offshore's Vermilion 252-A facility experienced two consecutive hydraulic boom cylinder (HBC) failures where the cylinder rod separated from the cylinder rod eye. The first failure occurred two days after installation of a refurbished unit, and the second failure occurred twenty-four days after replacement. Investigation revealed that both failed cylinders had inferior quality welds with voids and lack of penetration, and more critically, the refurbished cylinders had a working stroke roughly one inch different from the originals. This mismatch created applied tension instead of compression, causing rod eye failure. Both failures occurred with little or no hook load and resulted in no personnel injury or additional crane damage.

Top Event: Contact Dermatitis from SOBM splash exposure
Incident Type: Improper PPE / Human Error
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM)
Summary: On July 1, 2019, at 2:00 pm, during pulling of workstring (POOH) operations on the Ensco 102 rig at Vermilion Block 369, a floor hand was splashed with Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SOBM) on his legs. The injured person was not wearing required personal protective equipment (barrier cream, tyvek suit, nitrile gloves, face shield, and goggles). The SOBM soaked through his clothing, causing contact dermatitis with lesions, scabbed blisters, and signs of infection. The injured person continued working and did not report the injury until July 7, when he sought medical attention. He was evacuated by helicopter for onshore medical treatment. Investigation revealed inconsistent completion of Job Safety Analysis (JSA) forms, inadequate hazard identification in pre-tour safety meetings, and failure of supervision to enforce PPE requirements.

Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: methanol, hydrocarbons, flammable chemicals
Summary: On February 21, 2024, at approximately 1349 hours, a fire occurred at the HI A 379 B facility during construction operations. A construction fitter was using an electric grinder to smooth welds on deck plating when sparks migrated through two layers of protective fire blanket barriers into a chemical/methanol storage area located approximately six feet from the grinding area, igniting flammable materials. The fire was extinguished and the facility was shut down. Investigation revealed that written procedures required removal of flammable chemicals from the work area, but the construction crew elected to use fire blanket barriers as an alternative control measure, which proved inadequate.

Top Event: Projectile Impact Injury from Sand Hopper Lid Failure
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: sand, compressed air
Summary: On 24-OCT-2014 at approximately 1455 hours during routine sandblast and paint operations on Arena Offshore's West Cameron 544-A facility, the top hatch lid of a sand hopper failed catastrophically due to poor condition of fastening components. The lid dislodged, was deflected by the upper rack assembly, and struck a blast and paint crew member along a nearby walkway, causing significant injuries including head lacerations, finger damage, mandible fracture, broken teeth, and pelvic fracture. Investigation revealed worn bolt threads with inconsistent washers and mismatched nuts, two of which were completely missing. Pre-job inspection procedures and daily maintenance checklists were not properly followed.

Top Event: Flash fire on water skimmer manway cover
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, condensate, combustible gas
Summary: On November 27, 2021, at approximately 09:30 am, a flash fire occurred while removing skid pads with a torch on the top deck at West Cameron Block 96 platform B. Slag and sparks from hot work operations fell onto the unsealed 24-inch manway cover of the water skimmer (MBM-0500), which contained undetermined amounts of condensate and combustible gas vapors. Root causes included incomplete vessel cleaning, loose manway cover securing, lack of gas detection survey, inadequate fire-retardant tarp placement, and failure to conduct jobsite walkthroughs before permit approval.

Top Event: Wire rope sling failure and struck-by injury
Incident Type: Struck-by / Crane Operations
Operating Phase: production
Materials: water abrasive cutter, wire rope slings, caisson
Summary: On August 10, 2023, at approximately 5:15 pm, during crane operations to remove a water abrasive cutter from a 48-inch caisson at WC-33 #1 platform, a 2.5-ton wire rope sling in the lifting arrangement parted and struck an Offshore Technical Services (OTS) employee on the head and neck. The incident occurred when the abrasive cutter hung up in the caisson simultaneously with a wave surge, introducing dynamic loading that exceeded the safe working limit of the sling. The injured person sustained a forehead laceration requiring 16 stitches and a C6 vertebrae fracture. Root causes included inadequate Job Safety and Environmental Analysis (JSA) that did not address dynamic loading from adverse sea conditions (5-7 foot sea state), failure to call an all-stop after the initial hang-up, and personnel positioned near the suspended load contrary to JSA requirements.

Top Event: Object strike injury from falling pump connection
Incident Type: Dropped object / Lost time accident
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, drilling fluid
Summary: On 13-APR-2019 at 0615 hours, a hammer union pump connection (Type 1502, 40.6 lbs) fell 36.8 feet from a tubing joint being lifted by liftboat crane during workover operations on the A1 well at WC-61A platform. The pump connection struck a DSS worker on the hard hat, upper right arm, right thigh, and right foot, fracturing the humerus and lacerating the big toe. The root cause was improper securing of the pump connection to the tubing joint; during the second attempt to make the connection with the work string, the crane sling unscrewed the pump connection as workers turned the tubing joint clockwise. Contributing factors included poor communication regarding shift progress, inadequate supervision, missing work permits, complacency, inattention to task detail, and failure to follow JSA procedures.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Flash fire during compressor maintenance
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, fuel gas
Summary: On 29-NOV-2020 at 0657 hours, a flash fire occurred during maintenance troubleshooting of a gas compressor engine (CBA-2030) at the West Delta Block 105 Platform E. A mechanic was troubleshooting a non-firing No. 6 left bank power cylinder. When the mechanic disconnected the secondary spark plug wire from the ignition coil, a spark was introduced into an area containing leaked fuel gas at explosive concentrations. The gas ignited, causing a flash fire. The root cause was a cut gasket on the fuel valve jumper line that allowed gas to leak. The mechanic immediately activated the ESD system, descended the catwalk, and extinguished the fire with a fire extinguisher. No injuries, pollution, or property damage occurred. The platform was shut in until repairs were completed and permission to restart was granted by BSEE on 01-DEC-2020. The probable cause was identified as inadequate hazard analysis in the Job Safety Analysis (JSA), failure to use a gas detector, and failure to utilize a hot work permit.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Pipeline Rupture
Incident Type: External Damage - Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, hydrocarbon
Summary: On September 9, 2018, at 1130 hours, a trawling vessel snagged a bi-directional pipeline (Segment No. 15815) in the Gulf of Mexico, causing it to rupture and release approximately 0.8 gallons of hydrocarbon. The pipeline failed due to excessive stresses imposed when the trawler's net snagged on an 8-inch to 6-inch tie-in spool and dragged it until failure. The tie-in spool, installed in 2007 as part of a Right-of-Way modification, was not sufficiently buried or covered as required by regulations. The incident resulted in an estimated $2,225,000 in property damage.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire in glycol dehydration unit
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Triethylene Glycol (TEG), hydrocarbon condensate, diesel fuel
Summary: On January 17, 2019, at 0930 hours, a fire occurred in the glycol dehydration unit on Renaissance Offshore's WD 152-A platform. During platform startup following an extended shutin, the Field Foreman started the glycol reboiler and increased temperature from 200°F to 250°F without proper circulation time or adherence to industry guidelines. The #1 compressor shut down due to high pressure, and the Field Foreman left the glycol unit unattended. Rapid temperature rise caused overpressure in the reboiler due to high water content (21% vs. typical 5-10%), forcing Triethylene Glycol (TEG) and liquid contaminates out of the still column onto the hot stack (operating temperature >660°F). The TEG ignited upon contact with the stack, creating a large flame. Personnel responded by activating ESD, fire alarms, and manually fighting the fire with fire reels. No injuries or pollution were reported. Damage included fire-damaged aluminum shell, broken sight glass, melted pressure gauges, burned insulation, heat-damaged motor/belt, damaged electrical wiring, and damaged asbestos panels. Root causes were inadequate startup procedures and lack of operator training on glycol phase vaporization characteristics.

Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On 24 November 2019 at 0700 hours, a fire occurred on West Delta 27 A Platform involving the cooler fan tower package (HAL 1161/62/63) for gas compressor unit CBA-1101. Top Event: Loss of Containment - Pin Hole Leak in Gas Compressor Inlet Piping
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil
Summary: On 09 January 2021 at approximately 0600 hours, a pin hole leak developed in the inlet suction piping of the gas compressor on West Delta 30 Platform P. The leak activated the Pressure Safety Low (PSL) system, which automatically shut down the gas compressor, LP separator, and all nine LP production wells. The leak was caused by excessive corrosion resulting from Cox's failure to maintain protective coatings on the process piping. No loss of containment or pollution was observed. A temporary clamp was installed with BSEE approval and the platform was returned to production within 4 hours.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Oil Discharge to Gulf Waters
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, produced water
Summary: On 21 September 2016 at 0828 hours, BSEE Inspectors discovered an oil sheen 7 miles long and 1 mile wide originating from WD 32 E Platform. Investigation revealed that the Wet Oil Transfer Pump (PBA-410E) failed due to a stuck internal pump shifting/slider valve, preventing oil transfer from the Floatation Cell (ABM-410). Oil level in the oil chamber rose to the level of the produced water side and flowed back into the overboard discharge chamber. The Fisher 2500 Level Controller maintained preset water discharge level, causing oil to discharge through the overboard water outlet. The Level Safety High (LSH 1) shut down device was not installed in the oil chamber as required, preventing automatic platform shut-in. Approximately 8.05 barrels of crude oil were discharged into Gulf waters. The unmanned facility was in-service and producing at the time of discovery.

Top Event: Crane Boom Structural Damage
Incident Type: Structural Damage - Crane
Operating Phase: production
Materials: N/A
Summary: On July 30, 2014 at 2030 hours at WD 32-E Platform, a crane incident occurred during wireline operation rigging down. A clipboard fell between the boom lever and crane cab window, causing the boom to rise and activate the boom kick-out. The right-side boom kick-out failed to retract due to improper maintenance, allowing the boom to contact the boom stops and sustain structural damage. No injuries occurred. Root causes included crane operator failure to follow API 2D and Energy XXI policies regarding cab cleanliness, improper maintenance of boom kick-outs, and failure to perform pre-use inspection of high angle kick-out.

Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: crude oil, hydraulic oil
Summary: A fire started in the cab of an American Aero Hydraulic Crane (WD 32 E) at 22:15 hours on March 25, 2014, while the crane was unattended. The fire was caused by faulty and worn electrical wiring under the control levers floor covering that arched and created a spark, igniting oil accumulated under the cab flooring. The incident was attributed to inadequate maintenance, failure to promptly address documented crane deficiencies, and poor management of known hazards despite available inspection reports.

Incident Type: Equipment failure and human error

Summary: On May 27, 2019, a crane incident occurred on Cox Operating's WD 73 D platform during crew change operations. incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Crane boom failure and fall
Incident Type: Equipment Failure - Structural Damage
Operating Phase: production
Materials: hydraulic fluid
Summary: On 27 August 2025 at 1045 hours, the WD-152 A platform crane failed during a dynamic lift of an 8,000-pound Power Pack from a Motor Vessel. The crane's boom cable separated, causing the crane boom to fall uncontrollably and strike platform handrails and the vessel below. The root cause was a failed bearing in the boom cable bridle sheave assembly that caused excessive wear to the sheave pin, allowing the cable to cut through the sheave and ultimately fail under load. No injuries occurred, but damage was sustained to platform handrails, the crane assembly, and the Motor Vessel. A small amount of hydraulic fluid was released into the Gulf of America.

Top Event: Gas leak from pipeline riser
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas, crude oil, slop oil
Summary: On 17 February 2023 at approximately 0315 hours, a gas leak was discovered on a 12-inch gas pipeline riser (PSN 759), approximately 5-7 feet above the water line on the WD 73AT platform. The platform's ESD was activated, shutting down the facility and sounding the alarm. All 26 personnel on board were accounted for and sheltered in place in the quarters building upwind from the leak. The pipeline was bled down over several hours. A light sheen was observed during overflight, later determined to originate from the slop oil tank rather than the pipeline. Investigation revealed inadequate preventative maintenance, failed repairs documented in a 2015 survey, and insufficient pipeline inspection methods.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Well Control
Incident Type: Uncontrolled release of well fluids
Operating Phase: decommissioning
Materials: crude oil, natural gas, seawater, well fluids
Summary: On April 21, 2025, at 19:30 CDT, the WD 90 B platform experienced a loss of well control during Plug and Abandonment operations on well B020. An uncontrolled release of approximately 20 barrels of well fluids occurred due to a leak at the starter head spool. The incident was triggered when the P&A crew pumped fluid down the tubing to assist pressure equalization across a downhole plug. Oil was observed leaking from between the starter head flange and the surface casing annulus. After approximately 5-10 minutes, the fluid flow reduced and stopped, but uncontrolled gas was then observed, prompting full platform evacuation. All 22 personnel were safely evacuated. The well was shut in by closing the crown valve and cutting the wireline. No injuries were reported.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Discharge of Synthetic Based Mud Overboard
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: exploration
Materials: Synthetic Based Mud (SBM), saltwater
Summary: On November 16, 2022, at 05:11 AM, a saltwater kick occurred during drilling of the 12-1/4 inch hole section at approximately 25,000 feet on the Diamond Offshore West Vela drillship. The kick caused Surface Back Pressure in the MPD system to exceed the 1,300 psi PRV setting. Approximately twelve barrels of Synthetic Based Mud were discharged overboard via the MPD system Pressure Relief Valves. The well was successfully shut in and the kick was circulated out. The incident was attributed to human performance error in the decision to divert drilling fluid overboard rather than to the flowline as specified in the New Technology Application.

recommendations: ["MPD procedures and hole section influx management envelope matrix should include a differential flow threshold to inform the Driller how to respond when using MPD", "When using PCVs for MPD, ensure modeling is performed to determine optimal PCV setpoints with down hole pumps on based on maximum kick intensity and maximum PCV setpoint based on required SBP for connections with down hole pumps off", "Set maximum PCV pressure to protect against PRVs from opening based on a maximum flow rate for the selected setpoint", "Review FMCA for plugging downstream of the MPD choke to confirm the correct PRV set point should limit system to pressure rating of equipment upstream of the choke", "Review the latest version of the flow control software that responds to influxes in step changes and switches the MPD from Flow Control to SBP mode when the high-pressure limit is reached"]

Summary: On August 13, 2015, while performing drilling operations on Statoil's #002 well aboard the Transocean Discoverer Americas, a 2.8-pound Backup Wrench Die fell approximately 136 feet from the Modular Derrick Drilling Machine (MDDM) to the rig floor. Summary: On October 22, 2015, while performing drilling operations on Statoil's #002 well, an employee working on the drill floor of the Discoverer Americas was struck on the foot by a coring barrel while retrieving a sample from the wellbore. recommendations: ["Update appropriate maintenance procedure with proper manufacturer procedure", "Ensure proper wire snakes are available onboard for wire rope replacement", "Update TBRA and SSP forms to reflect changes in manufacturer's procedure"]

Top Event: Dropped object - IBOP actuator plate
Incident Type: Structural damage to crane/lifting device
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drilling fluid
Summary: On February 14, 2015, bolts on an Inside Blow-Out Preventer (IBOP) actuator plate located on the top drive of the Vantage Titanium Explorer sheared, causing the actuator plate to fall approximately 140 feet to the rig floor. Investigation revealed that tight tolerances between the actuator plates and lever arms allowed contact during drilling operations, generating sufficient force to shear the bolts. The manufacturer, MHWirth/Aker, recommended machine grinding of the contact areas. Similar equipment on other rigs was identified and corrected.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unintentional discharge of synthetic oil based mud
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM)
Summary: On December 11, 2014, the Noble Jim Day unintentionally discharged 142 barrels of Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM) into the Gulf of Mexico during mud pit cleaning operations. The discharge resulted from valve misalignment during the transition between crew changes. Keys to the dump valves were passed between personnel without proper Lock-out/Tag-out documentation. The Derrickman, lacking knowledge of proper valve alignment and receiving poor communication and labeling, misaligned the valves and created a pathway for hydrocarbons to discharge overboard. The Pit Volume Totalizer alarm detected the loss after approximately 5 minutes, at which point the crew secured the pits and corrected the valve alignment.

Top Event: Employee struck by chain hoist handle during riser skate repositioning
Incident Type: Struck-by injury from defective lifting equipment
Operating Phase: production
Materials: riser joints, subsea BOP
Summary: On July 27, 2016, during riser running operations on the Maersk Viking, a riser skate trolley derailed from its track. While attempting to reposition the skate using a 3.2 ton Vital Lever Hoist, the chain hoist malfunctioned when tension was applied. The hoist handle spun and struck an employee on the wrist, causing a bone fracture. The root cause was a defective chain hoist missing a vital retaining pawl latch. Contributing factors included poor job planning, improper pre-use inspection, and inadequate training.

Top Event: Electrical fire on thruster #2
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: abandonment
Materials: diesel fuel, electrical equipment
Summary: On December 23, 2015, at 11:20 hours, the drillship Discoverer Clear Leader experienced an electrical fire on the Motor Side of thruster #2 while performing abandonment operations for Chevron in Walker Ridge Block 758. The fire was caused by capacitor failure, which led to high temperatures, pressure buildup, and release of conductive gases. The incident triggered a muster and resulted in loss of thrusters #2 and #6, though the vessel maintained station-keeping ability. The well was in a secure state and a controlled disconnect was performed. The capacitor failure was attributed to stress from a previous incident on May 15, 2015.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Unintentional discharge of Synthetic Oil Based Mud into Gulf of Mexico
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: Synthetic Oil Based Mud (SBM), deck drain water
Summary: On June 27, 2017 at 2120 hours, the Pacific Sharav discharged 29 barrels of 14.0 PPG Synthetic Oil Based Mud into the Gulf of Mexico. The discharge occurred due to misalignment of two valves on the low pressure header while the Mud System Operator was pumping deck drain water from the muddy water tank overboard. The valves were incorrectly opened, allowing mud from the active mud system to enter the overboard line. The incident was caused by human error and failure to follow approved procedures, specifically the absence of the Mud Engineer who is responsible for verifying valve alignment during the semi-weekly discharge procedure.

Top Event: Loss of Load Control
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: drill pipe, drilling fluid
Summary: On 07-JAN-2023 at 1920 hours, a single joint of 6.625" drill pipe (60.78 lbs./ft) being laid down using the Pipe Laydown System (PLS) on the drillship Transocean Deepwater Atlas was released and fell 58 feet onto the pipe skate. The PLS failed to fully grip the pipe due to an unknown hydraulic leak affecting the centralizer and gripper arms, with only the roller jaw engaged. The Assistant Driller's view was obstructed by a derrick beam, preventing visual confirmation of proper grip. No injuries were reported as all personnel were in designated safe step back areas.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Fire
Incident Type: Fire
Operating Phase: production
Materials: lube oil, crude oil
Summary: At approximately 0130 hours on July 26, 2018, a fire ignited in the skid of a rental oil filtration unit on the North side of the production deck at the Big Foot facility (OCS-G 16942), located 120 miles from shore in 5185 feet of water. The fire was approximately 10 feet high by 10 feet wide with heavy black smoke. The root cause was that an operator restarted the filtration unit but failed to reopen the inlet and outlet valves after a supper break. The operator then left the unit unattended for approximately 14 minutes. With the valves closed and safety devices disabled, the temperature in the unit rose high enough to degrade the integrity of the hose, causing it to burst and release hot lube oil. The lube oil ignited, causing the fire. The fire was extinguished within a few minutes using AFFF and firewater. No injuries were reported. Property damage totaled $3,275,000.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Dropped Casing
Incident Type: Equipment Failure
Operating Phase: drilling
Materials: 22" casing, 36" drive pipe
Summary: On March 26, 2015, while performing drilling operations on the Noble Jim Day in Walker Ridge Block 508, approximately 3,107 feet of 22" casing was dropped to the seafloor. The rig was pre-running the 22" casing in an attempt to minimize downtime. Unforeseen loop currents increased from the forecasted 2-3 knots to 4.9 knots, causing the rig to lose dynamic positioning capability. A controlled drift was initiated to reduce loads on engines, thrusters, and the suspended casing string. At 09:00 on March 26th, uncommon rig movement indicated mechanical failure. Investigation revealed the Wellhead Running Tool (WHRT) Mandrel had snapped in two within an O-Ring groove due to excessive force from the casing weight combined with high loop currents. No subsea infrastructure or rig equipment was damaged. Metallurgical testing showed no equipment anomalies.

incident_embed_text:
Top Event: Loss of Containment - Synthetic Based Mud Discharge
Incident Type: pollution
Operating Phase: production
Materials: Synthetic Based Mud
Summary: While performing drilling operations on the Diamond Ocean Blackhawk for Anadarko Petroleum Corporation, two incidents occurred in which Synthetic Based Mud was inadvertently discharged into the Gulf of Mexico. The first incident on July 4, 2015 released 2.5 barrels of SBM, and the second on August 8, 2015 released 3.3 barrels of SBM. Both incidents were caused by a malfunction in the rig's software for the Slip Joint Packer management system, combined with inadequate crew monitoring of packer pressures. The software logic automatically adjusted low alarm set points based on instrument air pressure fluctuations without crew knowledge, causing the packer system to vent mud when pressure dropped below the adjusted set point.

Top Event: High pressure gas release
Incident Type: Loss of Containment
Operating Phase: production
Materials: natural gas
Summary: On 26-NOV-2016 at approximately 1406 hours, a high pressure gas release occurred around the gas export metering skid on the FPSO Turritella at Walker Ridge Block 551-A. During an attempt to collect gas samples, an engineer mistakenly pulled a half-inch plug from a double block and bleed valve without first bleeding down the line, despite system pressure of up to 2500 psi. The escaping gas was detected by the gas detection system, triggering an automatic facility shutdown. The engineer sustained a minor injury and received onboard medical treatment. No evacuation was necessary.
